They’ve never had this happen before, like, wow. We have to figure out our preparations for tomorrow. We might need to plan for a run-off, maybe get some new flyers going, figure out the strategy—”

“Patty,” Charlie said.

His voice wasn’t much more than a hollow exhale, but something in the absolute emptiness of it made her stop mid-sentence.

“Patty, I can’t,” he whispered to the dark kitchen. “I’m… I’m in my hard, lonely place right now.”

The line was silent for a second.

“I don’t care about the election anymore,” Charlie continued, with the words spilling out of him, so unbidden and bruised, driven by a deep, exhausting despair. “I keep thinking about myself. I must be something so terrible, Patty. I really must be. I think God made me this way on purpose. Like He put me together just to see how much one person could fail without actually dying from it.”

“Chuck—”

“No, listen to me. It’s true,” Charlie choked out, his grip tightening painfully on the phone. “I can feel it. I could never be more than the poison flooding my own veins. I hurt people, I ruin things. I’m destined to fail again, and again, and again. And I am rotten inside. I’m just entirely rotten, and I—”

“Charlie, just stop.”

· · ·

Her voice was sharp, cutting through the static like a knife, but it wasn’t angry. It was, curiously, impossibly firm.

“Stop talking like that,” she said. Her manic energy from a moment ago seemed almost completely gone now, or at least she wasn’t showing it. “You are loved. Do you hear me, Charlie? You are loved.”

Charlie opened his mouth, but no sound came out. A fresh wave of tears pricked at the corners of his eyes, burning in the cold.

“You’re not poison, and you’re definitely not rotten,” Patty told him, her voice unwavering. “Look, I know I get excited about these things, but this election is about you. If you’re not doing well, then I don’t care about the flyers right now. I don’t care what happens with the run-off. I’m coming over tomorrow, and we’re going to spend some time together, just you and me. Regardless of what happens with any of this. Is that alright with you?”

Charlie leaned heavily against the wall, sliding down an inch or two as the fight completely left his body. He felt small, and exhausted, but the awful, suffocating weight in his chest had cracked, just a fraction.

“Okay,” he breathed.

“Okay,” Patty echoed softly. “Go back to sleep, Chuck. And take care of yourself, alright? Good night.”

He pulled the phone away from his ear, looking at the receiver in the dark for a second. Then he brought it back to his face.

“Good night, Patty,” Charlie said. His voice was fragile, but clear. “And… I love you.”

On the other end of the line, through the faint crackle of the late-night connection, Patty let out a low, warm chuckle. It was a sound that somehow held no teasing, only an easy, unshakeable grace.

“Yeah, yeah,” she murmured fondly. “See you tomorrow, Chuck.”

The line clicked dead. Charlie lowered the phone back to the cradle, and for the first time in what felt like a lifetime, the dark in the room didn’t feel so heavy.

`, song: { title: "Little Dipper", artist: "HUM", cover: "https://resources.tidal.com/images/351f3b12/f495/4099/942a/df4fc1bb3b2c/320x320.jpg", url: "z2TOcWTQpBQ" } }, { id: "tie", image: "https://i.pinimg.com/1200x/3b/1e/72/3b1e7251247ff3d57ffbb780f0ab3d71.jpg", title: "tie", condition: (ev, opEV) => ev < 270 && opEV < 270, html: `
★
that’s the way love goes

The coiled cord of the landline phone was stretched to its absolute physical limit. It was a taut yellow spring that vanished under Patty’s bedroom door and snaked all the way down the hall to the kitchen wall mount. It was a dangerous game, honestly. If her dad walked out of the bathroom too fast, he’d trip and take the entire plaster wall down with him.

Patty was lying upside down on her bed, her head hanging off the edge of the mattress, blood slowly rushing to her brain. She had the heavy plastic receiver wedged between her shoulder and her ear.

“I’m laying down a ground rule, Marcie,” Patty announced to the ceiling. “Right here, right now.”

On the other end of the line, there was a faint rustle of paper. “I am listening, Sir.”

“If you say the word ‘ballot’, ‘recount’, ‘council’, or ‘tie’, I am going to hang up this phone, pack a duffel bag, and walk into the ocean.” Patty blew a stray lock of hair out of her eyes. “I am serious, I want it out of my head. My brain feels like it’s been put through a meat grinder. Talk to me about literally anything else.”

The line was quiet for a moment, save for the static from the corded phones. And then, the sound of a page turning.

“Are you inquiring about our history assignment, Sir?” Marcie asked; her voice was a calm, even anchor in the dark.

“God, no,” Patty groaned. “Well, maybe. Actually, yeah, let’s do that. What is it? Did we even have homework? I haven’t looked at the chalkboard since Tuesday.”

“Chapter fourteen, the Industrial Revolution. We are to write two pages on the socio-economic impacts of the steam engine on the working class.”

“That sounds terrible. I hate the steam engine.”

“But it revolutionized modern transport!”

“Yeah, well, it’s ruining my weekend.” Patty reached up and twisted a loop of the phone cord around her index finger, pulling it tight and letting it snap back. “Are you reading right now? While you’re on the phone with me?”

“I am multitasking, Sir,” Marcie replied smoothly. Patty heard another page turn. “It’s a novel. Very dense, too. Lots of brooding Russians standing in the snow having existential crises.”

“I’m wounded, Marcie. I bare my soul to you, I demand a distraction from the crushing weight of reality, and you’re reading about freezing Russians. What’s that for?”

“You requested a distraction. I am providing a steady, comforting… erm… auditory presence while you process your displaced anxiety.”

“I don’t have displaced anxiety,” Patty scoffed defensively.

“Of course not, Sir.”

Patty let out a long sigh, letting the silence stretch out. It wasn’t an uncomfortable silence, though. It wasn’t like Marcie was a bad person; only she knew how many lumps of sugar Patty put in her tea, or exactly how hard she had to be pushed before she shattered.

“I was down at the baseball diamond today,” Patty said softly, changing the subject to the only other thing that truly occupied her mind. “Just looking at it, you know? The city hasn’t cut the grass in the outfield yet. It’s getting up to my shins out near center.”

“We are still three weeks away from the first practice. I imagine the parks department is taking its time, Sir.”

“Yeah, but the pitcher’s mound is a mess, too. The rain washed away half the dirt on the left side.” Shifting, Patty rolled onto her stomach and propped herself up on her elbows. “I was thinking about Chuck’s form. You know how he always drops his elbow right before the release?”

“I am not intimately familiar with Charles’s biomechanics, but I will take your word for it.”

“Well, he drops his elbow,” Patty insisted, gesturing with her free hand even though Marcie obviously couldn’t see her. “And it kills his velocity. Plus it leaves him wide open for a line drive right back at his face, which, let’s be honest, happens entirely too often. So I was thinking… if I just stand behind him during practice… and literally hold his arm up, maybe muscle memory will kick in.”

· · ·

Patty kept talking about baseball for a solid ten minutes. She talked about the rotation of the seams on a curveball, the merits of pine tar, the way the leather of her glove was starting to crack along the webbing. She talked and talked until she realized her throat was getting dry. And through it all, Marcie just listened.

Eventually though, Patty ran out of steam. She rested her chin on her pillow, with the plastic phone receiver pressing hot and heavy against her ear. It was getting sweaty, but she didn’t want to switch sides and risk dropping the call.

“He’s gonna be okay, right?” Patty asked. Her voice was much quieter now.

Of course, she had broken her own rule. She didn’t say the forbidden words, but she didn’t have to. They both knew exactly who she was talking about, and why.

The sound of pages turning stopped.

“I think… that Charles is remarkably resilient, Sir,” Marcie said, her tone softening, shedding the dry, academic layer she usually wore. “In his own, erm, very inefficient way. He absorbs a great deal of impact, yeah, but he does not shatter.”

“I just…” Patty closed her eyes. “I hate seeing him like that. When I was over there… he just looked like he was waiting for the ceiling to collapse. It’s like he didn’t believe he was actually allowed to exist without something terrible happening to balance it out.”

“It is difficult to rewire a brain that has grown accustomed to the dark,” Marcie noted gently. “But he is not alone in the dark anymore. He has you. And he has me.”

Patty swallowed the sudden lump in her throat. She gripped the phone a little tighter.

“Yeah,” Patty whispered. “Yeah, he does.”

“Hold on a second,” Patty mumbled, awkwardly fumbling with the receiver. “My ear is sweating. I gotta switch hands.”

There was a loud, staticky clatter as she dropped the phone on the mattress, scrambled to grab it, and shoved it against her other ear. “Okay. I’m back.”

“Very graceful, Sir.”

“Shut up. How many more pages do you have in that book, anyway?”

“About four hundred. From what I see, the protagonist is currently contemplating a duel over a woman he does not actually like, simply because society expects it of him. It is quite exhausting.”

Patty laughed. It was a tired sound, but one that finally relieved the last of the tension coiled in her chest. “Sounds like an idiot.”

“He truly is,” Marcie agreed; the fondness in her voice was palpable through the copper wires. “Are you feeling a bit more settled, Sir?”

Patty looked at the digital clock on her nightstand. It was flashing 1:14 AM in bright red numerals. The house was dead quiet, and the frantic, manic energy that had been threatening to consume her for the last forty-eight hours had finally receded, leaving behind a heavy, peaceful exhaustion.

“Yeah, Marcie,” Patty said, a sleepy smile pulling at her lips. “I am. Thanks.”

“Anytime, Sir.” There was a soft shifting sound on the other end, like Marcie was closing her book and setting it on the desk. “You know, I enjoy these calls. Even when you are demanding I ignore the geopolitical realities of our school district… and our nation… and life…”

“Ah, don’t use big words at me this late at night, Marcie. I’ll get a nosebleed.”

“My apologies. Goodnight, Patty.”

“Goodnight, Marcie.”

Patty waited for the click on the other end, and then reached over, pressing the receiver firmly back onto the base. She let the coiled cord snap back into place, turned over, and finally closed her eyes.

`, song: { title: "700 Mile Situation", artist: "Res", cover: "https://yt3.googleusercontent.com/k1VQiB1zqOCZ3yxJX9emvRzy80JuH2kHcGksNnNYlCy-xI16lJQNwohcMoXMIcA0kTGbSBr9JpoegLlQfw=w544-h544-l90-rj", url: "IdtZiuWZ5yU" } }, { id: "differentcorner", condition: (ev) => linusWin && ruthlessness >= 3 && linus_bond < 0 && linus_bond > -5, image: "https://i.pinimg.com/1200x/1e/93/a0/1e93a022d1c0c14dbd39ab0b63aeff55.jpg", title: "a different corner", html: `
★
a different corner

If this were a movie, the camera would start low and wide in the living room, and pan seamlessly through the crowd.

It tracks Linus Van Pelt as he glides through the victory party. Oh, there he is. The golden boy, the philosopher king, the newly elected President of the Student Council. The lens stays locked on him as he shakes a hand, spins on his heel, clinks a plastic cup of fruit punch against someone else’s, and laughs; it’s a big, bright, flawless laugh. The music is loud; it was some pulsing old school music he liked, and that vibrated in the floorboards. Streamers hang from the ceiling fans, and everyone is smiling at him. Everyone wants to be near him.

He moves through the kitchen in one fluid, continuous motion. He points at Schroeder, who gives him a salute from the counter. He dodges a stray balloon. Linus is, ultimately, being just like the version of himself he sold to the school for two months: confident, capable, and perfectly at ease with his own triumph.

But the camera doesn’t cut. It stays fixed tightly on his face as he leans against the edge of the kitchen island to take a sip of punch. And right there, in the middle of the frame, the facade slips.

His smile doesn’t drop all at once, but it rapidly decays. The corners of his mouth twitch, fighting to stay elevated, but gravity and guilt drag them down. His eyes, which had been bright and engaged a second ago, glaze over, staring blankly past the crowd. God. The ambient noise of the party, all the cheers, the bass, the clattering of cups, begins to sound muffled to him, so hollowed out, like it’s happening underwater…

He tries to shake it off. A sophomore girl bumps into his shoulder and congratulates him. How fantastic! Linus forces the smile back onto his face, wide and eager, and nods, saying something polite. But the moment she turns away, the smile collapses completely, faster this time.

He can’t stop thinking about it. He keeps seeing Charlie Brown’s face, over and over again. Linus sees the look of betrayal he had during the debate. He sees the sheer misery in Charlie’s eyes walking down the hallway. Worse, he sees the ghost of the kid he used to sit with on a brick wall, talking about the stars among other topics, a kid who had looked at him over the last few weeks not as a friend, but as a monster.

He had won, he had everything he ever wanted, the school was his. But it felt like absolutely nothing.

The camera tracks him as he sets the red plastic cup down on the counter. He doesn’t look at anyone now. He keeps his head down, weaving through the dense pack of bodies in the living room. Someone calls his name — “Hey, Mr. President!” —, but he ignores it, pressing forward. The walls of his own house feel impossibly tight, and the air too thick to breathe.

· · ·

Linus reaches the sliding glass door at the back of the dining room. He throws the latch, pushes it open, and steps outside.

He slides the glass shut behind him. Immediately, the thumping bass of the party is cut off, reduced to a dull, rhythmic thud against the glass. The camera follows him out into the cool, damp night air. Ahh.

And so Linus walks down the wooden steps of the deck and steps onto the wet grass of his backyard. He walks until he is near the back fence, where the glow of the party lights can’t quite reach him. The yard is vast, dark, and perfectly silent, save for the hum of crickets.

He stops. The camera circles around him slowly, coming to a halt just in front of him. He is still wearing his victory outfit: a fairly bland red sweater. His blanket is neatly folded over his forearm, but he looks incredibly small.

Linus tilts his head back, staring up at the sprawling, black expanse of the sky. The stars are barely visible through the autumn cloud cover, and his chest rises and falls in a long, shaky breath. He stands there in the dark, in silence. The shot holds on his face, illuminated only by the faint, distant moon. And then, finally, the screen fades to black.

`, song: { title: "Rub You The Right Way (Extended Hype 1)", artist: "Johnny Gill", cover: "https://yt3.googleusercontent.com/qjBPl2kXFfEKpRdp01xc2v4J-xNMyes0TFWgsQUbj5Ct9ys7fPA10jutwXbHW17k7pmuftUpfuq-VXnL1Q=w544-h544-l90-rj", url: ["2C0FDyXiwuE"] } }, { id: "missyoubuddy", condition: (ev) => linusWin && linus_bond >= 4, image: "https://i.pinimg.com/1200x/c8/ed/27/c8ed273eda12f3ca73527ecd74d82a60.jpg", html: `
★
do i have to?

The rain had been coming down in violent, freezing sheets since ten o’clock.

Linus Van Pelt was still awake, sitting quietly in the glow of the hallway nightlight, with his blanket draped securely over his shoulders. The results had been finalized hours ago, thankfully. He was the student council president. The phone had rung a few times earlier in the evening, carrying congratulations from voices he recognized and other voices he honestly didn’t, but the house was completely silent now, save for the rhythmic, heavy drumming of the storm outside.

When the knock came, it was so faint Linus almost mistook it for the wind rattling the porch furniture. He hesitated, tightening his grip on the edge of his blanket, before padding softly to the front door and pulling it open.

Charlie Brown was standing on the welcome mat, soaked entirely to the bone.

His jacket was plastered to his frame, with water dripping steadily from his chin, his nose, and the edges of his damp blonde hair. But the rain wasn’t the worst of it. His eyes were red and horribly swollen, his breathing was jagged and uneven in the cold air. Hell, he looked like he had been walking for miles.

“Charlie Brown?” Linus whispered, his eyes going wide.

“Hi,” Charlie managed to say. His teeth were chattering, but he forced a trembling, awful facsimile of a smile onto his face. “I came… I wanted to come by. To tell you…”

He swallowed hard, his throat bobbing visibly.

“To tell you congratulations. On the—on the win. You earned it, Linus. You really…”

But he didn’t get to finish the sentence.

His facade crumbled all at once. Charlie’s knees simply gave out, and he collapsed right there in the open doorway, dropping hard against the threshold. Linus let out a sharp gasp, dropping to his knees immediately as the rain blew in past them.

Charlie pulled his hands up to his face and absolutely fell apart.

God. It was a devastating, agonizingly messy breakdown. He was sobbing loudly, his chest heaving with every ragged breath. Snot and tears mixed violently with the rainwater on his face, and he kept scrubbing at his cheeks with his soaking wet sleeves, sniffing loudly, trying desperately to reel himself in and failing completely. He hated it. He hated this. He hated that he was crying, he hated the pathetic noises he was making, and he hated that he was doing it right here, at Linus’s feet, ruining his night.

“I’m sorry,” Charlie gasped out through a thick layer of mucus and grief, with his voice breaking into a high, reedy pitch. “I’m so sorry, Linus. I’m sorry.”

“Charlie, what—” Linus was pale, utterly shocked, and his hands hovered uncertainly over Charlie’s trembling shoulders. “You’re freezing. Jesus, just come inside, please—”

“Why am I like this?” Charlie wailed, shaking his head frantically, refusing to move. “Why am I like this? W-why was I born like this? Why did I think hating you was a good idea?”

Linus froze, his heart dropping into his stomach.

“Why did I do that to you?” And Charlie kept sobbing, his breath hitching painfully. He wiped his nose with the back of his hand, producing another wet, miserable sound. “You’re— you were my best friend. Why can’t we just be friends again? W-Why can’t things go back to how they were before? I ruin everything. I’m so stupid, I’m so sorry—”

· · ·

“No,” Linus said. His voice shook, but he forced it out louder, cutting through the sound of the rain. “No, Charlie, don’t say that.”

“I hate myself for it, I’m so sorry—”

“None of that is true!” Linus pleaded, sounding thoroughly distraught now. Without a second thought, Linus unwrapped his beloved blanket from his shoulders and forcefully draped it over Charlie’s soaked, shivering back, pulling the older boy forward into a tight, desperate embrace.

And Charlie slumped against him, burying his face in Linus’s shoulder, sobbing uncontrollably. Linus didn’t care about the mud, or the freezing water soaking into his pajamas, or the mess he’d have to clean up later. He just held on, rubbing his hand firmly up and down Charlie’s back, trying to anchor him to the earth.

“You’re not stupid, and you didn’t ruin anything,” Linus said fiercely, his own eyes welling up with tears as he tended to the boy shaking in his arms. “We are friends. We never stopped being friends, do you hear me?”

Charlie just let out another loud, wet sniff, crying so hard he was beginning to hyperventilate. “It hurts, Linus,” he confessed in a small, broken whisper.

“I know,” Linus said softly, resting his chin against the top of Charlie’s wet head.

He tightened his arms around him, pulling him just a little further inside, out of the reach of the storm. He couldn’t fix the election, and he couldn’t erase the awful things they had felt over the last few weeks. But as he sat there on the cold entryway floor, holding his friend… his best friend… as he wept, Linus leaned in close to his ear.

“You’re going to be alright, Charlie Brown,” Linus whispered into the dark. “Despite everything… you’ll be alright.”

He promised. And Linus never broke a promise.

`, song: { title: "Talking Shit About a Pretty Sunset", artist: "Modest Mouse", cover: "https://resources.tidal.com/images/3629419c/6c64/40e3/8295/7ea1ab83f9d9/1280x1280.jpg", url: "9O9hY30PVUw" } }, { id: "iwishuheaven", condition: (ev) => linusWin && linus_bond <= -7, image: "https://i.pinimg.com/1200x/8b/a5/52/8ba5526d912306352ce11ec808859f1e.jpg", html: `
★
pillowhead

The rain was coming down in violent, freezing sheets, punishing the pavement and rattling the windows of the Van Pelt house.

Inside, the victory party was long over. Deflated balloons lay scattered across the living room carpet, and empty paper cups sat abandoned on the coffee tables. Linus was alone in the entryway, quietly sweeping up a trail of muddy footprints left by departing well-wishers. The student council presidency was his, but the quiet of the empty house felt heavier than a celebration ought to. At least it was all over.

He heard the gate.

Then footsteps on the porch, all uneven, too fast. Then the knock… well, not a knock, really. He sighed. In fact, he knew before he opened it. He didn’t know how he knew; he just did. He opened the door anyway.

Charlie Brown was soaked through. His jacket was dark with rain, his hair was flat against his forehead and his eyes were red in a way that wasn’t only from the cold. He was breathing hard, like he’d been walking fast or like he’d been crying, and from the look of him it was probably both. He stared at Linus from the porch with an expression that Linus had never seen on his face before. It was, Linus thought, something past hurt, something that had gone all the way through hurt and come out the other side as something uglier.

For a moment neither of them moved.

Then Charlie said, “You happy?”

· · ·

Linus said, carefully, “Charlie—”

“You fucking happy now?” He stepped forward, over the threshold, and Linus stepped back, and that was how Charlie Brown ended up inside, dripping on the hallway floor, with his voice starting to climb. “Big night for you, huh. Wow, big party, everyone very excited.”

“I think you should sit down—”

“I don’t want to sit down.”

“You’re soaked, you’re clearly—”

“I don’t care,” said Charlie. His voice broke on it, he pressed on anyway. “You know what you did. You know what you— you ruined it, Linus. You ruined everything, my whole— you just—” He stopped. He was shaking, a little. “Why? That’s all I want. Just— why?”

Linus looked at him. He kept his voice level. “Look, I ran because I believed I could do something with it. That’s the only reason.”

“That’s—” Charlie laughed; it was short and terrible. “That’s such a you thing to say. That’s such a— you always have a reason, don’t you. Everything’s got a reason with you, everything’s all considered and very—”

“Charlie.”

“You knew I wanted this.” His voice dropped. “You knew. And you did it anyway.”

Linus said nothing. He had decided, before Charlie finished the sentence, that he was not going to answer that. Not because it wasn’t worth answering, but because Charlie wasn’t in a place to hear the answer, and because some things you just have to let a person say.

This turned out to be the wrong decision.

“Say something,” said Charlie.

Linus didn’t.

“Say something, you—” His jaw tightened. “You fucking coward.”

Something shifted in Linus’s chest, but he kept his face where it was.

“You’ve destroyed my life,” Charlie said, and his voice was cracking badly now, wet at the edges. “You know that, you little bitch? My whole— everything I was trying to— and you just— you just walked in and took it, and now you’re standing there and you won’t even—”

He swung. It was not a very good punch; it was too high, off-angle, and probably the kind of punch thrown by someone who had never punched anyone before, which Charlie hadn’t, and so it glanced off Linus’s shoulder and didn’t do much. Linus took a step back. Charlie swung again, hitting his arm, then his chest, neither of them with any real force, just desperate, just needing somewhere to put everything that had no other place to go.

“Stop,” said Linus.

Charlie didn’t stop. He was crying now, openly, not trying to hide it, hitting and sobbing and saying things that weren’t quite words anymore. Linus caught one of his wrists.

“Charlie, stop—”

“Let go of me—”

“I’m not going to just stand here and let you—”

“Let go—”

Charlie wrenched his arm back hard and Linus moved to block him and somewhere in the tangle of it, his arm came up and caught Charlie across the face. The sound of it was small and flat. Though it was not a meaningful punch, and definitely not something Linus meant, Charlie’s head snapped back anyway, he lost his footing, and then he was on the floor.

The hallway went very quiet.

Charlie Brown sat on the floor with one hand over his nose, blinking. A thin line of red had started down over his lip. Oh no. He stared at it on his fingers, and something in his face changed.

Linus stood very still. His arm was still half-raised, but he lowered it slowly.

He thought: this is what it looks like. He thought: we are not coming back from this hallway. He thought it with a terrible clarity. This shouldn’t have happened, not like this.

“Charlie—”

“Don’t,” said Charlie. His voice was strange. Flat in a way it hadn’t been before, all the heat gone out of it.

“I didn’t mean—”

“I know you didn’t.”

“Please, just let me—” Linus moved toward him and Charlie flinched back against the wall, fast, with both hands up, and Linus stopped.

“Stay away from me,” Charlie said. He didn’t seem angry; he was worse than angry. “Please. Just— stay away.”

“Your nose—”

“I said stay away.” His voice rose on the last word. He was looking at Linus with red-rimmed eyes, blood on his upper lip, and an expression Linus did not know how to hold against. “Please, I’m begging you. Just don’t. Leave me alone.”

Linus stayed where he was.

Charlie got up from the floor slowly, half because his body hurt, half because his soul hurt. He didn’t look at Linus at all. Instead, he looked at the door. He put one hand over his nose and walked to it, opening it. The rain came in cold from outside.

“Charlie,” said Linus, but his voice came out wrong. He hadn’t meant for it to come out like that… so watery, small, as if something was pressing on it from the inside.

At first, Charlie stopped in the doorway. He didn’t turn around. Then he walked out into the rain, and the door swung shut behind him.

· · ·

Linus stood in his own hallway for a long time. The lamp was still on in the living room. A book he planned to read was still open on the couch where he’d left it. Everything was exactly as it had been an hour ago except that it wasn’t, not really, not in any way that mattered.

He went to the window. He could see Charlie at the end of the street, a dark shape in the rain, moving away with his hand still at his face, hunched a little against the cold, trying — Linus could tell even from here — to be smaller than he was. It was no wonder that Charlie, at times, just wanted to take up less space in the world, and right now, it seemed like he wanted to pass through the night without being noticed, without anyone seeing, and especially without his parents seeing.

Linus watched until he couldn’t see him anymore.

Then he sat down on the floor of his hallway, which was damp where Charlie had dripped on it, and put his back against the wall and his knees up and stared at the ceiling. The house was very quiet; outside the rain continued its work.

He thought about winning. He thought about what it looked like, apparently. He thought about the sound in the hallway, and the blood on Charlie’s fingers, and the way he’d just told him to stay away from him. If was as if he were someone saying goodbye in a language that didn’t have a word for it. He looked scared.

Linus closed his eyes. He let out a breath, slowly, all the way down. It didn’t help, he hadn’t thought it would.

Finally, he let out a long, trembling sigh, and eventually, Linus gently closed the door.

`, song: { title: "Motherless Child", artist: "Martin L. Gore", cover: "https://resources.tidal.com/images/ed0a54b8/9094/4319/821e/e6e13cdeafaf/320x320.jpg", url: ["ZJn1VukFa4E", "ghR8w6FSu50"] } }, { id: "moes", condition: (ev) => charlieWin && cynicism > 5 && ruthlessness > 5, image: "https://i.pinimg.com/1200x/d3/44/25/d3442532e134104064fa174dd783590f.jpg", title: "two divided by zero", html: `
★
two divided by zero

The neon sign in the window of O’Malley’s Pub cast a dull, flickering red glow over the sticky mahogany of the bar. It was a Tuesday night, which meant the place was mostly empty, save for a couple of tired regulars nursing their drafts and the bartender half-asleep by the register.

At the far end of the counter, sitting perfectly still in the dim lighting, was Roy.

He didn’t look like a high schooler. Maybe he never really had. With the collar of his jacket turned up, his posture relaxed but guarded, and an eerily convincing fake license tucked safely back into his wallet, he fit right into the landscape of the dive bar, to his relief. He was nursing the last third of a Manhattan, staring at the cherry resting at the bottom of the glass as if it held the answers to the universe.

The heavy wooden door of the pub creaked open, letting in a sudden gust of the freezing night air. Roy didn’t turn his head, and just watched the red neon reflect in his drink. Footsteps approached before someone slid onto the vinyl barstool right next to him. Roy took a slow sip of his drink, and when he looked to his side, he didn’t seem surprised in the slightest.

“I thought I might find you here,” Patty said.

She wasn’t drinking, of course. She just rested her arms on the sticky counter, looking at him with that gaze she usually saved for the pitcher’s mound. The bartender gave her a passing glance, decided she wasn’t worth the trouble of carding if she wasn’t ordering, and went back to wiping glasses.

“You tracking me now, Patty?” Roy asked, his voice smooth, carrying its usual easy cadence. But there was a slight edge to it tonight… a subtle fraying at the seams.

“Not exactly,” she replied, brushing a stray leaf off the sleeve of her jacket. “Just figured you’d want to avoid the fallout zone. I came to see how things were with Chuck. And with the campaign, now that he actually pulled it off.”

Roy let out a quiet, hollow laugh. He swirled the amber liquid in his glass, listening to the ice clink against the rim.

“He pulled it off, alright,” Roy murmured. He stopped swirling the glass and finally looked up, staring straight at the rows of liquor bottles on the shelf opposite them. He raised a hand, rubbing his temples slowly. “I think I have a headache, Patty.”

Patty frowned. It wasn’t like Roy to admit discomfort. He was the guy who breezed through everything, the detached drifter who always knew what to say and when to leave. Seeing him sit here, genuinely worn down, felt wrong.

“You sick?” she asked softly.

“No.” He exhaled, a long, tired sound. “No, I’m just… this whole campaign, Patty. It really brought me down.”

· · ·

He turned his head to look at her. The neon light caught his eyes, and Patty was startled to see how profoundly exhausted he looked. The cool, impenetrable mask had completely slipped, leaving behind someone who looked as bruised as Charlie Brown did on a bad day.

“I don’t get it,” Roy said, his voice dropping into a tight, quiet register. “I really don’t. I’ve known Charlie for a long time. I thought I knew who he was. But like, how could he be so incredibly inconsiderate to someone who used to be his best friend?”

Patty looked down at the bar top. “Chuck was desperate, Roy. He just wanted to win for once.”

“Desperate isn’t an excuse to carve someone open on a stage,” Roy countered, shaking his head. He took another slow sip of his Manhattan, letting the bourbon burn down his throat. “He didn’t just beat Linus, he humiliated him. He took everything he knew about the kid and weaponized it. I stood there and watched him do it, Patty, and he didn’t even flinch.”

Roy set the glass down harder than he meant to. The clack echoed briefly in the quiet bar.

“And it wasn’t just Charlie,” he continued, his frustration bleeding out into the open. “It was all of them. How could Linus let Lucy and the others parade around like that? Throwing dirt, berating Charlie in the hallways, organizing those mobs just to break him down before he even got to the podium? It was vicious. It was sick.”

He leaned forward, resting his forehead against his hand, shielding his eyes from the red glow of the window.

“Why are they like this?” Roy whispered into the empty space between them. “It’s just a stupid student council election. It doesn’t matter, none of it matters. But they tore each other to pieces for it anyway.”

The silence that followed was heavy, filled only by the low hum of the pub’s refrigerator and the distant, muffled sound of a siren a few blocks away. Patty didn’t have an answer for him. She wished she did, though. She wished she could tell him that it was just a phase, or that things would go back to normal by next week, or that Charlie and Linus would eventually laugh it off over a game of baseball.

But she couldn’t. Because she had seen the look on Charlie’s face tonight, and she had seen the dark, silent house on Elm Street, and she knew the damage was deeper than she thought it would be. It had fundamentally changed the chemistry of their little world, and Roy, for all his cool detachment, was grieving the loss of it just as much as anyone else.

Patty placed her hand firmly over his on the sticky counter, and gave it a gentle, grounding squeeze.

“I know, Roy,” she said quietly, her voice full of a steady, sad grace. “I know.”

Roy, to her relief, didn’t pull away. He just sat there in the dim red light, staring at his half-empty glass, letting the warmth of her hand anchor him for a moment in a world that suddenly felt entirely too cold.

“Just…” Patty murmured, giving his hand one final squeeze before she slowly stood up from the stool. “Take care of yourself, okay?”

Roy looked up at her. He offered a small, broken smile that didn’t quite reach his eyes, a ghost of the effortless drifter he usually played so well.

“You too, Patty,” he said softly.

She turned and walked out into the freezing night, the door creaking shut behind her. Roy turned back to the bar, picked up his drink, and finished it in the dark.

`, song: { title: "She Fades Away", artist: "Alphaville", cover: "https://yt3.googleusercontent.com/gpJ3hEV2iZMHusRAsRUijML2NIiGt5Vl8Tt1cCjeR6JScXY8DNlh-XqikZz8mzNHxkom1Xup4uOYlpFK=w544-h544-l90-rj", url: ["LxGHOGcHcoU", "8RgrttqY91w"] } }, { id: "ouch", condition: (ev) => charlieWin && cynicism >= 4 && ruthlessness < 5, image: "https://file.garden/aNtAfG887DiA_7lO/TML_2000/tv.webp", title: "the sun always shines on t.v.", html: `
★
the sun always shines on t.v.

Linus Van Pelt stood on the porch of the Brown household. It was past midnight, and the rain was misting into the collar of his jacket.

He had rehearsed what he was going to say approximately twelve times on the walk over. “Charlie, congratulations. You ran a hard campaign, and the voters have spoken.” It was civil, it was polite, but it was the right thing to do, regardless of how much venom they had spat at each other over the last two months, and regardless of how heavily the loss was currently sitting in Linus’s own chest.

He knocked. Nothing. He knocked again, a little louder. Still nothing.

Frowning, Linus reached down and tried the doorknob. To his surprise, it turned; it seemed like Charlie had forgotten to lock it again.

Linus stepped inside, gently pushing the door shut behind him to keep the draft out. The house was entirely dark, lit only by the flickering, bluish glow of the television in the living room. The TV was muted, displaying a rapidly updating electoral graphic that showed exactly what Linus already knew. Snoopy was sitting at the bottom of the stairs, looking mildly distressed, which for the dog was a state of high alarm.

Then, Linus heard it.

It was a wet, guttural, agonizing sound echoing from the downstairs hallway. A choke, a gasp, and then another violent, echoing heave.

Linus froze in the entryway; the hair on the back of his neck stood up. For one brief, terrifying, entirely irrational second, his mind supplied the absolute worst: Charlie Brown was choking on his own tongue. Or he had been poisoned? Or maybe the stress had finally snapped his brain, and he was turning into some kind of undead ghoul in the downstairs bathroom. Oh, heavens.

“Charlie?” Linus called out, dropping his umbrella on the rug.

Another horrible, retching sound answered him.

At that point, Linus stopped thinking and started moving. He bolted down the hall and barged into the bathroom.

“Charlie, are you— oh.”

Charlie Brown was collapsed on the bathmat, his arms wrapped desperately around the porcelain bowl of the toilet, panting heavily. He was pale, shivering faintly, and looked completely, utterly defeated. The room smelled sharply of stomach acid and… God, was that Cheerios?

And Charlie slowly turned his head, resting his cheek against the toilet seat. His eyes were bloodshot and glassy. He looked at Linus, blinking slowly as if his brain was struggling to process the visual information, and then looked back down at the water.

“I threw up,” Charlie whispered. His voice was hoarse and incredibly small.

“I can see that,” Linus said.

“I think it was the milk,” Charlie mumbled, closing his eyes. “Or the anxiety. Or both, I dunno.”

It was a very awkward moment. The newly elected president of the student council, lying on the floor in his pajamas, smelling of vomit, looking up at the opponent he had just defeated. All the grand speeches, all the bitter debates, all the philosophical arguments about the future of the school… well, to Linus, it all instantly evaporated in the fluorescent light of the bathroom.

Linus let out a long, quiet breath through his nose. He didn’t say his rehearsed speech. In fact, he didn’t mention the election at all.

· · ·

Instead, he walked over, knelt on the bathmat, and put a firm hand on Charlie’s clammy shoulder.

“Come here,” Linus said softly.

He grabbed Charlie by the arm and helped him to his feet. Charlie was unsteady, and his legs were trembling as he leaned heavily against Linus’s side. Linus reached over, flushed the toilet, and then guided Charlie to the sink. He grabbed a washcloth from the rack, ran it under the cold tap, and gently wiped the sweat and mess from Charlie’s face.

Charlie didn’t resist. He just stood there, letting it happen, to his relief. Linus opened the medicine cabinet, found a small paper cup, filled it with water, and pressed it into Charlie’s hand without much ceremony.

“Rinse,” Linus ordered.

Charlie rinsed, and he spit it into the sink. Jeez. He looked terrible, like a ghost haunting his own house. Not enjoying the experience, he looked away.

“Do you need a doctor?” Linus asked, and his brow furrowed with genuine concern. “Should I wake up your parents?”

“No,” Charlie sighed, leaning the back of his head against the doorframe, and closing his eyes. “No, please. I just need… I just need the world to stop spinning for a minute.”

Linus nodded. He put an arm around Charlie’s waist and guided him out of the bathroom, down the short hallway, and into the living room. He carefully lowered Charlie onto the couch. Charlie immediately curled into a tight ball, pulling his knees to his chest and shivering, trying to occupy as little space as possible.

Looking around, Linus spotted a heavy, knit throw blanket draped over the armchair, and pulled it down. This one would suffice, he thought. And so he shook it out and draped it carefully over Charlie, tucking the edges in tightly around his shoulders and under his feet, so the cold air wouldn’t get to him. It wasn’t his blanket, of course… but the motion was the exact same. It was a gesture of security, all of this.

On the television, the news anchor was pointing to a graphic displaying some of the night’s margins. He didn’t want to hear that. Linus picked up the remote and turned the television off. And so the room plunged into a soft, quiet darkness, save for the amber glow of the streetlight filtering through the windowpanes.

Linus set the remote down and sat on the edge of the couch, right near Charlie’s chest. Again, he didn’t say a word.

Charlie didn’t speak either, but after a minute, he reached out from under the heavy knit throw. His hand, shaking slightly, found Linus’s sleeve. He didn’t pull on it, no, but he held on, gripping the fabric as if making sure Linus wouldn’t disappear into the dark. Perhaps unsurprisingly, Linus didn’t pull away. He adjusted his position, reached down, and gently took Charlie’s hand in his own.

“You’re okay, Charlie Brown,” Linus whispered to the quiet room, letting his thumb slowly rub the back of Charlie’s knuckles. “You’re okay. Don’t let anyone change that. Just rest.”

And for the first time in two months, Charlie Brown closed his eyes, let out a long, shuddering breath, and, moments later, fell into a deep sleep.

`, song: { title: "Are You Someone?", artist: "Radiohead", cover: "https://file.garden/aNtAfG887DiA_7lO/TML_2000/areyousomeone.png", url: ["7hHMjap09js"] } }, { id: "lonelynight", condition: (ev) => charlieWin && ruthlessness >= 4, image: "https://i.pinimg.com/1200x/5e/83/c5/5e83c5c2c758e2d6dee65eaaa0c8aedb.jpg", html: `
★
we can funk

The campaign buttons were still sitting in a cardboard box by the front door, staring up at him like hundreds of tiny, accusatory eyes.

Charlie Brown had won. It was official as of two hours ago. He was the new student council president, as hard as it was to imagine. But as he sat on the edge of his bed, looking at the wall, he didn’t feel the rush of validation he had spent the last two months chasing. He mostly just felt completely, hollowly exhausted.

He had played dirty. Terribly, ruthlessly dirty. When the polls had started slipping in October, it was like something inside him had simply snapped. He had done things that he never thought he would do to win, that he hated to do, and yet, he won. He finally won.

By the time the doorbell rang, he didn’t jump. He just stood up, slowly, feeling like he was moving underwater, and walked downstairs to open it.

Peppermint Patty was leaning against the porch railing, twirling a set of car keys around her index finger. She was wearing her dad’s old leather jacket, and the brisk night wind was messing with her hair. When she looked up at him, her expression was an unusual mix of awe, unease, and a strange sort of pity.

“Well,” she said, catching the keys in her palm. “You did it, Chuck.”

“Yeah,” he said softly. “I did.”

She studied his face for a long moment, taking in the dark circles under his eyes and the completely defeated slope of his shoulders. “I gotta admit,” she murmured, stepping a little closer. “I didn’t know you had it in you. This whole campaign… it was brutal, Chuck. It was like watching a completely different person.”

Charlie swallowed the lump of ash in his throat and looked away. “It’s politics, Patty. People do what they have to do.”

Patty didn’t argue. She just jingled her keys again, letting the metallic sound cut through the quiet suburban street. “Well, grab your coat. We’re going for a ride.”

He blinked. “A ride? Where?”

“Anywhere that isn’t this house,” she told him firmly. “You won an election! You’re like the president now. The least we can do is get some food and celebrate before you turn into a ghost completely.”

· · ·

Twenty minutes later, they were cruising down the interstate in Patty’s battered old sedan; poor Todd, despite news to the contrary, was still kicking it. The heating vents were blasting warm air, smelling faintly of dust and old vanilla air fresheners. Between them, on the center console, sat an assortment of cheap victories: two large sodas, a crushed bag of sour gummies, and a greasy paper sleeve of lukewarm french fries. It was good.

Patty had the radio tuned to a classic rock station, with the volume dialed down just enough to allow the hum of the engine to fill the space between them. She was doing most of the talking, keeping up a steady, one-sided stream of chatter about baseball spring training and how much she hated her chemistry teacher.

But Charlie was half-empty. He was zoned out, his head resting against the cool glass of the passenger window, watching the yellow streetlights blur past. He was physically there, sure, but mentally, he felt untethered, for the lack of a better word, drifting somewhere far above the car.

Every now and then, Patty would toss a fry in his direction or nudge his shoulder, and he would manage to lend her a slight, vague smile. It wasn’t a real smile, given it didn’t reach his eyes, but it was enough to keep her from pressing the issue. But he was trying. He really was.

“…so I told her, look, if you want me to memorize the periodic table, you’re gonna have to make it rhyme,” Patty was saying, taking a long pull from her soda straw. “Otherwise, it’s just alphabet soup. Right, Chuck?”

“Right,” he murmured, his breath fogging the glass slightly. His slight smile lingered a bit; it was a weak, automatic reflex.

Eventually Patty signaled, taking the off-ramp that curved back toward their side of town. The commercial glow of the highway faded, replaced by the quiet, tree-lined darkness of the far more boring residential neighborhoods. Charlie watched the houses roll by, his mind blank, just letting the motion of the car carry him.

And then, they turned onto Elm Street.

Charlie didn’t realize where they were until they were already halfway down the block. Patty hadn’t done it on purpose; it was just the fastest route back to his place, but as the familiar shape of the Van Pelt house materialized out of the dark, Charlie felt his stomach drop into his shoes.

He sat up slightly, pulling his head away from the window. God, the house was pitch black. Not a single light was on, inside or out. Even the porch light, which Linus’s mother always kept burning until midnight, was extinguished. It looked abandoned. What a nightmare…

In his mind’s eye, Charlie could see exactly what was happening behind those dark windows. He could see Linus, sitting in his room, humiliated and betrayed, holding onto his blanket in the absolute quiet of a devastating loss. A loss that Charlie had orchestrated, too.

The slight smile that had been keeping his face together instantly vanished. It was replaced by a heavy, crushing frown, as the full weight of what he had done crashed down on him all at once in the passenger seat of Patty’s car. He hadn’t just defeated an opponent; he had destroyed someone who once was his best friend. And for what? For a title? For a stupid badge that meant nothing in the dark?

Patty noticed this shift. She glanced over, following his line of sight to the dark house, and her grip tightened slightly on the steering wheel. She didn’t say anything. There was nothing for either of them to say anymore.

`, song: { title: "A Question of Time (Remix)", artist: "Depeche Mode", cover: "https://yt3.googleusercontent.com/rrU_3mlqZTDSalQ9h2Rh0hKB6wRnPeCb_P8mN-d1cxyaAgENV_ckxPy4aiissPtP9TsI9Va5432qG-nnkA=w544-h544-l90-rj", url: "RlyOV_6551Q" } }, { id: "gorelinuswin", condition: (ev) => ev > 269 && linusWin, image: "https://i.pinimg.com/1200x/e5/14/bc/e514bcc277c7eabc51d8a668cd617702.jpg", title: "train of thought", html: `
★
train of thought

It was a smaller gathering than he’d imagined, admittedly. Not that he’d imagined it obsessively, or anything like that, or more than once a day across the last four weeks of campaigning. But he just thought that, hypothetically, there might have been more streamers.

Linus Van Pelt, now the Student Council President-elect, sat on his living room floor and pretended to look like he was perfectly content with nine people and a bowl of pretzels. He was not, but in fairness, it was nine very good people. Sally was there, and Franklin, who had driven over uninvited. He did that a lot, too; he just appeared and disappeared, but to be fair, when he did appear, he did become necessary; he had been helping Schroeder with Linus’ home theater system. The television was on, and across it, in several shades of red and blue and gray, but especially blue, the national election returns were scrolling in a way that nobody had really asked for, but that everyone kept looking at anyway.

Well, his parents were home.

This had not been the plan. The plan had involved his parents being at his Aunt Ruth’s, but his Aunt Ruth had apparently had some strong feelings about the returns. Maybe that wasn’t so surprising, given she was a Bill Bradley supporter and the closest thing to a Democratic voter in his family. And so they were all here now, sitting at the kitchen table with expressions Linus had learned, from his years of careful study, to interpret as we are not angry, we are just disappointed in the direction of the country. In fact, his mother had already turned the television off twice, and his father had turned it back on. The atmosphere in the Van Pelt household wasn’t all that good. If anything, it was pretty similar to air pressure before a thunderstorm, which Linus had once written a paper about, and which was characteristically bad timing.

Still, he had won. The polls had said so, and the school had said so, and even Mr. Harris from the main office had given him what Linus believed to be a nod of acknowledgment when the results were posted. Voila, he was a winner! He’d given his speech in the gymnasium, to a very decent crowd, and he thought it had gone well. He’d nearly left his blanket at home for it, thinking it was unpresidential, and maybe it was, but Sally had looked at him with a bit of a, erm, funny expression that communicated several things without words. Chief among them, do not, and so it was there, tucked under his arm, and he was beginning to think that was right.

He was, after all, elected as himself.

Schroeder had found a song that felt just triumphant but not too aggressive for him — U2’s Beautiful Day —, and was rotating it at a thoughtful volume out of respect for the general atmospheric pressure of the house. Meanwhile, Franklin tried to make himself comfortable in the room, as if he had been there longer than anyone else present… and Sally was just eating pretzels in the kitchen. How boring.

· · ·

The call came at ten forty-seven, which was fairly late. Linus’s phone was on the arm of the sofa, and the contact name read: CHARLIE BROWN (DO NOT PICK UP DURING DEBATE PREP).

He looked at it for a moment.

Franklin looked at him looking at it. He tried his best to ignore this.

Linus picked up and walked into the hallway, which was quieter, and where the television was not.

“Charlie Brown,” he said.

There was a pause on the other end that had a very specific texture to it. He had known Charlie Brown long enough to understand the taxonomy of his silences. This one, generally speaking, was much flatter and quieter, and so he could gauge he was not happy at all.

“Hey,” said Charlie Brown.

“Hey,” said Linus.

Another pause. In the background of the call he could hear what sounded like a television, and maybe someone like Snoopy doing something ambiguous, but it overall became clear to him that this was the quiet of a house where nobody was having a party.

“So you won,” Charlie said. It wasn’t a question, but it wasn’t quite flat either. Little did Linus know that Charlie had been rehearsing something and then, at the last moment, decided to say a different thing instead.

“I did,” said Linus.

“Right.” A pause. “Well. That’s— yeah, that’s— I mean, good. Good for you, right? Y-you ran a… ran a…. erm, ran a good campaign.”

Linus leaned against the hallway wall. “So did you, Charlie Brown.”

“But I lost.”

“But your ideas were good.”

There was a long exhale on the other end that was not quite a laugh. “’Your ideas were good.’ Oh great. I’ll tattoo that on my fucking head.”

The television in the other room went off again. Some muffled voices, then on again. Linus closed his eyes briefly. God, were his parents going to do that all night? Not this time, for Christ’s sake.

“Are you— is anyone with you?” he asked.

“Sally’s at your thing, I guess.”

“We— well, she is, yes.”

“And Schroeder’s probably there.”

“Also yes. He’s my friend, you know.”

“So.” Charlie let out a breath. “It’s pretty much just me and Snoopy here. Which is fine, uh… he doesn’t really understand elections, so he’s been in pretty good spirits.”

“That sounds like him.”

“He keeps just staring at the television. I-I don’t know what he thinks is happening.”

Linus smiled, quietly, in a way he was aware nobody could see. “That also sounds like him. But maybe he does.”

· · ·

There was another silence, a different kind this time. Charlie had gone somewhere a bit further away from the phone, or maybe just further away in general, as he was cycling toward something he didn’t quite want to say.

“I really thought,” he started, and then stopped. “I just— I really thought, this time—”

“I know,” said Linus.

“People kept saying I had a real shot—”

“I know.”

“—and I know you’re going to say the polls—”

“I’m not going to say anything about it, I promise.”

Charlie Brown went quiet. A different kind of quiet or not, Linus waited in it and did not fill it. This was unnerving.

“I’m tired,” Charlie said, finally. Not as a complaint exctly, or not only as a complaint, but merely as a fact. Linus did believe him.

“I know,” said Linus. As repetitive as it sounded, he meant it. He had known Charlie Brown since before either of them were old enough to understand what it cost to be someone like this, a person who kept trying, trying, trying, and failing. If anything, Charlie Brown had been failing it longer than most. “You should get some sleep.”

“Yeah.” Another pause. “Yeah, I will.”

Noticeably, he didn’t move to hang up.

Linus shifted the phone to his other ear. From down the hall, he could hear Sally laughing at something Schroeder had said, and the television again, and his father saying something to his mother. Whatever it was, their conversation was likely not finished, but was being put away for now.

“Hey, Charlie,” he said.

“What.”

He thought about it for a moment. He had a lot of things he was good at saying: organized things, argued things, citations and reference… he was less practiced at the other kind, but he said it anyway.

“Tomorrow, uh… when you see me… I just want you to know I’ll treat you with respect. Alright? You deserve it, really.”

Yet another pause.

“Okay.”

“I mean it.”

“I know you do,” replied Charlie. “That’s the thing about you, Linus. You always mean it.” He didn’t sound entirely sure whether that was a compliment. “Good night.”

“Good night, Charlie Brown.”

He stood in the hallway for another moment after the call ended, with the phone in his hand and the quiet around him. Through the wall he could hear the television, and then his mother’s voice, and then the television again, and he realized he was going to have to deal with this throughout the rest of the night, or what was left of it.

He went back into the living room.

Franklin looked at him. Linus gave a small shrug that meant it’s fine and also it isn’t, quite, but that’s all right. Maybe it was? Franklin nodded, which meant he understood both parts.

Sally handed him the pretzels without being asked.

Calmly, Linus sat back down on the floor, with his blanket across his knees, and ate a pretzel, and thought: well. He had won something today. Several things, even… and tomorrow he would go to school, and there would be a great deal to do, and it would probably, sure be difficult…

He just hoped all of this was worth it.

`, song: { title: "Hummer", artist: "The Smashing Pumpkins", cover: "https://resources.tidal.com/images/a61f0af3/6669/4f7c/a979/1465fe60b01a/320x320.jpg", url: "Zxo_MYrADmY" } }, { id: "bushlinuswin", condition: (ev) => ev < 269 && linusWin, image: "https://i.pinimg.com/1200x/7c/33/6f/7c336f326039a670630c70e4960df460.jpg", title: "back to the shack", html: `
★
back to the shack

It was terrible. The gym smelled like streamers and spilled fruit punch, and every flat surface had someone’s shoe on it. Linus Van Pelt, now the Student Council President-elect, stood in the approximate center of this good chaos, and tried to look like a man who had expected exactly this all along.

Of course, he had expected exactly this all along. The polls had said so. Every poll had given him at least a seventeen-point lead. Still, he thought, there is something about winning that refused to feel familiar to him until he was actually in it, surrounded by such uncultured people.

Schroeder had found a portable speaker from somewhere and was playing something triumphant, and hopefully not German, that nobody could identify but that felt exactly right. Franklin, who shouldn’t have been there but could never miss the opportunity, was shaking hands with what seemed like a structurally improbable number of people. And even Pig-Pen, who was a patriot at heart, had thrown an entire bag of confetti at himself.

The school had, as it turned out, come to its senses. They always did, Linus thought — eventually. You just had to give them the facts, state your case clearly, and then trust, with a kind of faith that was admittedly difficult to maintain around the third week of campaigning, that people were fundamentally reasonable.

His security blanket was tucked under one arm. He’d nearly left it at home for the victory speech, thinking it unpresidential, but Sally had looked at him with an expression that said “don’t you dare” without moving her mouth, and so here it was, and here he was, and he was beginning to suspect it had been the right call all along.

· · ·

The call came at eleven-fourteen. Linus had just accepted a second cup of fruit punch; it was a gift, from a seventh grader whose name he thankfully hadn’t caught, and who looked at him as if he were someone bestowing something sacred. Pathetic. It was then that his phone buzzed on the folding table.

The contact name read: CHARLIE BROWN (DO NOT PICK UP DURING DEBATE PREP).

Sighing, he picked up.

The sound on the other end was approximately what he had anticipated. There was a long, wet, and uh, architecturally complex sniff. Then a pause, then another sniff, of a slightly different register, and finally, Charlie Brown said, in a voice that was doing an extremely brave impression of being composed:

“Linus.”

“Charlie Brown.”

“Well,” said Charlie Brown. “Well. I suppose. I suppose you— snf— y-you think you’re pretty smart.”

At that point, Schroeder had turned the music down. Several people were looking at Linus, knowing that something interesting was about to be happening. Linus straightened up and felt a complicated duty settle across his shoulders, not unlike a mortarboard.

“I think,” he said, warmly and at a volume just appropriate enough for everyone to hear, “that I’m very grateful. Really, Charlie Brown, truly.”

“Grateful,” said Charlie, on the other end, with a wet noise. “Grateful. You wanna know what you are? You are— you are a two-faced, wishy-washy, pseudo-intellectual, pompous little b—”

“—example of what this school can be,” Linus finished, aloud, as if reading it off a card. He smiled at Schroeder, who gave him a careful thumbs-up. “That means so much coming from you, really.”

There was a sound on the other end that sounded like he was trying to swallow a cactus.

“I,” replied Charlie, “did not say—”

“I know,” said Linus, pleasantly. “I know exactly what you said, Charlie Brown. And I want you to know I hear you. I hear all of it.”

Another long pause, another sniff. Shit, was he going to do that all night? There was a muffled sound in the background that Linus thought might be Snoopy, though Snoopy’s contribution to the emotional atmosphere of any given situation was always a matter of interpretation. Poor dog.

“You know what your problem is?” Charlie said, and his voice was going thin and cracked around the edges. “You always think you know everything. You with your— your philosophy, and that fucking blanket, and your big words—”

“Your generosity tonight is really something,” said Linus, to the rest of the gym. Franklin gave him a look. Was he still supposed to be there? Linus gave Franklin a very small shake of his head anyway.

“—and nobody ever listens to me and I had good ideas, Linus, I had genuinely good ideas, and everyone just— everyone just laughs, they always just laugh, and I—”

There was a pause in which Linus stopped performing for everyone else.

“I know, Charlie Brown,” he said, quieter now. “I know you did.”

· · ·

There was another long silence. In it, Linus could hear, very faintly, some breathing on the other side of the line. Charlie seemed tired. This was not unusual, obviously, but he recognized it because he had heard it many times before, across many years, and because Charlie Brown had always required exactly this much effort just to get through an ordinary day, which was perhaps why he was the way he was, which was, erm, to say… exhausting, and impossible, and, once, the truest friend Linus had ever had.

“I’m gonna go,” said Charlie Brown, finally.

“Okay,” said Linus.

“I mean it, I’m going.”

“I know, I know.”

The line stayed open, though. In the background of the call, Linus could hear the quiet sounds of the Brown household. It seemed he wasn’t having much fun with anyone tonight.

“Okay,” said Charlie Brown. “Okay, well. Congratulations, I guess. Good night.”

He hung up.

Or — Linus was fairly certain he hadn’t, actually. At least not yet. He stood there another moment, with the phone to his ear, listening to the silence that was not quite silence, and then he said, very quietly, “Good night, Charlie Brown,” and ended the call himself.

Eventually, the gym reconvened around him as Schroeder turned the music back up. Someone put more fruit punch in his cup without asking, which he found oddly touching. And Franklin clapped him on the shoulder, saying something that was lost in the general noise and probably didn’t need to be heard. He would hear a lot of these tonight, too.

Then Lucy was beside him. She had materialized from, uh, wherever Lucy materialized from — she did this; she had always done this, too. Although she seemed to have been paying attention to him, Lucy would never intend to admit this.

“So,” she said.

“So?” said Linus.

“We’ve got a lot of stuff to do now, you know. Real, uh, stuff! School board meetings, budget hearings… oh, I feel like a superintendent already! Someone’s got to fix the situation with the vending machines in the east hall, you know. That’s been a problem for like, three semesters now?”

“I’m aware,” said Linus.

Lucy reached over, without ceremony, and put her hand on top of his head. It was a single pat. For someone who had known him since before he had opinions, which for Linus was a very short interval indeed, that was probably enough.

“Come on,” she said. “Schroeder found more punch.”

She walked back into the noise. Linus stood still a moment longer, fruit punch in hand, now president of something, listening to the gymnasium celebrate.

He sighed.

`, song: { title: "Corridor of Dreams", artist: "The Cleaners from Venus", cover: "https://i.scdn.co/image/ab67616d0000b2732820c6f455e8273ef29be439", url: "Yqh-WPtmWMo" } }, { id: "charliegorewin", condition: (ev) => ev > 269 && charlieWin, image: "https://i.pinimg.com/1200x/67/74/4d/67744db6e6f1a96a87d9f7087b5e499a.jpg", title: "murder suicide meteor slave", html: `
★
murder suicide meteor slave

The television had been off for about two hours.

Charlie Brown was sitting on the floor of the living room, with his back against the couch, his knees pulled up, and beside a bowl of cereal that he had made at some point and then not eaten, and which had now achieved a consistency that was honestly no longer really cereal and not quite anything else. Snoopy was asleep on the couch directly behind him. The house was fairly quiet; his parents, though home, had run out of things to say and were long asleep. This happened sometimes on nights like this. News was too much to keep talking around, he thought.

He had turned the television off himself. He hadn’t meant to watch it in the first place; he’d just sort of ended up in front of it, and then the results had started coming in, and then—

Well.

He didn’t want to think about that.

His phone was on the floor beside him, face-down, because Charlie had put it that way on purpose. It had been buzzing on and off for a while, and he was aware of this. He was choosing, in what he felt was a very mature and considered way, not to engage with it. Whatever was happening out there — at the school, in the world, in the specific gym where the results had apparently been announced —, oh, he could engage with that tomorrow. Or possibly never. Hopefully never; never had a certain appeal right now.

The doorbell rang at eleven twenty-three. But Charlie Brown did not move.

It rang again.

Snoopy lifted his head, regarded the general direction of the door, and then put his head back down. This was not, apparently, his problem.

The third ring was longer, and was followed immediately by knocking. It was at that point that Charlie Brown closed his eyes. Then he got up, because there was clearly no avoiding it, and he looked, he knew, like someone who had been sitting on a floor for two hours doing absolutely nothing, because he had been, and there was nothing to be done about that now.

And so he opened the door.

Peppermint Patty was standing on his porch with her jacket half-unzipped and her hair blowing in the wind, as amusing as it looked. The expression on her face that he recognized, after a moment, was something close to relief. She looked at him, he looked at her. God, he looked like total crap, as much as Patty would never admit it. His general situation made it evident enough.

“Chuck,” she said.

“Patty,” he said.

“I called you like eight times, what the hell.”

“I imagined.”

“I even called your house phone, Chuck, thinking you would definitely pick up. Why can’t you just answer?”

“I’m sorry,” he said, which he was, genuinely, though he was also too tired to be very sorry about anything right now. “I just— I didn’t really want to talk.”

She looked at him for another moment. Then she said, “Okay, but here’s the thing,” and she said it in a strangely calm, soothing voice. “You won, Charlie Brown.”

· · ·

He stared at her.

“What,” he said.

“You won the student council race, yeah. You’re the president.” She said it plainly and clearly, without much decoration. “It came in about an hour ago. Marcie called me, and I tried to call you, and then I— so here I am.” A beat. “Here you are.”

Charlie looked at her. He looked past her, at the porch, and the street beyond it, and the dark that was just ordinary dark. He then looked back at her.

“No,” he said.

“Yes.”

“No, Patty.”

“Chuck—”

“That didn’t happen.” He shook his head, once. “That doesn’t— that’s not a thing that happened. That couldn’t have happened.”

She put her hands in her jacket pockets. “Of course it did. Why not?”

“Because,” he said, and his voice was shaking a bit, “it’s me. It’s— Patty, it’s me. That’s not how it goes. I’ve run for things before and it always— I always—”

He stopped and pressed his mouth together. Somewhere behind his eyes, his brain was making a very poor effort at holding itself in place, and he was aware of this, and he was also aware of Patty standing on his porch watching for it to happen. He wished, very sincerely, that he were anyone else in the world right now, anyone at all, anyone who cried less visibly (or at least more elegantly), or at least anyone whose face didn’t do the specific awful thing his face was currently doing.

Charlie had never been a pretty crier. This had always been true. Every time he cried it involved his whole face, and some of the neck, and sounds that he would have preferred not to make in front of another person. He’d cried in front of Patty before — once, actually, over the red-haired girl, which had been mortifying, and also once at the end of a very bad baseball season, which had also been mortifying —, and she had never once been unkind about it. She wasn’t unkind about it now.

She stepped forward, off the porch and over the threshold without asking, and put her arms around him. It was blunt and unceremonious, which it was the way she did most things, but it was without hesitation and without making it weird. And so Charlie Brown stood there in his own doorway, crying in a genuinely embarrassing way, and she just held on.

“It’s true,” she said, into the approximate vicinity of his ear. “Marcie checked it twice, you know she always checks it twice.”

“I can’t—” he started.

“I know.”

“It doesn’t make any—”

“I know, Chuck.”

“This never—” He exhaled, something ragged and unfinished. “This never happens to me.”

“Well,” said Patty. She didn’t say it dismissively, he thought. “Maybe it does now.”

· · ·

After a while, the crying stopped, mostly because he ran out of tears. Charlie Brown straightened up and wiped his face with his sleeve and looked, he was certain, absolutely terrible, and Patty looked at him with an expression that contained neither pity nor embarrassment on his behalf. It was one of the things about her that he had never fully known how to account for.

“Okay,” she said.

“Okay,” he said.

Behind him, Snoopy had gotten off the couch. He pressed his nose briefly against the back of Charlie Brown’s knee, which was the most he was willing to offer emotionally, and then sat down as if he were there to supervise him. Maybe he was.

“The thing is,” said Charlie “I don’t know how to do this.”

“Nobody does,” noted Patty. “You figure it out.”

“What if I’m bad at it?”

She shrugged. “Then you’re bad at it for a while and then you get better, Charlie.” She looked at him steadily. “We’ll live through this. You know that, right?”

He looked at her.

“Yeah,” he said, after a moment. He didn’t quite believe it yet, but he thought he might, eventually, and that seemed like enough for tonight. “Yeah, okay.”

Patty nodded once, satisfied, and then looked past him at the uneaten cereal on the floor and the general atmosphere of the room.

“You got anything that isn’t… uh… cereal?” she said.

Charlie almost laughed. It wasn’t quite a laugh, but it was something in that direction, which he thought was probably the best he could do tonight, and which felt, surprisingly, like enough.

“I’ll check,” he said, and held the door open, and she came in out of the dark.

`, song: { title: "New Genius (Brother)", artist: "Gorillaz", cover: "https://file.garden/aNtAfG887DiA_7lO/TML_2000/newgenious.webp", url: ["77m-RvWF2uo", "WFrd_HyHf3E"] } }, { id: "charliebushwin", condition: (ev) => ev < 269 && charlieWin, image: "https://i.pinimg.com/1200x/bc/38/da/bc38da9ff4ef4462168d621ddf567b2e.jpg", title: "mad world", html: `
★
it's a mad, mad, mad world

The television had been off for about two hours.

Charlie Brown was sitting on the floor of the living room, his back pressed hard against the base of the couch. Beside him sat a bowl of cereal he hadn’t touched, with the milk having long since turned the flakes into a depressive, beige sludge. Snoopy was dead asleep on the cushions just above his head, softly snoring. The house was stiflingly quiet; his parents were asleep upstairs, blissfully unaware of the knot twisting in their son’s stomach.

He had turned the television off himself. He hadn’t meant to watch the local broadcast in the first place, but the election results had started rolling in, and the anxiety had spiked so fiercely that staring at a blank screen felt like the only form of self-preservation left. God, it was terrible.

The house phone rested face-down on the rug. It had buzzed frantically a dozen times in the last hour. But Charlie was choosing, in what he felt was an act of profound self-care, to pretend it didn’t exist. Whatever disaster had unfolded at the school gym, oh, he could read about it tomorrow. Or next week. Or maybe he could just transfer schools and get over it.

The doorbell rang at eleven twenty-three.

Charlie Brown squeezed his eyes shut. He didn’t move, or at least didn’t want to. A few seconds later, a sharp, rhythmic knocking rattled the doorframe. Snoopy huffed, cracked one eye open to glare at the entryway, and immediately went back to sleep. Truly man’s best friend.

With a heavy, trembling sigh, Charlie forced himself up from the floor. He knew he looked awful; he was pale, exhausted, and worse, he had spent the entire night bracing for a public humiliation. As much as he didn’t want to see who it was, he realized that noise probably wouldn’t go away soon. And so, he shuffled to the door and pulled it open, fully expecting to face his own doom.

Roy was standing on the porch, hands shoved deep into the pockets of his jacket, looking entirely too relaxed for the hour.

“Hey shitass,” Roy said, with a lazy, lopsided grin spreading across his face. “You won.”

· · ·

Charlie stared at him.

The wind blew past them, rustling the dry leaves on the lawn. Charlie looked at Roy, then at the empty street behind him, and then back at Roy’s face, searching for the punchline.

“What,” Charlie whispered.

“You won,” Roy repeated, stepping over the threshold without waiting for an invitation. “Seriously, you actually won. Student council president, all that jazz. It’s done.”

Charlie felt the air leave his lungs. “No,” he said, his voice cracking.

“Yeah, man. I wouldn’t drag myself all the way out here at midnight to lie to you.”

“Roy, no. That doesn’t— that’s not how it works.” He backed up a step, his chest heaving as a sudden, terrible panic seized him. “It’s me. People don’t vote for me. No, no, I lose. I always lose, Roy, they must have miscounted, they’re going to recount it tomorrow and I’m going to look like a fool—”

And then, to his absolute horror, Charlie began to cry.

It wasn’t a gentle, relieved sort of crying, unfortunately. It was the messy, ugly, heartbreaking sob of someone who had carried a heavy weight for so long that having it suddenly lifted felt like a physical injury. It was hard for Charlie to explain this to anyone, and so he didn’t. Soon, the tears spilled over hot and fast, with his shoulders shaking as he buried his face in his hands, utterly devastated by his own victory.

Roy didn’t flinch. He didn’t look pitying, nor did he make it weird. But he sure felt bad for him. He just shut the front door behind him, hooked a foot around a stray pair of sneakers to kick them out of the way, and rested a heavy, grounding hand on Charlie’s shaking shoulder.

“Whoa, hey. Chill out, man,” Roy said, his voice dropping into a low, easy cadence. “Breathe. Nobody miscounted, really. You did it. Just let it go.”

Charlie hiccuped, trying desperately to scrub the tears away with his sleeves. “I can’t… I don’t know how to do this…”

“Nobody does. But we’re not worrying about that tonight,” Roy replied, giving his shoulder a final squeeze before walking right past him, and heading deeper into the house. “Right now, we’re gonna unwind a bit. I got something good in store for us.”

Charlie sniffled, blinking through blurry eyes as he turned to follow. “You do?”

“Yeah,” Roy called out. A second later, the bright, hum-heavy light of the kitchen fridge spilled out into the hallway. “Assuming you’ve got anything in here that isn’t expired… uh… Jesus, Charlie, do you guys just survive on condiments?”

· · ·

An hour later, the living room felt entirely different.

They were sprawled out on the couch, Snoopy having been unceremoniously shooed to the opposite end. Between them sat a bizarre feast consisting of leftover cold cuts, half a box of crackers, and two sodas Roy had triumphantly unearthed from the back of the crisper drawer. What a ride! In fact, it had miraculously managed to bleed the adrenaline out of Charlie’s system.

Now, the exhaustion was setting in.

Charlie was slouched low against the cushions, his eyelids drooping heavily as the quiet of the night wrapped around them. He was staring blankly at the ceiling, with his voice still low and raspy from the crying.

“…it’s a miracle, really,” Charlie mumbled, half to himself, with his sentences beginning to slur together as sleep dragged him under. “An actual miracle. Things like this, they don’t happen to me. I thought for sure it was a joke. I thought everyone was just playing along to see the look on my face. But it’s real. I have been blessed. A miracle…”

He kept rambling, soft and disjointed. He was talking about the ballots, then the campaign posters, and how the sky looked that morning, until his words eventually dissolved into a soft, steady rhythm of breathing.

Roy sat beside him, holding a half-empty soda can, listening quietly until the rambling stopped.

He turned his head, watching the gentle rise and fall of Charlie’s chest. For a long moment, a fond, achingly sad smile touched Roy’s lips. He knew exactly how much the kid had gone through to get to a point where a simple win felt like an anomaly of the universe. This made him feel bad; it wasn’t like he made his life any better before today.

Roy set his can down on the coffee table. He reached over, flicked off the small table lamp, plunging the room into comfortable darkness, and shifted down against the armrest, settling into the cushions to sleep right there next to him.

`, song: { title: "Leave The Others Alone", artist: "Sophie Ellis-Bextor", cover: "https://yt3.googleusercontent.com/lSqP1MVlO_kX_27wkYeh-zlWsch6i35HdFFeFiOntSxX-dFLXkcK74d0pseOesQBFbj7j2jrzKl6Gqb-=w544-h544-s-l90-rj", url: "zHYWIN_0SJQ" } }, ]; // bonus slides (3+) // the code will check ALL of these. if condition is true, it gets added // note we uses slide 2 (id 1) and 3 (id 2) for the congress and cabinet slides const bonusSlides = [ { id: "tom", condition: (ev, op, leftThirdEV, rightThirdEV, right2ThirdEV) => (leftThirdEV > 269), image: "https://file.garden/aNtAfG887DiA_7lO/TML_2000/tom.jpg", html: `
Tom Holland Have A Little Mercy
Part Two
Chapter Seven
The Piss Poems
Being a brief, embarrassing, and entirely voluntary
account of what I wrote between 2 and 4 a.m.
for approximately eighteen months
— ✦ —

The body has its own hours. The mind,
left alone with it, will say anything.

— Sufjan Stevens, from a Tumblr post, 2014

I should explain the piss poems. I owe you that much, at least, given that you’ve presumably paid money for this book and are not owed explanations for the ways I’ve wasted your time, but are owed them for the ways I’m about to.
