The piss poems are what I called them, privately, for about a year and a half before I made the mistake of mentioning them in an interview, and what I have called them publicly ever since, mainly because the interviewer laughed in my face, and I am incapable of walking back a bit once it’s gotten a laugh, which is one of the seventeen character flaws this book is ostensibly about. They are poems I wrote after I had a piss in the middle of the night.

Yeah, that’s it, that’s the whole concept. You go to the toilet at two or three in the morning. You stand there. You wash your hands, if you’re being honest with yourself about who you want to be. Then you catch your face in the mirror. And something happens in the gap between those two things… that feels, in the moment, like it has to be written down. I don’t know what that something is. I have had a therapist for four years and we have discussed the piss poems on at least six occasions and her best current hypothesis is that the combination of sleep disruption, reduced cortisol, and the specific quality of late-night bathroom light creates a temporary lowering of what she called “the protective narration” — that is, the story you tell yourself about yourself during the day. Which I found both illuminating and slightly devastating, because it implies that all my best self-knowledge happens in a state of mild sleep deprivation while holding, um, that, and I have to live with that. Weird.

· · ·

The first one happened in the flat I was renting in East London in November of 2021. I’d come back from a shoot — I won’t say which one, not because it’s a secret, but because the contractual process of saying which one is a bureaucratic exercise I’d rather not perform in the middle of what I’m trying to say —, and I was so particularly tired I might have just been exhausted. I was too tired to sleep, yet too depleted to be awake and the only thing I was actually capable of was feeling things at a volume that I would have not have chosen. So I went to the toilet at half two and stood there for what was probably thirty seconds and felt, with a clarity that I found both bracing and inconvenient, that I was profoundly unhappy. Profoundly, persistently, and quite specifically unhappy, as I had felt for years. It was if I had been swimming in my own vomit and had accepted this as the ambient condition of my life; it was not something worth addressing.

And I went back to bed and wrote this on my phone:

i. November, E8
The mirror shows me a version of myself I do not remember commissioning. This is the face, apparently. This is what happened to the other one. I go back to bed. The bed is the same. I am less sure about me. 02:34 · Nov 9, 2021

It’s not very good. I want to say that upfront, about all of them: they’re not very good. They suck. I am not a poet. I’m an actor, which means I’m better at being inside other people’s feelings (and congenitally unreliable about my own), and the piss poems are the written record of what happens when those two things collide at three in the morning and there’s no script to hide behind. They are, as a body of work, approximately as artistically distinguished as you’d expect from someone who chose the word “congenitally” two sentences ago because it sounded more self-aware than “naturally.” I would blame my ghostwriters for that, but they are, however, mine, which is more than I can say for most of the words I said in public during this period.

Some of them are quite short. Some of them are longer than they should be, which is also true of this chapter. And some of them have what I think of as attempts at humor… some little swerves at the end that were supposed to land as rueful but landed, I suspect, as desperate; I include them all. I promised myself, when I decided to include this chapter, that I would not curate myself into being more interesting than I was. I was, during the eighteen months these poems cover, not very interesting. I was sad and I was tired and I was, on several occasions, in love with someone I had no ability to tell about it, and the piss poems are what came out of all three of those conditions between the hours of midnight and five, in various bathrooms, over a period that I can now look back on with at least some sort of affectionate condescension. I would very much prefer it not to happen again.

· · ·

There’s a particular quality to bathroom light at three in the morning that I became obsessed with and tried to describe probably a dozen times across these poems without ever getting it right. It’s too bright! And yet it does something to the face that daylight doesn’t, something reductive, and I developed a theory — which I am aware is not an original theory —, that this is why people cry in bathrooms at parties. Not because they’ve gone there to cry, but because the light tells them something they’d been successfully avoiding, and the privacy of it means there’s no one to help you look away.

Here is one from January, which was not a good month:

viii. January, W1
I would like, at some point, to love someone in the morning. Not the way I do it now, so quietly, in bathrooms, at hours that don’t count. I would like to love someone in the full light of day and have them know about it. I think that would be quite nice, actually. If anyone’s asking. 03:11 · Jan 14, 2022

The “if anyone’s asking” was the swerve I mentioned, uh. I don’t know if it works. At the time I was pleased with it, though I haven’t been very satisfied with it since.

My friend Harry,1 who read an early version of this chapter, said it made him want to hug me and ask me if I was okay. I told him I was fine, but he said “the piss poems say otherwise.” I said they were from a year ago, which they were! And he said “yeah but you’re including them in a book, which is the least-fine thing I’ve ever heard.” I don’t have a rebuttal to that, so I’m including them anyway.

· · ·

There is one that I nearly didn’t include. I wrote it in February during a week I’d rather not describe in detail — the details are not mine alone to own —, and it’s longer than the others and doesn’t have the tonal inconsistency that I think of as the piss poems’ distinguishing feature, that is, the quality of starting in one register and ending in a slightly different one, like a note played flat. This one is just flat all the way through, which makes it either more honest or more self-indulgent, and I’ve never been able to decide which. I include it because leaving it out felt like the kind of editorial decision I’m trying not to make about myself anymore.

xiv. February, SW3
The thing about the vomit is it’s usually warm. You get used to it. You mistake it for the sea. I have been swimming in the same direction for a long time now and I cannot tell if I am getting anywhere or just getting good at the motion of getting nowhere. The tiles are cold. And the light says what it always says. My face in the mirror is doing its best which is, tonight, not saying much. 04:02 · Feb 23, 2022

I went back to bed after that one and didn’t sleep. I lay there until five-thirty when the light changed, and made the ceiling look briefly like it was somewhere else. Then it went back to being the ceiling. So I got up and made coffee and thought about calling someone, but didn’t, which is the piss poem I didn’t write, and probably the one that would have benefitted most from being written.

The last one in this collection is from April of 2022, and it’s the only one that ends well, or at least less badly, which in context I’m counting as well:

xxii. April, E8 again
I have been thinking about the word temporary. How it means: this will end. How I keep forgetting that. How I keep needing to be reminded in bathrooms at four in the morning by a face that knows things I haven’t caught up to yet. I wash my hands. I go back to bed. Still here. 03:58 · Apr 7, 2022

I stopped writing them after that, more or less. Not because anything resolved — I want to be clear about that, because I’m tired of memoirs that imply resolution where there wasn’t any — but because I started telling people things in the daytime, which is the more socially acceptable version of the same activity, and it turned out to accomplish some of the same things. Sure enough, my face in the mirror is still doing that face, but I’ve just started talking back to it at better hours.

The rest of this chapter, for the record, is going to be about something else entirely. I promise it will be at least slightly less like this.

It will not be completely unlike this.

1 Not his real name. His real name is Harry. He asked me to use his real name. I told him using his real name would feel like outing him as someone who cared about me, and he said that was fine, and I said it wasn’t fine with me, and we agreed on this compromise, which is to use his real name but pretend we haven’t. Hi, Harry.
112 Have A Little Mercy
`, song: { title: "The Long and Winding Road", artist: "George Michael", cover: "https://i.scdn.co/image/ab67616d0000b27355d2e6eb533fab5ebe01ecf9", url: "2PQjbnfJHk4" } }, { id: "finn", condition: (ev, op, leftThirdEV, rightThirdEV, right2ThirdEV) => (rightThirdEV > 269), image: "https://i.pinimg.com/1200x/88/b0/fb/88b0fb40fb6cd6ec44de6d056561ab43.jpg", html: `

the memory of direction

[wind noise, camera adjusting, then sound of something being set down on a hard surface. The subject’s breath is visible in the cold air.]

Okay. Okay, it’s — I don’t know what day it is. November something. I stopped counting after the whole thing with the bridge. I’m somewhere north of Squamish, I’m sure. I haven’t seen another person in three weeks. Which is uh, good, honestly. After what happened on the Sea-to-Sky highway in the first month… no, yeah, no. Seeing people is not my goal anymore.

Food update, because I know future-me loves a food update. I ate plain rice tonight. Pretty good, honestly. I mean, plain rice after two weeks of Triscuits and lentils is… oh God, it’s good in a way it never was before. Weird to think about it this way. I also found some mushrooms yesterday, and I’m like, ninety percent sure they were chanterelles? I ate them. I’m still here. So either I was right, or I got lucky. Which, at this point, is basically the same thing.

I’ve been trying to remember what I was doing before. Like, what I had planned. Wait, y’know, I had this script, it was something new. Well… uh-duh, but I mean, it was a sci-fi thing. Very A24, very grounded. It was about a total societal collapse, ironically. Look where we ended up now…! But in my script, people banded together and built a localized utopian farm commune. And in reality… I saw a guy in North Van trade a Rolex for a half-empty jar of peanut butter, and then get… stabbed for the peanut butter anyway. So… my script had some pacing issues, I guess.

[subject shifts, and something rustles for a bit. He looks off-camera for a long moment, then returns.]

Let me-—let me try to go through the timeline again. Because I’ve been piecing it together. Kaliningrad was first, or it was the one that started the clock, I think. And then the Führers from the east decided that the best response was a localized EMP over the seaboard, which — surprise, surprise — accidentally cascaded and bricked the entire North American grid. Brilliant fucking move, by the way. I know “exchange” is a weird word for what happened next.

You expect the end of the world to be incredibly loud. Sort of… but right now it’s just birds. And the wind, too, it’s actually a pretty great soundscape. I’d use it in a mix if I had an editing bay.

Reminds me of— uh, there’s this game, I think it was called Ecstatica. I played it when I was— I don’t know how old I was, too young probably, someone had it on their computer and I watched them play it. And it had this quality where, uh, the village looked okay at first. Like, slightly unsettling, slightly off, but okay. And then you went a little further and it just got— wow, it got wrong in ways that built very slowly and you couldn’t really identify the exact moment things stopped being fine. That’s what this feels like. The moment things stopped being fine was- was not the moment things stopped looking fine. Those were two different moments and I keep trying to figure out which came first and I can’t.

I miss home. You don’t realize how much of a luxury it is to just be mildly annoyed at the things there instead of… whatever this is. But I’m okay. Uh, not good. Not good. But okay.

But uh… that’s not the weirdest feeling I’ve had so far. There was something last night….

[long pause. The wind picks up significantly, then dies back down to near silence.]

I thought I heard someone call my name. From the treeline, I think, so just past the perimeter of my camp. It was dark, pitch black, and it sounded… it sounded exactly like my bandmate Malcolm. But Malcolm was in Los Angeles when the airspace closed. He’s like three thousand miles away.

It said, “Finn, I’m so cold.”

So. I’m not going to check. I’m not going out there, I’m not going to. I’m just going to sit here with my crowbar, and I’m going to eat my food, and I’m gonna focus on happier things. Yeah, happier things. Yeah…

[a shaky exhale. His hands briefly come into frame, trembling hard enough that the image judders.]

I didn’t sleep. I don’t think I slept. I must’ve, but it— it doesn’t feel like it counted. It felt like— like being held under something. Like a weight or whatever. And, uh…

Well, I mean, when I looked at Malcolm then, it was really him. It wasn’t like in dreams where people are kind of… approximations. It was him, it was. Like I could’ve reached out and—

[he stops himself. Looks off-camera again, but this time doesn’t come back right away.]

He was just standing there, a-and he kept looking at his hands. Over and over again, like he couldn’t— like he couldn’t understand what he was seeing. And I asked him what was wrong, and he said—

[his voice drops, quieter now, like he’s afraid of being overheard.]

He said, “There’s blood in my hands.” And I— I laughed. I think I laughed. Because it didn’t make sense. Why? There wasn’t anything there. They were just— his hands, you know? Same as always. And I told him that, I said, “No, there’s not, you’re fine,” and—

[he inhales sharply.]

He looked at me like I was lying. I wasn’t, I really wasn’t, but he just— he seemed certain that I was lying to him. And then he said, “You just can’t see it yet.”

[Finn’s hands rise into frame again, though slower this time. He turns them over, palms up, studying them like he expects something to surface if he stares long enough.]

And then I looked down.

[the camera struggles to refocus slightly as his hands move closer.]

And they were— they were covered in blood. But like— not splattered, not… not like something you could wash off. It was… it was in the lines, in the skin, in my veins, as if… as if it had always been there and I’d just— just missed it somehow.

[he begins to shake more visibly now. The camera rattles against whatever it’s propped on.]

And I remember thinking, in the dream, I remember thinking—

Oh. Oh, this is it. There’s no— there’s no way to make it go away. There’s no version of this where I could wake up and boom, it’s gone, because it’s not— it’s not something that happened, it’s something that is.

[he lets out a small, broken laugh that doesn’t quite form.]

It felt—

[he struggles to find the word, eyes unfocused now.]

Pure…? T-that’s the worst part. But it felt… felt like it made sense in a way everything else doesn’t anymore. Like to me, it was the only honest thing left.

[his breathing grows uneven, shallow. He keeps staring at his hands.]

And I knew— I knew there wasn’t any… there wasn’t any—

[he trails off. His jaw tightens, like he’s refusing to say the word out loud.]

Yeah.

[a long silence. The wind fades almost completely, leaving only the faint sound of his breath and a distant, indistinct rustling.]

I woke up, and—

[he looks down again, faster this time, almost panicked.]

They were still—

[he abruptly drags his thumb across his palm, hard, like he’s trying to smear something away. Then again, then again, then again.]

No. No, no, no, no—

[Finn brings both hands close to his face, turning them over, then back, then over again, scanning every line and crease. His breathing is audible now, ragged at the edges.]

It’s— it’s fine, it’s just— it’s just my head, it’s—

[he presses his fingers to his mouth. Licks them— it’s quick, as if he’s testing it. He then freezes, and does it again, slower, moving to the heel of his palm. His expression doesn’t resolve into anything clean. He pulls his sleeve back and drags his tongue across the inside of his wrist, right along the vein, and goes very still.]

[his eyes are wet now. He doesn’t blink.]

I can still— I can still—

[he makes a sound that isn’t quite a word. He grabs his own forearm with both hands and brings it up close, squinting at it, the skin going white under his grip. He checks the other arm… the back of his hand… between his fingers. His breath is coming in short, high pulls now, and it doesn’t seem like he’s actually getting air in.]

Where— where is it, where did it—

[he scrubs both palms hard against his thighs, stands half-up out of frame for a moment, then drops back down. His hands go to his face: first his cheeks, then jaw, then the corner of his mouth; checking, pressing, smearing nothing away from nothing. His eyes are glassy and too-bright, and his jaw is shaking now.]

It’s there, I can— I know it’s there, I know what I—

[he stops. He sits very still for a long moment, hands in his lap, staring at them. Something in him doesn’t quite come back.]

I can’t do this shit again.

`, song: { title: "It Was A Very Good Year", artist: "Robbie Williams, Frank Sinatra", cover: "https://is1-ssl.mzstatic.com/image/thumb/Music123/v4/7e/cc/71/7ecc7100-e9d9-3ff7-d8a6-452cbc7b892c/13UMGIM19717.rgb.jpg/486x486bb.png", url: "dV83ph7K9po" } }, { id: "blur", condition: (ev, op, leftThirdEV, rightThirdEV, right2ThirdEV) => (right2ThirdEV > 269), image: "https://file.garden/aNtAfG887DiA_7lO/TML_2000/blur.png", html: `

Common People
BBC Two  ·  Sitcom  ·  Series 3  ·  2002
Channel: BBC Two Transmission: Fridays, 9:30pm Episodes: 6 (+ 1 unaired) Stars: Blur Creator: Ben Elton

Series 3 Episode Guide View all series →
Ep. Title Synopsis Air Date Rating
3×01 The Boiler The boiler is broken again. Damon insists he can fix it, but he cannot fix it. Dave drums on the boiler as Graham reads the manual incorrectly on purpose. Noticeably, Alex has somehow already fixed his side of the house without telling anyone. Jan 11, 2002 ★★★
7.1
3×02 The Dinner Party Damon invites Justine over for dinner, but Alex has also invited Justine. Graham has forgotten Justine was coming and has made a different dinner for someone else entirely, which nobody asks about. Dave is audible from upstairs for the entire episode. Jan 18, 2002 ★★★
7.3
3×03 The Jumper Graham has lost his jumper. The question of who borrowed it and whether it was borrowed or stolen occupies the entire episode. The jumper is eventually found on Alex, who is wearing it inside-out and had no memory of putting it on. The episode ends with Graham wearing it expressionlessly. Jan 25, 2002 ★★★½
7.8
3×04 The Council Tax Nobody will pay the council tax. Damon has a complex theory about whose responsibility it is that goes on for half the episode length. Alex claims to have already paid it. Turns out Dave has paid it, but paid last year's by accident, and it turns out last year's was never paid either. Graham does not speak in this episode, he just stares at people. Feb 1, 2002 ★★★★
8.4
3×05 The Ex-Girlfriend An ex-girlfriend of one of them turns up; it is unclear whose. Curiously, all four claim some ownership of the situation. She has come to collect a lamp. She leaves with the lamp; the lamp was not hers. Feb 8, 2002 ★★★
7.0
3×06 THE SERIES FINALE
"Ah, F... This"		 A water leak, a misplaced fax from their manager, and an inexplicably large delivery of artisan cheese push the household to its limit. Midway through act two, during a scene in which Damon attempts to explain to a plumber that the band's record royalties technically make them all joint homeowners and therefore the plumber should negotiate with all four of them simultaneously, Graham puts down his mug, says "Ah, fuck this," and walks out of the front door. He does not return. The live audience response is, by all accounts, the longest sustained applause in BBC Two history. The episode ends with Damon, Alex and Dave standing in silence in the kitchen, looking at where Graham was, as Dave begins, slowly, to drum on the counter. Feb 15, 2002 ★★★★★
9.6
Series 3 Review Posted by DigitalSpy Staff · Feb 15, 2002
Laugh Track Analysis
Series 3 average · per episode
Episode 1
68%
Episode 2
74%
Episode 3
80%
Episode 4
85%
Episode 5
72%
Episode 6
100%
* ep. 6 figure includes 11-second ovation event. BBC Records Office notified.

It has become fashionable, in certain quarters, to treat Common People as a curiosity: that is, as a Britpop-era novelty that somehow survived the death of Britpop by being, against all reasonable expectation, funnier than most of the actual sitcoms on television. The thing is, Series Three does nothing to discourage this view and a great deal to confirm it.

The engine of the thing remains, as ever, the four-way dynamic between its leads. Damon Albarn plays Damon as a man in the grip of a continuous and self-renewing confidence, one that has never once made contact with evidence. Alex James plays Alex, as someone for whom charm is, to be fair, a kind of passive climate. Dave Rowntree plays Dave as though acting is, well, a thing he is just doing with his body while his mind is elsewhere, as unusual as it occasionally seems. And Graham Coxon plays Graham, as a person who has been watching the other three for two and a half series and has arrived at a conclusion.

This conclusion arrives in episode six. The scene, in which Graham infamously puts down his mug and says, "Ah, fuck this" and walks out, had been, according to the Radio Times, written into the script three series ago as a placeholder joke that was never meant to air. (Ben Elton has declined to explain why it was kept.) Our review of the scene is that it is one of the most unusual things BBC Two has broadcast this year, and that the audience's response, which was about eleven seconds of cheering that the laugh track barely contains, constitutes a genuine cultural event. Everyone, on some level, recognised something.

The final image, of Dave drumming on the counter in the empty kitchen, will stay with this reviewer for some time.

Review by Mark Newby, DigitalSpy TV Team Overall Series 3 Rating: 8.1 / 10
Audience Reactions
"The moment Graham left I stood up in my living room. My housemates thought something had happened... and something had!!"
— WessexBlurFan, DigitalSpy Forums
"The council tax episode is the most accurately observed piece of television since I don't know when."
— FlatshareVeteran, DigitalSpy Forums
"I have been watching this programme since series one hoping Graham would do something and he has finally done something and I am at peace."
— GrahamWatcher99, DigitalSpy Forums · 312 upvotes

`, song: { title: "Strange News from Another Star", artist: "Blur", cover: "https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/aovas6mvuRwzzr5PD3dUFKLEFIG402lAnky4dHsTRI8J2sn9B9vnVCW1uuLm-VqzJ95yBSaC-RufXixoBw=w544-h544-l90-rj", url: "GMnZXcCjFio" } }, { id: "fightfightfight", condition: (ev) => isRecountElection(), image: "https://www.propublica.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/11/20201101-bush-v-gore-b-1x1.jpg", html: `

US Election 2000 front page
Florida recount: the full timeline Scalia recusal: a legal analysis The Supreme Court's 4-4 tie explained Emergency Presidential Determination Act: full text Don King: 'the most monumentally stupendous event in history' Bush v Gore: the physical matchup Vegas odds: Bush -140, Gore +115 — then it moved Roy Jones Jr to train Gore in Tennessee Tipper Gore barred from "striking" Bush camp members MGM Arena sold out in 11 minutes Floor seats reach $18,000 on secondary market Cheney confirmed 'non-participatory' attendee Clinton signs act with 'visible cheerfulness' Europe reacts: bewilderment from Berlin to Paris Bild: 'Haben die Amerikaner den Verstand verloren?' Bush: 'Clearing brush is basically training' What happens if the fight ends in a draw? The election that broke America
Gloves off: Bush and Gore to settle Florida dispute in pay-per-view title bout
After the Supreme Court deadlocks four-to-four following Justice Scalia's shock recusal, Congress passes the Emergency Presidential Determination Act; fight set for January 6 at the MGM Grand Garden Arena, with Don King as promoter

The 2000 US election crisis: Special Report
Andrew Pilkington in Washington
Friday December 15, 2000

The strangest presidential election in American history took yet another extraordinary turn last night when Al Gore and George W Bush formally agreed to settle the outcome of the Florida dispute in a sanctioned boxing match, after the Supreme Court deadlocked four votes to four following the sudden recusal of Justice Antonin Scalia.

Under the terms of the Emergency Presidential Determination Act, signed into law by President Clinton on Thursday afternoon in what observers noted was his most visibly cheerful signing ceremony in eight years, the two candidates will meet at the MGM Grand Garden Arena on January 6 — the same date on which Congress was due to certify the electoral college results.

The fight will be broadcast on pay-per-view by Fox Sports at $29.95 per household. Proceeds, after costs, will be split between the Federal Election Commission's administrative modernisation fund and the American Red Cross. Rupert Murdoch, the chairman of News Corporation, issued a statement in which he described himself as "proud to bring this historic moment to the American public". The broadcast rights were sold to Fox in a transaction that attorneys for both campaigns declined to describe in detail, citing ongoing negotiations.

Don King, the boxing promoter, described the bout as "the most monumentally stupendous and historically unprecedented event in the 224-year history of the American republic." He will receive a commission of 12%.

The Scalia question

The crisis began on Tuesday when Justice Scalia, who had been among the court's most vocal supporters of hearing the Bush campaign's challenge to the Florida recount, abruptly withdrew from the case after the Associated Press reported that his son, Eugene Scalia, was a partner at Gibson, Dunn & Crutcher — the law firm representing George W Bush before the court.

Legal scholars expressed surprise less at the conflict of interest, which had been noted in passing by several commentators in the preceding days, than at the decision to recuse at all. "It's unusual," said Laurence Tribe of Harvard Law School, with considerable understatement.

Without Scalia, the court divided four to four. Justices Stevens, Souter, Ginsburg and Breyer voted to allow the recount to continue; Justices Rehnquist, O'Connor, Kennedy and Thomas voted to halt it. A tied Supreme Court produces no binding precedent and no resolution. The Florida Supreme Court's order to continue counting stood, and chaos ensued.

By Wednesday evening, with no constitutional mechanism available to resolve the dispute before the January 20 inauguration date, congressional leaders from both parties convened in emergency session. Senator Trent Lott of Mississippi emerged at 2am to announce that a "frankly unprecedented but entirely workable" bipartisan solution had been reached.

The legislation

The Emergency Presidential Determination Act passed the Senate 97-3 and the House 418-11. It designates a sanctioned athletic contest between the two principal-party nominees as a legally binding method of resolving an electoral deadlock for which no other constitutional remedy exists.

The three senators voting against — Wellstone, Feingold and McCain — objected on different grounds. Senator McCain, reached by telephone, said he found the legislation "insane," then paused for an extended moment, and then said he would "probably watch anyway."

The rules of the bout, as set out in the Act's 340-page annex, are broadly standard boxing regulations with several amendments. The match will be ten rounds of three minutes each. Both candidates will be permitted a trainer and a cut man. Mr Cheney, as Bush's running mate, may attend "in a non-participatory capacity," as will Mr Lieberman as Gore's running mate. Tipper Gore will be permitted at ringside but may not, under subsection 14(b), "strike or otherwise physically engage" any member of the Bush campaign. This provision was reportedly requested by Karen Hughes.

Reaction

Mr Bush, 54, reached at his ranch in Crawford, Texas, expressed confidence. "I'm feeling great," he said, jogging lightly on the spot for the assembled press pool. "I've been clearing brush all week. That's basically training." When a reporter asked whether he had spoken to Don King, Mr Bush confirmed he had and called King "a fantastic guy, very smart, really just a great supporter of democracy."

Mr Gore, 52, convened a press conference at his official residence and struck a more methodical tone. He announced that he had retained the services of former light-heavyweight champion Roy Jones Jr as his personal trainer, and that he expected to be "fully prepared on every dimension of this contest." Asked whether he was concerned about the physical requirements of the match, Mr Gore removed his jacket, rolled up his shirt sleeve, and flexed, causing the press pool to be briefly silent.

President Clinton, signing the Act in the Rose Garden, said he was "very proud of the bipartisan spirit that produced this legislation" and had every confidence the American people would "determine the right outcome." He then appeared to notice the cameras, rearranged his expression into one of greater solemnity, and returned to the White House without taking further questions.

A challenge was filed overnight by the American Civil Liberties Union, which argued that the act violated the 14th amendment's equal protection clause on the grounds that it discriminated against candidates who were, among other things, elderly, physically disabled, or fundamentally disinclined towards violence. The ACLU conceded in its filing that neither of the present candidates fell into any of these categories, but maintained the principle was sound.

International reaction

A spokesman for Tony Blair said the prime minister had "taken note" of developments and looked forward to working constructively with "whoever emerges." The French foreign minister, Hubert Védrine, told RTL radio that the situation confirmed France's "longstanding concerns about the structural peculiarities of American democratic process," then declined to elaborate. The German tabloid Bild ran the headline: Haben die Amerikaner den Verstand verloren?, and the German chancellor, Gerhard Schroeder, issued a communiqué in which he described the contest as "without precedent in any democratic state" and said he hoped the American people would "find a pathway back to constitutional normality" following the result. The Vatican said it was praying for a peaceful resolution and did not indicate a preferred candidate, while in Moscow, the Kremlin issued no official comment.

The White House said President Clinton would not be attending the contest in person. He issued a statement through his press secretary saying he wished both men "the very best" and that he had "every confidence the American democratic system will endure this as it has endured everything else". Mrs Clinton, reached at her transition office in Washington, declined to comment. A spokesperson said the senator-elect was "focused on the work of the people of New York".

Speaker Dennis Hastert, asked by reporters on the steps of the Capitol whether he had any reservations about the legislation he had helped pass at three in the morning, paused for what witnesses described as a considerable length of time before saying: "We did what we had to do." He then went inside.

Las Vegas opened the odds at Bush -140, Gore +115, reflecting the governor's slight advantage in upper body mass. By Thursday afternoon, following confirmation of Gore's Roy Jones Jr connection, the line had shifted to Bush -105, Gore -115.

Tickets for the MGM Grand Garden Arena in Las Vegas, which holds over 17,000, opened at $1,500 and surged within minutes. The event sold out in under 9 minutes. Ringside seats were trading on the secondary market for upwards of $22,000 by Thursday night, and Former President George H W Bush reportedly purchased two tickets himself.

The fight is scheduled for January 6, with the winner to be inaugurated on January 20.

Don King said: "This is beautiful. This is America."


 
 
 
Advertisement
Advertisement
Guardian Unlimited © Guardian Newspapers Limited 2000
` }, { id: "biden", condition: (ev) => itsATie, image: "https://whyy.org/wp-content/uploads/2017/08a/apjoebidenx1200.jpg", html: `
↩ Reply
↪ Reply All
↪ Forward
🗑 Delete
⬇ Archive
🏷 Tag
From
Joe Biden <jrb@biden2012.com>
To
DNC Leadership, Surrogates, Interested Parties, National Press Corps
Date
August 14, 2012, 8:42 AM EDT
Re: The State of the Race

Folks, let me say this as clearly and simply as I can: Fuck You.

I'm the Democratic Party's nominee. No one is pushing me out. I'm not leaving.

I woke up this morning to read front-page stories in the Times and Politico full of anonymous quotes from "senior party officials" and "concerned donors" saying I need to step aside for Janet, or hand the keys over to some fresh-faced kid from the Senate, because my poll numbers in Ohio "dipped" three points. I'm watching cable news anchors openly pontificate about a brokered convention in Charlotte.

It's absolute malarkey.

When I took this job in 2008, the economy was in freefall. We were bleeding 800,000 jobs a month. We were drowning in two unwinnable wars. I dragged us out of the worst financial crisis since the Great Depression. I gave the order to take out Osama bin Laden. I passed healthcare reform when everyone in Washington, including half the people on this email chain, told me it was political suicide. And now, everyone acts like they loved Bidencare from the start, when half of them were sabotaging me.

Now, things get a little tough against Romney and his billionaire buddies, and your first instinct is to run for the hills? To stab the guy who got you here in the back?

Janet is a hell of a Vice President. God love her, she's doing an incredible job securing the homeland and helping me govern. But she is my Vice President. She's on my ticket. And as for the "rising stars" you're all whispering about at your Georgetown cocktail parties? They can wait their damn turn.

I am the only person who has beaten these guys, and I am the only person who is going to beat them in November. If you want to spend the next three months wetting the bed, do it in your own homes. But stop leaking to the press, stop the cowardly backroom plotting, and start doing the work to win this election.

Get behind me or get the fuck out of my way.

Joe

Joseph R. Biden Jr.
President of the United States
Paid for by Biden-Napolitano 2012
✅ This message appears to be legitimate. 1 attachment — 24 KB
` }, { id: "trump", condition: (ev) => charlieWin && ev < 269 && support_jocks >= 3 && anti_establishment >= 3, image: "https://d7hftxdivxxvm.cloudfront.net/?height=800&quality=85&resize_to=fit&src=https%3A%2F%2Fd32dm0rphc51dk.cloudfront.net%2FsYasG-Avf3SfG9jALDvSlw%2Fnormalized.jpg&width=658", html: `

TRUMP'S DEBATE SHOCKER: 'APPRENTICE' STAR BULLIES BUSH, PUSHES UNIVERSAL HEALTHCARE

Friday, October 8, 2004
By Fox News Channel Staff
"I'm looking at George, and frankly, I feel bad for him. Very low energy. And Dick Cheney, who is hiding in a bunker somewhere — he's a loser, folks."

ST. LOUIS — Pundits and voters alike were left speechless Friday night after what was arguably the most bizarre, chaotic, and downright hostile presidential debate in American history.

Democratic nominee Donald J. Trump, the billionaire real estate mogul and star of NBC's "The Apprentice," completely abandoned traditional political decorum during the town-hall style debate at Washington University. Instead of offering wonky policy details or parrying on foreign policy, as most nominees before him did, Trump spent the 90-minute broadcast mercilessly bullying President George W. Bush and Vice President Dick Cheney.

Bush, who appeared visibly frustrated and occasionally bewildered, repeatedly tried to steer the conversation toward his record on tax cuts and the War on Terror. But Trump interrupted the incumbent at every turn, pacing around the stage, invading Bush's personal space, and labeling the sitting administration as "low-energy losers."

In perhaps the most shocking policy moment of the night, Trump vigorously defended a sweeping, government-funded universal healthcare system— a traditionally left-wing policy that he pitched using his trademark brash, populist rhetoric, alienating conservatives while leaving moderate Democrats scratching their heads.

Below is an excerpt from the official debate transcript, provided by the Commission on Presidential Debates:

MODERATOR CHARLES GIBSON: Mr. Trump, I want to return to the issue of the rising cost of healthcare. President Bush has proposed tax credits and health savings accounts, and you have proposed a system that sounds very similar to socialized medicine. How do you pay for it?

DONALD TRUMP: Charlie, first of all, look at George. Look at him. He's sweating, he's a mess. He's been President for four years and our country is falling apart. Health savings accounts? Give me a break. George, nobody knows what that is. It's garbage.

PRESIDENT GEORGE W. BUSH: Well, Charlie, let me— let me just say, my plan empowers the patient. My opponent wants the federal government to—

TRUMP: (interrupting) Nobody wants your plan, George. You don't even want your plan. You know who wants your plan? Dick Cheney's rich friends at Halliburton. And by the way, where is Dick? Is he in a bunker? He's always in a bunker. Either that or Tahiti, he's a low-energy guy. A total loser, so bad at business. I've seen his balance sheets, they're a disaster.

BUSH: Now wait a minute. The Vice President is a good man, and we are fighting a global war on—

TRUMP: (waving his hands) Quiet, piggy. Let me talk, I'm answering Charlie's question. We are going to take care of everybody. Okay? Universal healthcare, everybody gets covered, the government pays for it, boom. We're the richest country in the world, and we have people dying in the streets because they can't afford a band-aid. It's a disgrace, really.

GIBSON: But Mr. Trump, to follow up—

TRUMP: In Canada, in Scotland — where I have beautiful golf courses, by the way, you should go there someday —, they don't let you die. They take care of you. We are going to make a deal with the hospitals, a beautiful deal, perhaps the greatest deal ever made. Lil' Dubya and Mr. Cheney are just letting the drug companies rob you blind because they have zero negotiating skills. Zero. Look at him, he can barely string a sentence together. He can't even stand up for the country! I've seen disabled vets who could stand up more than him, really. And you're going to let this guy negotiate with Pfizer? I don't think so.

BUSH: (laughing nervously) Charlie, he wants to put bureaucrats between you and your doctor. It's... it's just not the American way.

TRUMP: (leaning into his microphone) "The American way." George, your American way is getting us ripped off by the Saudis while we spend trillions in the sandbox. We're going to use that money to fix our own country. And this November, I'm gonna let you know: you're fired. You're fired, George. It's over, get the hell out of here.

Following the debate, the Bush campaign released a blistering statement calling Trump's behavior "unhinged and un-presidential." Top Republican strategists appeared on Fox News immediately after the broadcast, warning that Trump's healthcare plan would bankrupt the nation.

Meanwhile, Democratic leadership remained remarkably quiet, seemingly unsure of how to handle their nominee's unorthodox, abrasive, yet undeniably commanding debate performance. Early flash polls taken by the Gallup organization suggest that despite the breach in decorum, Trump's performance resonated strongly with undecided blue-collar voters in key rust-belt states.

The third and final presidential debate is scheduled for October 13 in Tempe, Arizona.

U.S. DOLLAR SLIDES AS SUBPRIME LOSSES MOUNT AT CITIGROUP, MERRILL LYNCH  ·  BRAUN SIGNALS FURTHER FED EMERGENCY MEASURES UNDER REVIEW  ·  SHARPTON ARRIVES PARIS FOR BAYROU SUMMIT; BRIEF BELGRADE STOPOVER DRAWS CRITICISM  ·  OIL ABOVE $94 A BARREL; OPEC MEETS THURSDAY
Thursday, November 15, 2007 14:32 EST English | Français | Deutsch | العربية
World / Americas / White House

Sharpton tours Europe amid mounting criticism over subprime response, Belgrade stopover

President spends week with Bayrou, Jackson, Olejniczak and controversially Milosevic, as VP Braun steers crisis talks in Washington
By Carolyn Ahern and Marcus Webb PARIS/LONDON | 4 min read Updated: Nov. 15, 2007, 2:32 PM EST
Email Print Share

PARIS (Reuters) — President Al Sharpton concluded a five-day European swing on Thursday, meeting with British Prime Minister Glenda Jackson at Downing Street after stops in Paris, Warsaw and a brief, sharply criticised visit to Belgrade that dominated press coverage on both sides of the Atlantic, while a widening crisis in U.S. mortgage markets continued to fall almost entirely to Vice President Carol Moseley Braun to manage from Washington.

The tour, billed by the White House as a "renewed Atlantic dialogue" ahead of next month's G8 preparatory sessions, took Sharpton to French President François Bayrou for a working dinner in Paris on Tuesday, to Polish opposition leader Waldemar Olejniczak in Warsaw on Wednesday morning, and then, in what aides insisted was a scheduled forty-minute courtesy call, to Belgrade, where he shook hands with Yugoslav President Slobodan Milosevic before flying north to London.

The Milosevic meeting, the first between a sitting U.S. president and the Serbian leader since his return to power following the collapse of his Hague proceedings in 2005, drew immediate condemnation from human rights groups and bipartisan rebuke in Congress.

"There is no diplomatic rationale for that handshake that is more important than the signal it sends to survivors of Srebrenica," said Senator John McCain of Arizona in a statement released Wednesday evening. Representative Nancy Pelosi called it "inexplicable" and said the House Foreign Affairs Committee would seek a briefing.

The White House pushed back. National Security Adviser Susan Rice told reporters aboard Air Force One that the meeting was "brief and in the U.S. interest," citing ongoing negotiations over Kosovo's status and the need to maintain a channel to Belgrade. She declined to say whether Sharpton had raised the question of outstanding Hague warrants.

"The President is focused on America's standing in the world. The Vice President is focused on the American economy. That's the division of labour, and it's working."
— White House press secretary Kevin Sulley

The London stop, by contrast, was described by both governments as substantive. Sharpton and Prime Minister Jackson, whose Labour government has faced its own turbulence over Northern Rock's emergency nationalisation, agreed to a joint working group on transatlantic mortgage market exposure, with results to be reported by February. Jackson, who had been openly sceptical of Sharpton's Keynesian stimulus proposals earlier in the year, was notably warmer at the podium than in previous encounters.

"We face common challenges," Jackson said. "And we have, I think, a genuine convergence on how to meet them." Asked whether she had raised concerns about the Belgrade visit, she said only that "we covered a full agenda" and moved on.

The Paris meeting with Bayrou had been the headline item on the tour's original schedule. The two leaders discussed NATO burden-sharing, climate negotiations, and, according to a French government readout, Iran, although the White House summary made no mention of Tehran. Bayrou, whose MoDem government has positioned itself as a bridge between Washington and a sceptical European centre-left, called the talks "frank and productive." There was no joint press conference.

In Warsaw, Sharpton met Olejniczak of the Democratic Left Alliance for approximately ninety minutes, a session that drew less coverage than it might have, given that it fell on the same news cycle as the Belgrade pictures. Olejniczak, whose party is positioning itself ahead of next year's parliamentary elections, described the conversation as covering "energy security, the role of NATO, and Poland's place in a changing Europe."

Throughout all of it, the dominant story back in Washington remained the accelerating deterioration of the U.S. housing market. Citigroup on Wednesday disclosed a further $8 billion to $11 billion in subprime-related writedowns. Merrill Lynch is expected to announce losses of a comparable scale next week, and consumer confidence figures released Thursday morning fell to their lowest since 2003.

It has fallen largely to Braun — who holds the economic portfolio as a formal Vice Presidential mandate, the broadest such arrangement since the Clinton/Gore years — to respond. On Tuesday she convened an emergency session with Treasury Secretary Roger Altman, Federal Reserve Chairman Janet Yellen and the heads of the major housing agencies. A White House statement said the meeting produced "a framework for coordinated intervention," though specifics were not disclosed, as markets responded with modest relief.

The optics of the divergence have not gone unnoticed. Several Democratic congressional leaders, speaking on condition of anonymity, expressed frustration that the President was photographed with Milosevic on the same day Braun was fielding calls from Citigroup's board.

"The Vice President is doing the work," one senior House Democrat said. "Everyone in this town knows it. The question is whether anyone outside this town knows it."

White House press secretary Kevin Sulley, when asked about the split-screen quality of the week, was unapologetic. "The President is focused on America's standing in the world," he said. "The Vice President is focused on the American economy. That's the division of labour, and it's working."

Sharpton returns to Washington on Friday. He is scheduled to address the Democratic Leadership Council on Monday.

Additional reporting by Philippe Girard in Paris, Anna Kowalski in Warsaw and Stephen Marsh in London. Editing by David Cutler.
Related Coverage

2010 New York gubernatorial election

2010 New York
gubernatorial election
November 2, 2010
← 2006    2014 →

Popular vote 3,168,442 / 2,271,809

Democratic
Contents
  1. Background
  2. Democratic primary
    1. Candidates
    2. Primary results
  3. Republican primary
  4. General election
    1. Campaign
    2. Results
  5. Aftermath
  6. See also

The 2010 New York gubernatorial election was held on November 2, 2010, as part of the broader 2010 United States midterm elections. Sean Combs, the Harlem-born music mogul and civic activist, won the Democratic nomination following a crowded and nationally watched primary, then defeated Republican nominee Carl Paladino in the general election by approximately 15 percentage points. Combs became the first elected African-American governor of New York and the first person without prior elected office to win a major-party gubernatorial nomination in the state's modern history.

The election took place against a difficult national environment for Democrats. The administration of President Hillary Clinton, which had taken office in January 2009, faced significant public discontent over the pace of economic recovery following the 2007–2009 financial crisis. Republicans gained 63 seats in the House of Representatives and six seats in the Senate on election night, recapturing majorities in both chambers.[1] New York's relative insulation from the wave, as Combs's margin was roughly half that of the party's 2006 gubernatorial win, was nonetheless seen as a sign of the Empire State's continued lean toward Democrats at the statewide level.

Background

Incumbent Governor David Paterson, who had assumed office in March 2008 following the resignation of Eliot Spitzer, announced in February 2010 that he would not seek a full term, citing persistently low approval ratings and ongoing investigations into his administration.[2] His withdrawal opened one of the most competitive Democratic primaries in New York in two decades.

Combs had been involved in civic and voter-registration efforts since the early 2000s, most notably through his Citizen Change campaign ("Vote or Die"), which was widely credited with boosting youth turnout for Paul Wellstone in the 2004 presidential election.[3] He had publicly considered a Senate run in 2006 but ultimately decided not to run. His entrance into the 2010 governor's race was announced in a September 2009 New York Times op-ed in which he outlined an economic platform centered on urban infrastructure investment, workforce development in the outer boroughs, and reform of the state's Rockefeller drug laws.[4]

Democratic primary

Candidates

The Democratic field was considered unusually crowded for a New York gubernatorial primary. Major declared candidates included:

Candidate Background Notable support
Sean Combs Music executive and entrepreneur; founder of Bad Boy Records; civic activist Rep. Charles Rangel, Rev. Al Sharpton, several Brooklyn party organizations
Andrew Cuomo Attorney General of New York (2007–present); former Secretary of Housing and Urban Development Much of the party establishment; labor unions
Richard Ravitch Lieutenant Governor (2009–2010); former MTA chair Good-government groups; editorial boards
Kathleen Rice Nassau County District Attorney Law enforcement associations; suburban moderates
Eric Schneiderman New York State Senate (1998–2010) Progressive activist groups; Working Families Party

Cuomo entered the race as the strong frontrunner, with name recognition and institutional backing that many observers believed would be decisive. However, his campaign was hampered in its early months by persistent questions about his tenure at HUD and his role in loosening mortgage-lending standards in the late 1990s, a line of attack Combs's campaign pressed aggressively in debates.[5] Combs, meanwhile, proved an unexpectedly disciplined debater and drew enthusiastic crowds in New York City, where turnout models consistently showed him running well ahead of projections.

Schneiderman, running on a more explicitly progressive platform, briefly surged in late May polling before consolidating support behind Combs in the campaign's final weeks, citing the "urgent need for a candidate who could energize the full Democratic coalition."[6] Cuomo, Ravitch, and Rice all remained in the race through primary day.

Primary results

Candidate Votes %
Sean Combs ✓ 481,203 33.8%
Andrew Cuomo 407,119 28.6%
Richard Ravitch 256,440 18.0%
Kathleen Rice 163,882 11.5%
Eric Schneiderman 106,774 7.5%
Others 8,991 0.6%
Total 1,424,409 100%

Combs carried all five New York City boroughs by wide margins and ran competitively in Westchester and Nassau counties. Cuomo led outside New York City but could not overcome Combs's metropolitan dominance. Cuomo conceded early the following morning and endorsed Combs, as did Schneiderman and Rice within the week. Ravitch withheld his endorsement for several days amid speculation about a third-party run before ultimately supporting the Democratic nominee.[7]

Republican primary

The Republican primary was won by Carl Paladino, a Buffalo-area real estate developer and Tea Party-aligned candidate, who defeated former congressman Rick Lazio with 62.3% of the vote. Paladino's campaign, which stressed fiscal austerity, opposition to the TARP bank bailouts, and sharp criticism of President Clinton's stimulus agenda, benefited from substantial Tea Party movement organizational support.[8]

General election

Campaign

The general election campaign was defined by two competing narratives. Paladino sought to tie Combs to the Clinton administration and to Albany's perceived dysfunction, arguing that a first-time candidate with no governmental experience was unfit to manage a state in fiscal crisis. His campaign repeatedly aired television spots juxtaposing Combs's hip-hop persona with images of budget shortfalls and empty manufacturing plants in upstate New York.

Combs countered by emphasizing job creation, positioning himself as an outsider untainted by Albany's failures. His campaign produced a widely-shared series of ads spotlighting South Bronx and Brownsville residents speaking directly to camera about economic need, credited by political analysts with boosting his favorability among Latino and working-class white voters in the outer boroughs.[9] He received the formal endorsements of the Working Families Party, the United Federation of Teachers, and 1199 SEIU, as well as a high-profile endorsement from President Clinton in October.[10]

The race tightened somewhat in late October, consistent with national trends showing Democratic candidates underperforming their summer polling averages. A Siena College poll released October 25 showed Combs ahead by 12 points, down from 21 points in August.[11]

Results

Candidate Party Votes %
Sean Combs ✓ Democratic / Working Families 3,168,442 54.8%
Carl Paladino Republican / Conservative / Tea Party 2,271,809 39.3%
Howie Hawkins Green 213,870 3.7%
Warren Redlich Libertarian 117,344 2.0%
Others / scattering — 11,204 0.2%
Total 5,782,669 100%

Combs carried New York City by a margin of approximately 78–17%, and won Westchester, Rockland, and Albany counties. Paladino swept most of upstate New York and performed strongly in Staten Island. The outcome was called by the Associated Press at 10:41 PM Eastern time.

Aftermath

Combs was inaugurated as the 57th Governor of New York on January 1, 2011. His transition team, co-chaired by former deputy mayor Dennis Walcott and venture capital executive Mellody Hobson, was described by Politico as "heavy on private-sector talent and light on Albany insiders, perhaps deliberately so."[12]

Nationally, the election results were parsed as a mixed signal. Democrats pointed to Combs's margin as evidence that the party could recruit non-traditional candidates capable of expanding the electorate, while Republicans argued that the 15-point result in a reliably blue state during a wave year illustrated the ceiling of such a strategy.[13] President Clinton, speaking at a post-election press conference, cited Combs's win as a "bright spot in an otherwise sobering evening" while acknowledging that the loss of Congressional majorities would require her administration to "govern differently" in its second two years.[14]

Andrew Cuomo subsequently announced in December 2010 his intention to seek the Democratic nomination for U.S. Senate in 2012. Eric Schneiderman was elected Attorney General in the same November 2010 cycle, a race that received considerably less national attention than the gubernatorial contest.

See also

  • 2010 United States midterm elections
  • Hillary Clinton administration
  • Citizen Change
  • List of governors of New York
  • 2010 New York State Senate election
References

[1] "Republicans Seize House Majority in Midterm Rebuke of Clinton" The New York Times, November 3, 2010.  ·  [2] "Paterson Opts Out, Clearing Path for 2010 Primary" New York Daily News, February 26, 2010.  ·  [3] Citizen Change press archives, 2004.  ·  [4] Combs, Sean. "Why I'm Running for Governor." The New York Times, September 14, 2009.  ·  [5] Transcript, NY Democratic Gubernatorial Debate, March 2010, WNBC.  ·  [6] "Schneiderman Endorses Combs, Cites Coalition Argument" Politico, August 3, 2010.  ·  [7] "Ravitch Backs Combs After Weighing Independent Bid" New York Post, September 21, 2010.  ·  [8] "Tea Party's Paladino Wins GOP Nod in Rout" Buffalo News, September 15, 2010.  ·  [9] "How Combs's outer-borough strategy held the margin" The Atlantic, November 12, 2010.  ·  [10] "Clinton endorses Combs, calls him 'future of the Democratic Party'" AP, October 19, 2010.  ·  [11] Siena College Research Institute, New York Governor Poll, October 25, 2010.  ·  [12] "Combs transition heavy on outsiders." Politico, November 20, 2010.  ·  [13] "Blue State, Narrow Win: What Combs's Margin Tells Us" FiveThirtyEight, November 4, 2010.  ·  [14] White House press briefing transcript, November 3, 2010.

the sky is yellow here, just so you know

by margeCentral
Rating:
General Audiences
Archive Warning:
No Archive Warnings Apply

Characters:
Homer Simpson Marge Simpson Bart Simpson Lisa Simpson Santa's Little Helper Kent Brockman Mr. Burns (mentioned, conspicuously)

Missing Scene Homer's Inner Monologue Springfield Nuclear Power Plant Slow Morning early seasons vibes impending doom no beta we die like rod and todd might honestly the news is never good Oh No Bro
Stats:
Published: 2004-11-03  |  Words: 1,604  |  Chapters: 1/1  |  Kudos: 341  |  Bookmarks: 67  |  Hits: 5,218
Author's Note

okay so i've been sitting on this one for a while and i kept thinking it wasn't done but then i read it again tonight and i think this is just what it is. it's a small thing. a slice of nothing, really. i promise!

i wanted to write Homer the way he was in like seasons 2-4, before he got flanderized into, uh, you know. we need a homer that's well-meaning and confused rather than actively stupid. in other words, a guy who genuinely doesn't understand a lot of things but isn't proud of it. there's a difference and i think it matters a lot actually

no beta. this is set vaguely in the early 90s. title from the depths of my puny brain. enjoy!

Summary

It is a Tuesday morning in Springfield. Homer Simpson wakes up, makes the most of it, and then watches the news.

Mr. Burns could not be reached for comment. Neither could Smithers. Nobody, in fact, could be reached for comment. This seems like it might be important.

The first thing Homer Simpson was aware of, on the morning of the last ordinary Tuesday he would have for some time, was that the ceiling had a new crack in it.

He lay on his back and looked at the crack. It ran from the light fixture to a point roughly above his left knee, which was sticking out from under the covers in a way that suggested his body had, during the night, made several decisions he had not been consulted on. Welp. The crack was new. Or maybe it was old and he had never noticed it? Either way, these were, for practical purposes, the same thing.

“Marge,” he said.

Marge’s side of the bed was empty, indicating she had been up for a while… which meant it was later than he thought… which meant he should probably be getting up. Homer processed all of this information with some deliberateness, as if he were trying to reassemble complex machinery in the dark, and realized both things would be a waste of his time.

He sat up and placed his feet on the floor. He looked at his feet; they were still his feet. So far so good!

Downstairs, something smelled like bacon, which was probably a good thing. It could’ve been either breakfast or a fire, though, and either way he needed to go find out.

The bacon, luckily, was breakfast. Marge was standing at the stove in her green dress, seemingly managing over three things at once and choosing not to remark on it. Lisa was reading something at the table. Bart was eating with his left hand, and doing something with his right hand that Homer decided, at seven-fourteen in the morning, not to investigate. Santa’s Little Helper was under the table. Homer could not see him, but assumed he was sleeping anyway. Though perhaps not for long… who could resist bacon?

“Morning, everyone,” Homer said.

“Morning, Dad,” said Lisa, without looking up.

“Hey,” said Bart.

Marge smiled at him over her shoulder. “There’s juice.”

Indeed, there was juice. Homer sat down, poured juice, drank some juice, and thought about nothing in particular for a moment. It was one of the things he was genuinely best at in the world. The juice was pineapple juice, too, which was very refreshing! Outside, a bird was making a noise. Probably one of those Springfield birds. Not that they were special, uh, nor that there were few of them, but um, they tended to sound like that?

Homer ate four pieces of bacon, and most of a piece of toast that had been buttered to an extent he considered, privately, slightly conservative. He thought about mentioning the butter situation, but ultimately decided against it. After all, he had been married long enough to know that some battles were not battles so much as they were announcements of intent, and that the appropriate response to an announcement of intent, at this time in the morning, was usually silence.

“Dad,” Lisa said, “did you know the average American consumes enough nitrate-treated meat products in a year to —”

“Mmm,” said Homer, in a tone he had developed specifically for this situation. “Meat…”

Lisa looked at him for a moment. She appeared to be deciding something, but she went back to her book.

Bart was the first to leave, followed shortly by Lisa with her saxophone case. The last one to leave was Marge, who kissed Homer on the cheek on her way to the laundry and said something about dinner that he only caught approximately the first and last word of, the middle having passed through him like light through a window. He nodded at a frequency he judged appropriate.

It seems he had forty minutes before he needed to leave for the plant. This was, in Homer’s view, a gift from the universe, and he intended to honor it in the traditional fashion: he went to the couch, arranged himself, found the remote (which was behind the left cushion where it always was) and, eventually, he turned on the television.

The television gave him Channel 4, which was showing a commercial for a mattress company featuring a man in a suit, sitting on a mattress. He had a funny expression; perhaps he learned not to underestimate mattresses… Homer watched this with mild interest. He had some opinions about mattresses, and had been meaning to form them into a more coherent position for several years now.

Then the commercial ended, and Kent Brockman’s face filled the screen.

Kent Brockman always looked the same. Square jaw, excellent hair, the slightly glazed confidence of a man who had been telling people things on television for, well, long enough that he had forgotten how to have an expression that wasn’t for cameras… but there was a specific version of Kent Brockman that appeared when the news was a bit different, and required a different graphic behind him. The thing is that version had appeared now: the graphic behind him was blood red.

A little unnerved, Homer sat up a little.

“—developing situation,” Kent was saying, “at the Springfield Nuclear Power Plant, where emergency crews were dispatched in the early morning hours following what officials are describing as, and I want to read this carefully, a” — he paused — “’significant radiological containment event affecting sectors seven-G through twelve-C.’ Now, to be clear, and we want to be very clear here at Newschannel 6, officials are emphasizing that Springfield residents in the immediate vicinity of the plant should not be alarmed.”

Homer stared at the screen. “Sector seven-G,” he said, to no one. “Hmm…”

Brockman straightened his papers. “We reached out this morning to Springfield Nuclear Power Plant management for comment. A representative for the plant said that—” he paused again, briefly, — “no statement would be forthcoming at this time. Attempts to reach plant owner C. Montgomery Burns were unsuccessful, and so were attempts to reach executive assistant Waylon Smithers. In a… related development, attempts to reach anyone at the plant above the level of, ah—” he glanced sideways at something off-camera, “cooling tower maintenance were, similarly, unsuccessful.”

Behind him, a live helicopter shot appeared, split-screen: he could see the plant’s towers, and something that was coming out of them that was not, as far as Homer could determine, the thing that was usually coming out of them. This was bad.

“Again,” Kent said, “there is no cause for alarm.”

Homer looked at the television for a long moment. He didn’t seem to believe him for a moment. Then he looked at his hands. Well, his hands looked fine, he knew that well. They were the same hands they had always been, slightly yellow-ish/pink, no notable changes. Hmm. This seemed like it should be more comforting than it was.

He got up and walked to the living room window, and pulled the curtain aside. Outside, there was nothing particularly unusual of notice. He could see that prick Ned Flanders’s sprinklers were running to his right. He could also spot a kid on a bicycle (that was not Bart) going past. The sky was its regular yellow, too. Things seemed fine.

Homer looked at the sky for a while. He then looked back at the television, where this time, Brockman had moved on to a story about a dog that had learned to do something, probably something exciting, and the anchor was smiling, and everything about the segment said: see, here is a thing that is fine.

And so Homer looked at the sky again.

First, he thought about Sector 7-G. He thought about his chair, specifically: the one he sat in, the one with the good armrests, and the one that was arguably the best chair in Sector 7-G and possibly in the greater Springfield area. He then thought about the donut he had been planning to eat at approximately ten forty-five, which was when Lenny usually had his, and which Homer used as a kind of scheduling anchor for his own. Homer thought about Mr. Burns, who was not available for comment. Finally, he thought about Smithers, who was also not available for comment. That was unusual; in his experience, when people were not available for comment, it was rarely because they were having a nice morning.

Outside, the sprinklers reached the end of their arc and started back the other way.

“Oh no.”

End Notes

anyway i hope this was okay. i don't usually do the short quiet ones but sometimes you just have to

Roger Ebertat the Movies · rogerebert.com
Friday, July 15, 2005
Movie reviews updated weekly
Home Reviews Great Movies Festivals Answer Man Video Ebert's Journal Interviews Archive
Home › Reviews › New This Week › Charlie and the Chocolate Factory
Charlie and the Chocolate Factory (2005)
★★★½ 3½ stars out of 4
Reviewed by Roger Ebert | Chicago Sun-Times | July 15, 2005
E-Mail This Review Print This Review Add to My Movies Comments (312)
Film Details
RatingPG
Runtime115 minutes
DirectorTim Burton
CastMichael Jackson, Freddie Highmore, Helena Bonham Carter, Noah Taylor, Christopher Lee
StudioWarner Bros.
OpensJuly 15, 2005
Written byJohn August (based on Roald Dahl)
MusicMichael Jackson, Danny Elfman

Charlie and the Chocolate Factory arrives with so much baggage attached to its central casting decision that it is almost impossible to watch the film fresh — which is a shame, because the film itself largely merits fresh watching. Tim Burton has made a dark, strange, and at moments genuinely beautiful adaptation of Roald Dahl's novel, and at its center is a performance by Michael Jackson that is, depending on your tolerance for the particular kind of sincerity that Jackson brings to everything he does, either the most perfectly calibrated piece of casting in recent memory or an act of spectacular, accidental correctness.

The story of how Jackson came to play Willy Wonka has been told and retold in the entertainment press over the past year, and I will not repeat it in detail except to say that the word "bought" appears in most accounts, and that Warner Bros., which reportedly had significant reservations about the casting, has said nothing publicly to contradict this characterization. What I will say is that the studio's reservations, whatever their origin, do not appear in the film. Either they suppressed them thoroughly or the dailies changed their minds, because Charlie and the Chocolate Factory is a Warner Bros. film that commits fully to its central performance, and Jackson, for his part, commits fully to the role.

"Jackson brings to Wonka something no other actor could have brought: the genuine quality of a man who has spent decades building a private world to retreat into, and who has made of that world something spectacular and strange." — Roger Ebert, Chicago Sun-Times

That performance is the crux of the matter, and so I should describe it carefully. Jackson's Wonka is not a broad comic creation. He is not a villain, and he is not precisely a hero. He is something more uncomfortable than either: a recluse whose reclusion has taken the form of elaboration, a man whose relationship to childhood is so consuming that it has colonized everything around him. The factory is not simply a factory. It is an autobiography in architecture. And Jackson, who has spent the better part of two decades building precisely that kind of autobiography in Neverland — who has, in other words, done this in real life — brings to the role something no actor reading from outside could have brought: the genuine quality of a man who has spent decades constructing a private world to retreat into, and who has made of that world something spectacular and strange.

This is either inspired casting, or a coincidence of such magnitude that it amounts to the same thing. I suspect Burton knew what he was doing when he agreed to it, especially considering Burton's own body of work is essentially one long autobiography in style, whose films are all about the loneliness of the person who sees differently, and who would naturally recognize a kindred sensibility. There is a scene midway through the film in which Wonka shows Charlie the room where he creates new candy — a room that is, we understand, the only room in the factory he truly loves —, and Jackson plays it with such a quiet, unguarded pleasure that is more moving than any of the film's more overtly emotional moments. He is not performing delight, perhaps, but he is experiencing it.

The rest of the cast orbits this central sun competently. Freddie Highmore's Charlie Bucket is earnest and open-faced in the manner the role requires; he is the audience's point of entry into a world that needs one. Helena Bonham Carter and Noah Taylor are reliable Burton repertory company as Charlie's parents, doing precisely what they always do, which is not a criticism. Christopher Lee's Dr. Wilbur Wonka — that is, Willy's cold, dentistry-obsessed father — provides the film's backstory with just enough of a menacing precision that borders on parody, and lands just this side of it. The Oompa-Loompas, rendered through effects, are exactly as unsettling as Dahl intended.

"The soundtrack, which Jackson wrote and recorded independently before being attached to the project, is the film's most coherent artistic statement. It sounds like nothing else in contemporary film music." — Roger Ebert

The music deserves particular discussion. The soundtrack, which Jackson wrote and recorded independently before being attached to the project and which was reportedly the subject of considerable negotiation before being integrated into the final film, is the film's most coherent artistic statement. Danny Elfman's orchestral score provides the conventional architecture, but Jackson's songs — the Oompa-Loompa numbers, principally, plus two original pieces ("World of Candy", "What More Do We Give?") that play over the film's opening and closing sequences — are something else entirely. They sound like nothing else in contemporary film music. They are simultaneously childlike and unsettling, exuberant and melancholy, and are precisely the quality that Dahl's source material requires and that most film adaptations of children's literature fail to achieve. The Grammy for Best Original Soundtrack, which this film will presumably receive next spring, is not something that needs to be speculated about.

None of this is to say the film is without problems. Burton's third act has a tendency toward the mechanical that his second acts rarely share, and Charlie and the Chocolate Factory follows this pattern: once the factory tour proper begins and the other Golden Ticket winners begin to be winnowed out, the film becomes more efficient and less strange, and yet strangeness is what it does best. The resolutions of Augustus Gloop, Veruca Salt, Violet Beauregarde, and Mike Teavee are handled competently, but without the anarchic joy that the sequences demand and that the novel provides. One senses that these scenes interest Burton less than the quieter ones, and the film's pacing reflects this.

And then there is the question, which reviewers are obligated to acknowledge and audiences are presumably free to set aside, of what it means to watch Michael Jackson play a man whose relationship to children is the explicit subject of the film. Jackson's recent legal history requires no rehearsal here; the Arvizo case concluded earlier this summer, and the verdict, whatever one makes of it, is part of the cultural context in which this film arrives. I will say only that Burton's film does not, to my eye, engage with this context — it takes Jackson's Wonka entirely at face value, as a man of genuine if unusual affection —, and that the film's success depends on whether the audience is willing to do the same. But many will not be. That is a legitimate response to a legitimate concern. But it is not a response to the film itself.

The film itself is generous, dark, funny, and occasionally beautiful. It is not quite a great film — it is too uneven in its second half and too willing to let its visual invention substitute for emotional depth in several key scenes —, but it is a very good one, and Jackson's performance is at its center like a strange jewel: improbable, specific, and impossible to look away from.

A final note, which belongs in this review because it has already entered the film's public life: President Gary Hart's enthusiastic and apparently unsolicited public endorsement of the film — he described it in a White House statement last week as "the most genuinely American film of the year" and reportedly called Jackson personally to convey his admiration, subsequently extending an invitation for Jackson to visit him and Vice President Bob Graham for a screenover. Apparently, this is the kind of thing that happens to this film and seems, somehow, to fit. A movie this peculiar, starring a performer this singular, arrives in a country where the President of the United States invites the star to the White House on the basis of a chocolate factory movie. This country... I understand Wonka completely.

Related & Further Reading
  • How Michael Jackson Came to Play Willy Wonka: A Timeline
  • Tim Burton on Casting, the Factory, and Jackson's Soundtrack
  • Ebert reviews: Edward Scissorhands (1990), Ed Wood (1994)
  • President Hart praises film, extends White House invitation to Jackson
  • Roald Dahl's Charlie and the Chocolate Factory: A Reading List
  • Ebert's Great Movies: The Wizard of Oz (2001)

UK Politics
Scott announces digital democracy review ahead of e-voting pilot
1 July 2010 | Last updated at 09:42 GMT E-mail this to a friend Printable version
Share this page: Facebook Twitter Reddit Email
Tom Scott speaks to reporters outside Downing Street following a cabinet meeting on Wednesday

Prime Minister Tom Scott has announced a government review into electronic voting, describing a future pilot programme as "long overdue" for a country still marking ballot papers by hand.

The Liberal Democrat leader, who entered Downing Street in May following the party's unexpected outright majority in the general election, said the review would be led by the Cabinet Office and would report to Parliament by spring 2011.

Speaking at a press conference in Westminster, Mr Scott said the programme would examine the experiences of countries including Estonia, which has offered internet voting since 2005, as well as the security concerns raised by critics of electronic systems.

"There is no reason in 2010 why a citizen should have to walk to a village hall, stand at a wooden booth, and make a mark with a pencil stub tied to a piece of string," Mr Scott said. "We should be the party of democratic participation, and that means asking seriously whether our voting infrastructure reflects the country we actually live in."

"We should be the party of democratic participation, and that means asking seriously whether our voting infrastructure reflects the country we actually live in."

The announcement has been welcomed by digital rights advocates, though electoral reform groups cautioned that the technical and security challenges of internet voting remain unresolved. The Electoral Reform Society said any pilot must include independent security auditing and a verifiable paper trail.

The review will also examine the case for extending voting hours and the feasibility of allowing voters to cast ballots from any polling station in their constituency, rather than only the one to which they are registered, a reform Mr Scott's party has advocated since 2007.

The move comes as the coalition's predecessor, the Labour government headed by former prime minister Gordon Brown, twice commissioned and then shelved reports into electronic voting, most recently in 2008 following a pilot in several local authority elections that produced mixed results on turnout and accessibility.

Conservative critics in the shadow cabinet have been broadly sceptical. Shadow home secretary Oliver Renshaw said the government must not allow "a fashionable enthusiasm for technology to outpace the public's confidence in their ballot," while suggesting that the resources might be better directed toward improving postal voting infrastructure.

Number 10 said the Cabinet Office review would be led by Minister for Political and Constitutional Reform Frances Drummond MP, and would include an advisory panel drawn from computer science, law, and civic society. A spokesman declined to confirm whether any specific local authority had been selected for a possible 2012 pilot, but said that several councils had already made informal approaches to the government.

Mr Scott is expected to face questions on the announcement during Prime Minister's Questions next Wednesday.

Related Stories
  • Scott's first 50 days: how the Lib Dem project is taking shape
  • Estonia's e-voting system: how it works and what the risks are
  • Electoral Commission warns on 2008 e-voting pilot 'lessons unlearned'
  • What does the Lib Dem majority mean for constitutional reform?
  • Drummond to lead Cabinet Office reform brief
