` }, { id: "frasier", condition: (ev) => charlieWin && cynicism < 3, image: "https://file.garden/aNtAfG887DiA_7lO/TML_2000/niles.jpg", html: `
Nintendo World Report
Independently covering Nintendo since 1999
NWR > GameCube > Reviews > My Dinner with Daphne (GC)
Extended Review | Issue 214 | Nov 2003

The Game Where Niles Is on the Cover

How a licensed Frasier game came to exist, why it is starring the wrong Crane brother, and what any of this has to do with the show's ninth season.
  • Author: Marcus Ohlendorf
  • Platform: Nintendo GameCube
  • Publisher: Paramount Digital Entertainment
  • Developer: Saffire Corporation
  • Released: October 14, 2003
Official North American box art. Note that Frasier does not appear on the cover.

The first thing you need to understand about My Dinner with Daphne, the Nintendo GameCube game, is that it was not supposed to be about Niles. There exists, somewhere in the archives of Paramount Digital Entertainment, a pitch document for a Frasier game in which the player controls Frasier Crane, the radio psychiatrist, the opera-lover, and the man whose name is in the title, and navigates a series of social disasters in Seattle. That game, which was reportedly titled Frasier: Crane's World at some internal stage and described in one leaked memo as "a sort of Harvest Moon for junkies," was never made. What was made instead is this: a third-person adventure game in which you play as Niles Crane, his younger brother, hosting a dinner party, and the central mechanical challenge is that you are in love with your housekeeper and everyone can tell.

This is, by any reasonable measure, a stranger premise. And yet it is also, if you have watched enough Frasier, a more honest one. Because the truth is that Niles Crane, that is, David Hyde Pierce's portrait of a man too self-aware to stop himself, too intelligent to be excused for his choices, and too besotted to be entirely unsympathetic, was always the show's most interesting character. The game, whether by accident or by some genuine insight on the part of its developers, understood this.

The development history of My Dinner with Daphne is not especially well-documented, but what is known is interesting. Saffire Corporation, the Salt Lake City-based developer responsible for a number of GameCube and GBA licensed titles in the early 2000s, was brought on to develop a Frasier game sometime in mid-2001, around the time the show was entering its ninth season. The contract, as far as can be determined, was negotiated by Paramount Digital Entertainment, who had licensed the show's brand and character likenesses for a number of ancillary products throughout the late nineties and into the new decade. Saffire had previously developed titles based on Rugrats and the Powerpuff Girls, which is perhaps why the resulting game has, in certain areas, a visual clarity that seems almost children's-educational — noticeably, everything in the apartment renders with some kind of a cheerful legibility, as though the game is trying very hard to make sure you know what a settee is.

Season 9 and this game

The connection to the show's ninth season is not exactly incidental. My Dinner with Daphne takes place, explicitly, in the continuity of Season 9, which means that Niles and Daphne are already together. The season ran from 2001 to 2002, depicting the early, often awkward realities of their long-anticipated relationship after years of Niles's increasingly desperate, eight-years-running romantic fixation on Daphne Moon, his father's live-in physical therapist and, crucially, his brother's employee. The game drops you into this particular emotional situation and invites you to inhabit it, which is an unusual thing for a licensed GameCube title to ask of its player. The comparison that kept occurring to me, while playing through the game's six-hour main scenario, was not to other licensed games but to visual novels — to that tradition of games that are less about what you do than about what you know and cannot say.

A note on voice acting
David Hyde Pierce does not provide the voice of Niles in this game, and the game does not pretend otherwise. The soundalike does however, a serviceable job of capturing the cadence without ever quite landing the vulnerability. Kelsey Grammer, by contrast, does voice Frasier in the game's cutscenes, and the contrast between the authentic and the ersatz is unfortunately audible.
The mechanics, or: what you actually do for six hours

The game's structure is a third-person adventure built around what the developers called "social inventory." In addition to the usual object-based inventory, Niles carries a secondary inventory of conversational obligations, emotional debts, and things he has almost said and then swallowed. These are represented as small symbolic objects: a word balloon with a lock on it, a small figure of Daphne rendered in wax, a folded piece of paper labeled simply "the thing about the Merlot." They, as in life, accumulate. By the end of the game, Niles is carrying so many of them that the inventory screen has acquired a small scrollbar.

The external puzzles are, admittedly, underdistinguished. There is a sequence involving a soufflé that collapses and must be reconstructed using items sourced from three different rooms. The game's actual mechanical interest lies in its conversation system, which is a branching dialogue tree of unusual depth and self-awareness. Niles's options are regularly annotated with small parentheticals like "(says too much)" or "(does not look at Daphne as he says this)."

Development and the cover

According to a former Saffire employee, the decision to position Niles rather than Frasier as the game's protagonist emerged from a script review meeting where a Paramount rep described the Niles-Daphne storyline as "the one everyone's actually watching for at this point." After all, making a Frasier game that ignored this would have been like building a house without walls!

The dinner party format is not arbitrary. Niles's dinner party, in the game, is proceeding perfectly, and this is somehow worse, because perfection, in-game, requires presence, and presence requires not thinking about Daphne. This is something that, generally speaking, Niles cannot manage for more than eight minutes at a stretch.

Fun fact: the game keeps a small, discreet timer that tracks uninterrupted minutes of composure. Eight minutes and seventeen seconds (8:17) was my personal best after three playthroughs. The achievement "Ten Minutes" has, reportedly, a completion rate of approximately three percent. — NWR Staff
The ending, briefly
NWR REVIEW SCORES
Gameplay
6.5
Competent, uneven in act 3
Writing
9.0
The real reason to play
Fidelity
8.5
Niles voice aside
Presentation
7.0
Grammer redeems it
OVERALL SCORE
8.0

Without giving away what the game ultimately does with its ending, it is worth saying that the most affecting of the possible conclusions is not the one that resolves anything. There is an ending, which one may describe as the "good ending," where the dinner party goes beautifully. The guests leave, Niles stands alone at the cleared table, and the game's end screen is white, with a single text line: "Dr. Niles Crane had a very nice dinner party."

And then it goes back to the main menu, and the inventory screen, if you look at it before exiting, still has all those locked words and small wax figures and folded notes in it, and the scrollbar is still there, and you have no idea what the fuck just happened. It is the most Frasier thing a game has ever done. One thing I learned from My Dinner with Daphne, ultimately, is that everything right is not the same as anything being resolved.

¹ Saffire Corporation developed primarily for licensed properties throughout the GBA and early GameCube era; the studio closed in 2006.

² The show's ninth season ran Sept 2001–May 2002.

³ "The thing about the Merlot" is never explained. It appears in the social inventory at the start of Act One and is still there at every ending.

Nintendo World Report is an independent Nintendo-focused publication. Review copy provided by Paramount Digital Entertainment.

` }, { id: "enron", condition: (ev) => ev < 269 && support_admin >= 3 && ruthlessness >= 4, image: "https://i.redd.it/zjzq2gb5mcce1.jpeg", html: `
engadget
Mobile Features Reviews

Enron Mobile E-300 review: A battery beast crippled by syllable shock

By Peter Rojas | August 14th 2005, 1:23 PM EST | 342 Comments

We finally got our hands on the highly anticipated Enron Mobile E-300. After Kenneth Lay’s aggressive push into the consumer electronics sector earlier this year, everyone has been wondering if the controversial energy-turned-telecom giant could actually build a decent handset. The hardware answer is a resounding "yes." The software and billing answer, however, is a dystopian "ugh" (which, incidentally, will cost you exactly five cents to say).

Let's talk form factor first. The E-300 is a sleek, black clamshell device featuring a vibrant 65k-color internal display and a very respectable 1.3-megapixel camera. But the real star of the show here is Enron’s proprietary "Smart-Grid" lithium-ion battery. We managed to squeeze a mind-boggling twelve days of standby time and 14 hours of continuous talk time out of this thing. It makes the Motorola RAZR look like it’s running on potato power! If we were rating the E-300 on hardware alone, it would be an absolute must-buy.

But we can't do that. We have to talk about the billing plan.

As you probably know, following Enron's massive 2001 restructuring and their subsequent (and highly controversial) lobbying efforts in Washington, the FCC basically handed them the keys to the kingdom. President Bush’s recently signed "Telecommunications Verbal Equity Act" allowed Enron to patent and privatize the digital transmission of verbal communication over cellular networks. That brings us to the E-300’s primary caveat: the Pay-Per-Syllable (PPS) billing structure.

"Calling your grandmother to wish her a happy birthday will now cost you upwards of twelve dollars if she's a talker."

Instead of charging per minute, Enron’s voice-recognition algorithm actively monitors your calls and charges your account $0.05 per syllable. Say "Hello" to the person on the other line? That's ten cents. Say "I am doing well, how are you today?" That's 45 cents. It sounds trivial until you get your first monthly bill. One of our editors racked up a $412 charge over a single weekend just by having a mildly heated argument with his girlfriend about where to eat dinner. It's insane!

This absurd monetization of vocabulary has already birthed a bizarre new counter-culture among American teenagers. If you’ve hung out near a high school or a mall recently, you’ve probably witnessed the so-called "Enron grunt." Because the E-300's voice-recognition software filters out non-linguistic vocalizations, teens are literally resorting to communicating in grunts, clicks, and extreme shorthand to save their prepaid minutes.

A typical phone conversation between two 16-year-olds in 2005 now sounds something like this:

"Sup." ($0.05)
*affirmative grunt* ($0.00)
"Mall?" ($0.05)
*hesitant grunt* ($0.00)
"Food." ($0.05)
"Bet." ($0.05)

It's fascinating from a linguistic standpoint, but absolutely infuriating from a consumer rights perspective. No wonder President Bush is currently facing his lowest approval ratings to date; allowing a megacorporation to nickel-and-dime the English language is a policy failure of epic proportions.

Text messaging (SMS) remains flat-rate for now, but Enron has already filed a patent for "digital alphabet taxation," so we wouldn't hold our breath.

The Engadget Score

Hardware: 9/10
Battery Life: 10/10
Software/UI: 7/10
Pricing/Value: 1/10

Wrap-up: The Enron Mobile E-300 is a gorgeous, long-lasting clamshell phone ruined by corporate greed. Only recommended for mimes, the incredibly wealthy, or teenagers who have fully embraced the new grunt-culture.
TAGS: enron e-300 cellphones reviews mobile dystopia
View all 342 comments...

Feature Essay  |  November / December 2003

The Pure in Heart:
Blessed Are the Pure and the Uses of a Controversy

A film about two boys and a gun, set in Littleton in 1998, arrives in the middle of a culture war that has almost nothing to do with it — and yet somehow illuminates exactly that.
By Amy Taubin Print Issue, p. 34
Film Information
TitleBlessed Are the Pure
Dir.Jed Sherrill
Year2003 | USA
Runtime104 min.
RatingNot Rated
Distrib.IFC Films
Rating★★★★☆

There is an interesting moment in Blessed Are the Pure, perhaps forty minutes into Jed Sherrill's devastating debut feature, in which the two boys at the center of the film — Eric and Dylan, though the film never uses their surnames — are sitting on the roof of a suburban house in the flat Colorado evening light, sharing a bottle of alcohol, not really speaking. It lasts maybe thirty-five seconds; nothing happens. The camera, mounted at a slight remove, watches them with a patience that feels almost devotional. And then one of them laughs at something the other has said, something we didn't hear, and the film moves on. It is, some have said, the most fully inhabited thirty-five seconds of American cinema this year, and it has become, curiously, and by the accident of political timing, the most argued-over.

The argument, by now, needs little rehearsal for anyone who has been paying attention to American cultural life over the past six months. Sherrill's film — which traces the lives of two Columbine High School students across the fourteen months preceding April 20, 1999, and which ends, famously, on a cut to black as they walk toward the school's entrance on the morning of the massacre — premiered at Sundance in January to genuine critical enthusiasm and a certain anxious reverence. IFC acquired it the following week. It opened in limited release in September, received admiring reviews in most of the serious press, and then, in early October, was transformed into a national controversy by a single sentence uttered by Vice President Dan Quayle at a fundraising dinner in Indianapolis.

"It has almost entirely obscured whether his original charge had any merit, and what we might learn from the fact that a film about two boys and a gun provokes it." — Amy Taubin

Quayle, whose relationship with the English language has never been one of the century's great love affairs, meant to say — or so his office subsequently insisted — that the film contained objectionable "homoeroticism." What he actually said, as captured on a C-SPAN recording that had been viewed approximately four million times on the internet by the end of the week, was that the film was "full of homereotism," which he then glossed, apparently without irony, as "like Homer Simpson, but for real." The clip was on Saturday Night Live within ten days. And this turned Will Forte's Quayle, presenting a PowerPoint captioned "The Male Gaze" with a clip art show of Homer in his small reading glasses and a variety of outfits, while earnestly explaining that Homer-erotism referred to a state of intense masculine identification with animated working-class protagonists, into the cold open most discussed since the 2000 recount. It has not left the cultural conversation since, which is a considerable shame, because it has almost entirely obscured the question of whether Quayle's original charge — whatever word he was reaching for — had any merit, and what we might learn from the fact that a film about two boys and a gun provokes that charge at all.

Let me be direct about the film first, and the circus second, because the film deserves that courtesy. Blessed Are the Pure is not, in any conventional sense, an erotic film, homo or otherwise. It does not sexualize its subjects. There are no sex scenes, no significant physical contact between the two boys, and no explicit declaration of romantic or sexual feelings in either direction. What the film has — and this is the thing that Quayle's malapropism, for all its buffoonery, was dimly pointing toward —, infamously, is an almost unbearable tenderness between its two central figures. The way they look at each other, and the way the film looks at them looking at each other. Sherrill, who grew up in Jefferson County and attended a neighboring high school, shoots Eric and Dylan with a muted ache that viewers inclined to see it will read as something — and viewers who are not so inclined will read as nothing at all. That ambiguity is not an evasion, perhaps, but is the film's most intelligent quality.

The critical debate

The question of whether Blessed Are the Pure is a queer film has organized itself into roughly three camps in the critical literature that has accumulated around it, and it is worth mapping those camps before assessing them. The first, associated most prominently with B. Ruby Rich's piece in the Guardian and J. Hoberman's equivocal notice in the Village Voice, holds that the film is deliberately and productively queer — that is, that Sherrill is using the formal grammar of queer cinema (the lingering gaze, the suppressed disclosure, the violence that arrives partly as the consequence of internalized shame) to make an argument about the relationship between American homophobia and American gun violence. The second camp, represented by David Edelstein and, more forcefully, by James Berardinelli in his curiously offended ReelViews notice, argues that reading queerness into the film is an imposition — that Sherrill is making a film about alienation and masculine socialization that has nothing specifically to do with sexuality, and that the queer reading reduces two real, dead teenagers to a thesis. And the third camp, which is where I find myself, thinks both positions are less interesting than the film itself.

What strikes me most about Blessed Are the Pure is not its queerness or its non-queerness, but its extraordinary attentiveness to what it means to be seen and not seen; to be legible to one other person in a world that is not looking at you at all. The film's Littleton is a place of absolute social indifference. Parents move through rooms without making eye contact, teachers address the middle distance, and the school corridors are organized by a social hierarchy so naturalized it has become invisible to those inside it. Against this background of systematic inattention, the relationship between Eric and Dylan is defined above all by mutual recognition: they see each other. And the camera is an extension of that seeing.

On the Quayle Affair: a brief chronology

October 4, 2003: Vice President Dan Quayle, speaking at a Republican fundraiser in Indianapolis, condemns Blessed Are the Pure as "full of homereotism — homereotism, like Homer Simpson, but for real," as C-SPAN cameras are rolling.

October 11: Saturday Night Live opens with a sketch in which a Quayle impersonator presents a film theory seminar on "Homer-erotism," featuring a PowerPoint slide of Homer Simpson. Will Forte plays Quayle.

October 14: A Quayle spokesperson issues a statement confirming the Vice President "has not seen the film and was responding to descriptions provided by staff."

October 22: Asked about the controversy at a White House press briefing, President Bush declines to comment on the film but says his administration "believes in the importance of artistic freedom." The statement is considered a mild rebuke of Quayle.

October 29: Quayle, appearing on The Tonight Show, is played the original C-SPAN clip by Jay Leno and doubles down: "I don't need to see it. The critics described it, it's right there."

What Quayle was pointing at

None of which is to say the political controversy is without interest as a cultural text in itself. The speed with which conservative commentators seized on the film's alleged homoerotic content, before the Quayle clip made the whole enterprise faintly ridiculous, revealed something about the ideological work that Columbine has been made to perform in the years since 1999. The dominant conservative interpretation of the massacre has always been cultural: the shooters were products of a permissive, godless, media-saturated environment. That interpretation is deeply invested in the heteronormativity of its villains; for instance, an Eric and Dylan who might have been in love with each other would complicate the narrative considerably.

This is, I think, why the film felt threatening to people who hadn't seen it: not because of what it shows, but because of what it implies about the limits of the available explanatory frameworks. Sherrill's boys are not exactly legible in the way cultural conservatism demands. They are private, they are specific, and their relationship to each other, for better or worse, is the most human thing in the film — which is to say, it is the thing that the standard narrative most needs not to be there.

Whether you call that homoerotic or not seems to me genuinely secondary. What the film is, in the end, is a film about love: about what it looks like when two people find each other in a landscape of indifference, and about the catastrophe that awaits when that love is not enough. Sherrill doesn't explain this turn, nor does he offer the massacre as an outcome, exactly; he offers it as a fact the film knows is coming and refuses to account for. And it leaves you wondering if the cut to black is an evasion, or, instead, if it's the most honest thing a film about Columbine could do.

Blessed Are the Pure is a serious film in a season that has not been short of them. It deserves to be discussed in serious terms, which means discussing what it actually does rather than what a C-SPAN clip suggested it might contain. See it before someone explains it to you.


Amy Taubin is a contributing editor at Film Comment and a critic for Artforum. Blessed Are the Pure is in limited release through IFC Films. Wider opening November 14.

FILM COMMENT

World › Middle East › Iraq

Rumsfeld, Facing Allied Rebuke and Senate Vote, Signals Iraq Exit Terms Are Not Negotiable

By DAVID E. SANGER and THOM SHANKER
Published: March 17, 2005
SAVE ★ Save Article E-Mail Print Reprints Share
President Rumsfeld at a White House briefing on Wednesday, where he said any timetable for withdrawal was a matter of "American strategic judgment, not international consensus."
Doug Mills/The New York Times
Related Coverage
  • U.N. Chief Calls on Washington to Accept Oversight Role in Iraq Transition (March 15)
  • Senate Passes Resolution Urging Iraq Timetable, 58 to 40 (March 14)
  • Allied Leaders Warn Rumsfeld of 'Irreparable Damage' to Coalition (March 12)
  • Vieira de Mello, in Washington Visit, Asks for Renewed Mandate (March 10)
  • Pentagon Says Drawdown Plans Are Ready; White House Has Not Approved (March 8)

Washington, March 16 — President Donald H. Rumsfeld, confronted simultaneously by a Senate vote demanding a withdrawal timetable for Iraq, a joint statement of reproach from four major European allies and a personal appeal from United Nations Secretary-General Sérgio Vieira de Mello, said Wednesday that the United States would determine the conditions and pace of any drawdown in Iraq on its own terms, and that the administration had no intention of submitting those terms to international approval.

The president's remarks, delivered in a brief and notably combative exchange with reporters in the Roosevelt Room following a cabinet meeting, came as the White House found itself in what several senior officials privately acknowledged was an increasingly difficult position: defending an Iraq posture that Rumsfeld himself had campaigned on as a corrective to the Bush administration's open-ended commitments, but which critics now argued had produced a different kind of ambiguity — one in which American forces remained in substantial numbers without either a clear mission or a credible end date.

"I ran against the idea that America should spend a decade turning someone else's country into a reasonable facsimile of Iowa," Mr. Rumsfeld said. "I still hold that view. What I will not accept is the notion that the pace of our withdrawal should be set by a committee in Geneva, or by a resolution drafted by senators who have never spent a night in Fallujah."

"The pace of our withdrawal will not be set by a committee in Geneva or a resolution drafted by senators who have never spent a night in Fallujah."

— President Rumsfeld, Wednesday

The Mondale-Smith Senate resolution, which passed Monday by a margin of 58 to 40 in a rebuke that crossed party lines, called on the administration to present Congress with a detailed plan for reducing American troop levels in Iraq by the end of the calendar year. The vote was largely symbolic, as the resolution carried no binding force, but its bipartisan character, with eleven Republican senators joining the Democratic majority, was seen by analysts as a significant marker of eroding political support for the administration's handling of the occupation.

Secretary-General Vieira de Mello, who survived the Canal Hotel attack in Baghdad in 2003 and whose early recovery became a symbol of continued United Nations commitment to Iraqi reconstruction, arrived in Washington on Monday for three days of meetings with senior administration officials, lawmakers on both sides of the aisle, and the Iraqi ambassador. In a joint news conference with Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice on Tuesday, he said that a withdrawal without a functioning international mandate for the successor security arrangement would create what he called "a vacuum that no Iraqi institution is yet equipped to fill alone."

People familiar with Mr. Vieira de Mello's private meetings said he had presented American officials with a detailed United Nations framework under which a residual multinational force, smaller than the current American footprint but drawn from a broader coalition, could provide a transitional security guarantee while Iraqi forces assumed primary responsibility. The framework had the support of France, Germany, Britain and Canada, whose foreign ministers issued a joint communiqué on Tuesday calling on the administration to engage "constructively and urgently" with the United Nations proposal.

Administration officials said the president had reviewed the U.N. framework and found it, in the words of one senior official who spoke on condition of anonymity because deliberations were continuing, "well-intentioned but pretty unrealistic." A second official was more blunt. "The secretary-general is proposing that we hand off to a force that doesn't exist yet, governed by a mandate that hasn't been written, in a security environment that nobody can predict," this official said. "This is no good plan."

Senator Joseph R. Biden Jr. of Delaware, the ranking Democrat on the Foreign Relations Committee and a co-sponsor of the Senate resolution, said in an interview that the administration was conflating two separate questions. "Nobody is asking the president to surrender command of American forces to the United Nations," Mr. Biden said. "We are asking him to describe, in terms that the American people and our allies can understand, what success in Iraq looks like and when he expects to achieve it. He has been in office for ten months, so that is not an unreasonable thing to ask."

The White House has been careful to avoid a direct confrontation with Mr. Vieira de Mello, whose international standing and personal history in Iraq make him a difficult figure to publicly dismiss. But in background briefings this week, administration officials have sought to characterize the U.N. proposal as a European-driven initiative rather than an independent United Nations judgment, noting that the four countries behind the joint communiqué are the same governments that opposed the original invasion. Officials pointed to the absence of Australia, Poland and several other coalition members from the statement as evidence that the coalition remained more durable than European commentary suggested.

Continued on Page A8 of the New York Times

AllPolitics» White House» Story

Gore signs landmark gun control law, calls it 'a promise kept to America's children'

President says new legislation closes loopholes, mandates safe storage, and brings U.S. gun laws into the 21st century

Posted: 11:34 a.m. ET, February 19, 2003  |  Updated: 12:02 p.m. ET
From CNN White House Correspondent John King and Congressional Correspondent Dana Bash
Story Tools
  • Save this article
  • E-mail this story
  • Print-friendly page
  • Subscribe to alerts
  • Feedback
President Gore, flanked by families of gun violence victims and Democratic lawmakers, signs the Safe Schools and Communities Act in the East Room of the White House on Wednesday.
AFP/Getty Images

Washington (CNN) — President Al Gore signed the Safe Schools and Communities Act into law Wednesday morning in an emotional East Room ceremony attended by survivors of the 1999 Columbine High School shooting, families of gun violence victims, and dozens of Democratic lawmakers who pushed the legislation through a Congress newly emboldened by the party's stronger-than-expected midterm showing last November.

The law — the most sweeping federal gun control legislation enacted since the Brady Handgun Violence Prevention Act a decade ago — closes the so-called gun show loophole that has for years allowed sales between private parties to proceed without background checks, mandates secure storage requirements for firearms kept in homes with minors, establishes a nationwide registry of lost and stolen weapons, and bans the manufacture and sale of ammunition magazines holding more than ten rounds for civilian use.

Speaking from a lectern bearing the presidential seal, Gore visibly struggled to contain his emotion as he described watching the news coverage of the Columbine massacre in April 1999, while he was still serving as vice president.

"I remember exactly where I was. I remember Tipper and I watching those images come in, children running from their own school, and thinking: this cannot be who we are. This cannot be the country we leave to our grandchildren. Today, we make good on a promise. Today, American schools take one step into the 21st century."

— President Al Gore

Vice President Joe Lieberman, who as a senator was a vocal proponent of gun control legislation throughout the 1990s and made the issue a central element of the 2000 campaign, stood directly behind the president as Gore signed the bill with a dozen pens, distributing them afterward to the families assembled in the front row.

The ceremony was deliberately staged to acknowledge the long road to passage. Among those present were the parents of Cassie Bernall and Daniel Rohrbough, two of the thirteen students killed at Columbine, as well as James and Sarah Brady, the latter of whom founded the Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence following her husband's shooting in the 1981 assassination attempt on President Ronald Reagan. Sarah Brady wept openly as the president signed, and Gore paused to take her hand.

The bill passed the Senate last month by a vote of 52 to 48, a margin that reflected the Democrats' improved position after November's midterms, in which the party gained six Senate seats and twenty-three House seats in a result that many analysts attributed partly to voter frustration with Republican obstruction of the administration's domestic agenda. The House passed an identical bill in December by a margin of 224 to 211, with twelve Republicans crossing the aisle to support it and three Democrats voting against.

Related Stories
  • What's in the Safe Schools and Communities Act? A guide to the key provisions
  • NRA president: 'We will fight this law in every court in America'
  • Columbine families: 'We never stopped hoping this day would come'
  • Republicans warn of Second Amendment showdown at Supreme Court
  • How the 2002 midterms changed the gun control calculus
  • Background: The Brady Act and the decade since

The National Rifle Association, which spent more than $18 million opposing Democratic candidates in the 2002 midterm elections, announced within minutes of the signing that it would file a federal lawsuit challenging the constitutionality of the law's background check expansion and magazine capacity restrictions. NRA President Charlton Heston said in a written statement that the law represented "a direct and deliberate assault on the rights guaranteed to every American by the Second Amendment" and vowed that the organization's legal challenge would reach the Supreme Court.

Legal scholars said that prediction was plausible. The Supreme Court has not issued a definitive ruling on the scope of the Second Amendment's individual right to bear arms since its 1939 decision in United States v. Miller, and several justices have indicated in concurrences and dissents in recent years that they believe the question of whether the amendment protects individual gun ownership independent of militia service remains unsettled. A challenge to the Safe Schools Act, if it moved quickly through the lower courts, could arrive at the high court as early as the 2005 or 2006 term.

"This law is going to be litigated extensively, and I don't think anyone should be surprised by that," said Laurence Tribe, a Harvard Law School professor and constitutional scholar who advised the administration during the drafting process. "The administration believed it was on solid constitutional ground and designed the legislation accordingly. But the courts will have their say, and the Supreme Court may ultimately have to provide the clarity on the Second Amendment that it has so far declined to give."

The White House said it was confident the law would withstand legal scrutiny, pointing to the fact that the Justice Department conducted a lengthy review of each provision before the bill was introduced. Press Secretary Chris Lehane said the administration "welcomes the opportunity to defend this law" and that the president expected it to be upheld in full.

Republican reaction was swift and almost uniformly negative. Senate Minority Leader Trent Lott of Mississippi said the law was "an overreach of federal authority" that his party would seek to repeal if it regained the Senate majority. House Majority Leader Tom DeLay of Texas called it "a cynical attempt by this administration to punish law-abiding gun owners for the crimes of a violent few," adding that he expected the Supreme Court to "set things right."

Several Republican senators from swing states were notably quieter. Olympia Snowe of Maine and Susan Collins of Maine, both of whom had faced intense constituent pressure over the gun show loophole issue in recent years, declined to comment when approached by reporters Wednesday morning.

Back to top E-mail story Print version Your E-mail Alerts: Gun Control Your E-mail Alerts: Al Gore © 2003 Cable News Network LP, LLLP
Most Popular

5. What happens now at the Supreme Court?

` }, { id: "cartman", condition: (ev) => linusWin && cynicism < 3 && ruthlessness < 5, image: "https://i.ebayimg.com/00/s/MTI4MVgxMDU2/z/1lEAAOSwBPRcPjU~/$_57.JPG?set_id=8800005007", html: `
STAR★WEEKLY
America's Most Trusted Source for Celebrity Truth
MARCH 17, 2003
VOL. 22 · NO. 11
$2.99 / CAN $4.25
INSIDE: BRITNEY'S NEW DIET PLAN ✦ JENNIFER ANISTON'S SECRET HEARTBREAK ✦ EXCLUSIVE: WHAT J.LO'S WEDDING DRESS REALLY COST ✦ CELEBRITY HOMES SPECIAL PULLOUT
★ Exclusive The interview everyone in America is talking about · Pages 24–28 · Eric Cartman opens up — for real this time
World Exclusive

"I Have Learned
From My
Sins — Big
League"

South Park's most controversial young man has crisscrossed the nation saying two words — and he says it's been "totally, absolutely tremendous." Star Weekly sat down with Eric T. Cartman for the only interview he gave this year. We regret to inform you that it was an experience.
By Donna Fichter, Staff Correspondent Photography: Rick Salter Styling: N/A (subject refused)
Cartman, photographed at the Radisson, Tulsa, OK · Feb. 2003
I am doing the most incredible apology in the history of apologies. People are calling me, they're saying, 'Eric, this is the best Sorry Tour they have ever seen.'

He arrives twenty minutes late to the hotel meeting room in an unusual brown suit, with his hat slightly askew, carrying what appears to be a partially eaten bag of Cheesy Poofs, and the confidence of a man who has recently been on the local news in fourteen states. Eric Theodore Cartman, age ten, native of South Park, Colorado, does not wait to be asked a question before he begins answering it.

"I want to say," he announces, setting down the Cheesy Poofs and gesturing broadly at a room that contains, at this point, only myself and a hotel pen, "that the response to this tour has been — and I'm saying this, I'm not having someone else say it, I'm saying it myself —, wow, incredible. Absolutely tremendous. The crowds, the love, the appreciation. Very classy. Very beautiful, frankly."

This is the I'm Sorry Tour: Eric Cartman's self-organized, self-funded, and aggressively self-publicized tour of the continental United States, in which he has appeared before public gatherings in twenty-two cities, stepped up to a microphone, said the words "I'm sorry" (sometimes followed by a brief, ambiguous pause), and departed the premises. The tour has been described variously by attendees as "confusing," "oddly moving," "extremely short," and, in the case of the Denver stop, "a parking nightmare."

Cartman's publicist, Mr. Leopold Stotch, reached out to Star Weekly in January with a press release described as "the most important press release in the history of print journalism, and uh, possibly all media?"

We drove to Tulsa. We have no regrets, mostly.

What inspired the Sorry Tour?
"Look. I have been — and I'll be honest, very honest, I'm an honest person, people say 'Eric, you're so honest' and I say yes, I know — I have been, in the past, not entirely respectful of certain groups. Certain people. Certain communities. And I looked inside myself — deeply, deeply inside, it was a very long look — and I said: Eric, you have to do better. You are a tremendous person with a lot to offer, and that energy has to go somewhere positive. And so I started the tour."
Walk us through how a typical stop works.
"It's very simple. Very elegant. I come out, I go up to the microphone — we have a very nice setup, great sound system, the best sound system, actually —, and I say 'I'm sorry.' And then I leave. That's it. That's the whole thing. People have said it's the most moving experience of their lives. Grown men have wept. In Cincinnati there were tears, many beautiful, beautiful tears. And I was like, wow."

[At this point I asked whether he specified, in the apology, what he was sorry for. He looked at me for a long moment.]

"I think the apology speaks for itself. You don't qualify a great apology, that's what makes it great. If I start putting conditions on it, I'm cheapening it. It's like if Michelangelo painted the Sistine Chapel and then put a little note at the bottom saying 'this was painted while I was tired.' Oh come on, you don't do that. You let the art breathe."
You've said this is about making amends with the Jewish community specifically.
"Huge love. Huge. Kyel- uh, Kyle is.. and I'll say this, publicly, on the record... Kyle is one of the most talented, most brilliant people I have ever known. I know, right? Fantastic person, incredible. Now, do I think he is the smartest? I would say — I would say he is very smart. Very. Not smarter than me, of course, but very smart. And yes, in the past I may have said things that were not entirely— uh, look, I was younger. I was finding myself. But now I love the Jews, all of them. Tremendous people. Tre-mendous. Very good with finances, which I respect enormously. I have always respected that. The stereotypes, they're wrong, so wrong. But I too am very good with finances."
And the gingers?
"Gorgeous, absolutely gorgeous. Red hair, no freckles, freckles, doesn't matter... all beautiful. I have said some things in the past about souls. About the, ah, soul situation. And I want to be very clear: I believe gingers have souls. I believe this. I have looked into this scientifically, and uh, well, — I have looked into it, yeah. And the conclusion I have reached is: probably yes. Almost certainly. And that's a very positive statement coming from me, if you know the history."
✦ Groups Cartman Now Claims to Love ✦
  • Jewish people (Kyle specifically; others "by extension")
  • Gingers ("probably definitely have souls")
  • Hippies ("some of them work hard, I'm told")
  • Tolkien / "Black people generally, great community"
  • Canadians ("beautiful country, tremendous geese")
  • Stan ("a real friend, once you get past the hippie whining")
  • Women ("incredible, powerful, frankly terrifying")
  • Wendy Testaburger ("an adversary I deeply respect")
★ The "I'm Sorry" National Tour ★
Jan.–Mar. 2003 · 22 Cities · One Apology
South Park, CO Hometown opener Done
Denver, CO "Parking nightmare" Done
Cincinnati, OH Reported tears Done
Atlanta, GA Crowd of ~40 Done
Tulsa, OK Interview dateline Done
Pittsburgh, PA Pending Upcoming
Cleveland, OH CANCELLED Never
Is there anyone you haven't forgiven?
"The Cleveland Browns."

[He said this very quickly and then waited for the follow-up.]
The... football team?
"The Cleveland Browns. As an organization, as a franchise, as a concept, f*ck it. I want to be fair. I love Cleveland, the city is wonderful, great people, I did a stop there— well, I was going to do a stop there, and then I did some thinking and said, you know, this is not —, uh, I can't. I can't endorse what that franchise represents. The losing, all the hatred. The heartbreak they cause those people, year after year, it's bad... Someone has to say it, I'm not afraid to say it. The Browns are a disgrace, frankly. A total and complete disgrace to football, to Ohio, to the concept of effort. And I will NOT be apologizing to them. The tour does not stop in Cleveland. The tour will never stop in Cleveland. Cleveland knows what it did."
What about Saddam Hussein? The war just started—
"I think it's very important, very important, that we— look, I'm a man of peace now. That's my brand, my authoritah, and the whole message of the tour. But I also believe in strength. You have to be strong, very strong. A weak apology helps nobody. My apologies are incredibly strong apologies. The strongest I've ever seen, too. Some people, they do a little 'I'm sorry', which is so quiet, small, and pathetic. Mine is big! Not as big as me, as I am not fat, merely big-boned. And yet it fills a room. It fills a... a, you know, Radisson ballroom, frankly. Saddam, I don't want to get political. I'll just say: he could benefit from a tour like this, he really could. A nice 'I'm sorry,' step back, let the crowd absorb it. So transformative..."
"The tour will never stop in Cleveland. Cleveland knows what it did." — Eric T. Cartman, Radisson Tulsa, March 2003
What's next after the tour?
"I'm considering a book. The Art of the Apology. It would be the best book on apologies ever written. I'd also consider a line of merchandise: the 'I'm Sorry' hat, the 'I'm Sorry' jacket, the 'I'm Sorry' Cheesy Poofs limited edition. Ooh, I like that one. I've spoken to some very serious people in business about this. Very serious, very wealthy people. They said it's the best idea they've heard. Now I'm not going to name names, they asked me not to, but they said: Eric, this is bigger than you know, you are so kewl."
Any message for the people who've attended the tour?
"Thank you. From the bottom of my heart, a very large, very giving heart, thank you. To the gingers, to the Jews, to everyone I may have wronged: you are forgiven. I mean— I am sorry. You are— I forgive you for being upset. No— I am sorry. That's the message. Those two words, I'm sorry.

"And to the Cleveland Browns: I am not sorry. Not even slightly."
★ Star Weekly Fast Facts
  • The tour has visited 22 cities in 7 weeks
  • Average crowd size: 47 (Denver: 200; Tulsa: 11)
  • Length of apology: 2 seconds (Cincinnati), 4 seconds (Atlanta, incl. pause)
  • Merchandise sold: 0 (not yet available; "negotiations ongoing")
  • Mother's response: "I'm so proud of my big, sweet boy" — Liane Cartman
Also Inside Winona Ryder's comeback · Brad & Jen's "perfect" vacation · Is Kelly Clarkson the next Whitney? · Demi Moore's body at 40 · Full horoscopes

Season 35 Episodes Most-Watched Interviews Political Profiles Health Stories
60 Minutes  ·  Health / Politics

'My Mind Has Been Given Back To Me':
Ronald Reagan Speaks

At 91, the former president discusses memory, regret, Keynes, the AIDS crisis, and the experimental stem-cell treatment that he says has "rewired" him — while his party looks the other way

By Ed Bradley, 60 Minutes Correspondent | CBSNews.com | October 6, 2002
E-Mail This Story Print This Story Full Transcript (PDF) Watch the Segment
Background

In November 1994, Ronald Reagan publicly disclosed his diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease in a handwritten letter to the American people. For the eight years that followed, he largely withdrew from public life, his condition worsening progressively. In late 2001, Reagan became one of twelve patients enrolled in a clinical trial of an experimental stem-cell-derived neural regeneration therapy at the Salk Institute in La Jolla, California. In the months following treatment, associates of the former president reported significant cognitive improvement. Reagan's office confirmed these reports in July 2002. His decision to speak publicly — and specifically to address the stem-cell controversy surrounding his treatment — is the context for this interview.

Note: 60 Minutes requested comment from the Bush White House regarding the apparent conflict between the administration's restrictions on federal stem cell funding and the therapy that has benefited the former president. The White House did not respond.

Santa Barbara, Calif. — On a warm September afternoon at Rancho del Cielo, the 688-acre ranch above Santa Barbara where he spent so many of the happiest days of his presidency, Ronald Reagan sat in a wooden chair on the porch and looked out at the Pacific-facing hills, reacquainting himself with a view he had thought he was losing forever.

He is ninety-one years old. His hair is mostly white, and his hands, which grip the armrests of the chair with a strength that surprises you, are the hands of someone who spent decades outdoors. And when he begins to speak — slowly and carefully —, what he says is not what you expect, from a man of his age, his party, his legacy. It is more honest than that, and also more complicated.

"I lost eight years," he tells me, after a moment. "I don't think about it that way all the time. But when I do think about it, I think: eight years. That's a long time. That's — well, that's two presidencies." He smiles at his own joke. The smile is Reagan's smile, the one that had been seen on a thousand posters, and it is startling to see it operating at full power again at ninety-one. "I have to say, it feels very good to be back."

The Treatment

Reagan received the experimental stem-cell therapy — a protocol developed over seven years by researchers at the Salk Institute in collaboration with a team from the University of California San Diego — in three sessions between November 2001 and March 2002. The treatment, which has not yet received FDA approval and is not available to the general public, uses neural progenitor cells derived from embryonic stem cells to target the amyloid plaques associated with Alzheimer's disease. Reagan is one of twelve patients in the Phase II trial. Of the twelve, seven have shown measurable cognitive improvement. Reagan's improvement has been described by his neurologist as "exceptional" and, in at least some respects, "unprecedented."

He does not use the word "miracle," however, to describe this. "I'm a man of faith," he says, "and I would be lying if I said faith had nothing to do with how I think about this. But what happened to me is science. It's careful, patient, serious science, done by very serious people. And I want to be honest about that, because I think — I think there are people who would prefer I wasn't."

"I want to be honest about the science, because I think there are people who would prefer I wasn't." — Ronald Reagan

He does not say who those people are, and perhaps he does not need to. A year earlier, President George W. Bush had announced severe restrictions on federal funding for embryonic stem cell research, a decision driven in significant part by the same conservative religious constituency that put both men in the White House. The treatment that returned Reagan's memory to him is the direct product of the science that policy was designed to constrain. No one in his party, in the months since Reagan's improvement became publicly known, has found a comfortable way to discuss this fact. Reagan, for his part, seems almost serenely unconcerned with giving them one.

The interview — selected transcript
60 Minutes — Ed Bradley interviews Ronald Reagan Recorded September 18–19, 2002 · Rancho del Cielo · Santa Barbara, California · Edited for broadcast
Bradley When you try to describe what the experience of the treatment was, that is, the recovery — what words do you reach for?
Reagan You know, I've thought about this a great deal. And the word I keep coming back to is "rewired." I know that sounds mechanical. Nancy doesn't like it, she thinks it makes me sound like a toaster. [laughs] But that's what it felt like. As though someone had gone into the back of the apparatus and reconnected things that had come loose. I would— I'd have a thought, and it would arrive. And that sounds simple, but for a long time, the thought would start and then it would just... dissolve. And now it arrives. That's extraordinary, that's— everything, really.
Bradley You've been very open about saying this treatment is stem-cell based. Was that a deliberate choice?
Reagan Well, it's the truth. I don't see the sense in being anything other than straightforward about the truth, at my age. [pause] There's a debate going on about this research, and I understand the moral concerns — I do, genuinely —, but I also know what this research did for me. And I think the American people deserve to know that too. All of it, and well, not just the parts that are comfortable.
Bradley We asked the White House for a response to your comments about stem cell research, but they didn't reply. Does that surprise you?
Reagan [long pause, a slight smile] No. I understand the position they're in. It's not a comfortable position. I was in uncomfortable positions myself, from time to time, during my years. You find a way to be very busy on other matters. [beat] I don't intend to make it easier for them than that.
Bradley Having had eight years away from public life, from — from full cognition, that is — what do you find yourself thinking about differently? What has the distance given you?
Reagan Oh, that's the question, isn't it. [long pause, looks out at the hills] I'll tell you something that will probably surprise people. I've been thinking a great deal about economics, about my economic record. About whether — whether what I believed, and what I said I believed, were the same thing. And I think, if I'm honest, the answer is... more complicated than I made it sound at the time.
Bradley What do you mean by that?
Reagan Well — I'll say something now that I wouldn't have said then, and that my friends at the Heritage Foundation will be very unhappy about. [laughs softly] I've been reading Keynes. Or re-reading, I should say. And I think — I think I always had a Keynesian heart, even when I was making anti-Keynesian speeches. You see, the deficits, the military spending, the '82 tax adjustments... if you look at what we actually did, rather than what we said we were doing, there's a case — a strong case — that we were Keynesians in practice. I just didn't have the courage to say so. Or maybe I didn't have the clarity, I'm not sure which is worse.
Bradley You said you had "lusted" after different ideas but were "Keynesian at heart." That's a striking way to put it.
Reagan Well, I have some experience with the word "lust" in a political context. [laughs] That was Jimmy, not me, sure, but I understand the sentiment. You hold an idea in your mind that you know isn't quite the whole story, and you dress it up in better clothes than it deserves, and you send it out and hope for the best. The supply-side argument was partly true, the spending discipline was partly real. But partly is not entirely. And I think we did, in some respects, prove Keynes right by accident. The stimulus worked. The deficits were enormous and the economy recovered. I don't think we should be too proud of the mechanism, but the outcome was — well, the outcome was strong. We got a strong outcome.
Bradley I want to ask you about something that you didn't address publicly during your presidency in the way that, in retrospect, many people — and perhaps you — feel you should have. The AIDS epidemic.
Reagan [long silence. Reagan is still for a moment. His hands, which have been relaxed on the armrests, tighten slightly] Yes.
Bradley What do you want to say about it now?
Reagan I want to say that I was wrong. I was— I was badly wrong. And I have had— I have had a long time, some of it more conscious than I'd like to admit, to think about what that cost. What it cost people who were sick and frightened and being told by their government, in effect, by my silence and by my administration's silence, that they didn't matter enough to be spoken to directly. That is— [he stops. His voice has changed a bit, and he does not look at the camera.] I am sorry for that. I don't have a way to say it that doesn't sound inadequate. It is inadequate. But it's what I have.
Bradley In an interview with an aide some years ago, you were reported to have said that when you tried to think about the epidemic, tried to imagine it, you became, in your word, "completely discombobulated." What did you mean by that?
Reagan I meant that when I— when I tried to do the thing that you're supposed to do, which is put yourself in someone else's position— to imagine yourself sick with it, frightened with it, alone with it... and I couldn't hold that image in my mind without it— without something breaking down in me. Something I didn't know how to manage. I was afraid of what it would mean to feel that fully. And so I didn't, I kept a certain distance. And people died in that distance. [he stops. His eyes are bright. He does not cry, but it is close.] I would give a great deal to have been a braver man on that particular question.
Bradley Is there anything you would want to say to the people who were affected?
Reagan Only that I am deeply, genuinely sorry. And that the silence was cowardice. I dressed it up as other things at the time. It was cowardice. I know that now and I expect I knew it then. [pause] I don't think sorry is enough. I don't have anything more than sorry. But you should have the sorry, at least.
Bradley A final question. You are ninety-one years old, you have been given, in some sense, a second chance at clarity. What do you do with that?
Reagan Well. [looks out at the hills for a long moment] I think you try to tell the truth as best you can. You try to say the things you should have said when it was costly to say them. It's not costly now, I'm ninety-one. There's nothing much they can do to me. [small laugh] So you say the true things, and you hope that the people who still have something to lose from the true things will hear them, and find a little courage somewhere. That's what I'm doing today. I think that's what this is.

The interview ends there, on the porch, with the Santa Ynez Mountains behind him. Reagan stands up from the chair slowly, walks to the porch railing and stands there for a moment, looking out. He does not say anything else. After a moment, he goes inside.

Nancy Reagan, who has not been present for the interview, meets him at the door. She takes his arm, and he says something to her that we cannot hear. She puts her head briefly against his shoulder. And then they go in together, and the door closes.

Charlie Rose
Transcript Excerpt — PBS
Original Air Date: November 12, 2003
Guest: Former President Gerald R. Ford
Charlie Rose
…but let me go back to what you wrote about Nixon. Because you argue in the essay… you write that the pardon was, for you, the ultimate confrontation with the “Big Other”.
Gerald Ford
That’s exactly right, Charlie. Because when you pardon Richard Nixon—
Charlie Rose
(interrupting) But people didn’t see it as the Symbolic order as you suggested. They saw it as a corrupt bargain, a quid pro quo.
Gerald Ford
Of course they did. Because they were entirely trapped in the Imaginary, they were projecting their own anxieties onto the event. But you step into the Oval Office under those circumstances, and you realize very quickly that the presidency strips you of your subjectivity. I was no longer Jerry Ford, I became a signifier. And Nixon had become the symptom. The whole country was caught in this neurotic loop, obsessing over the tapes. And if you read Lacan’s seminar on “The Purloined Letter,” the tapes are precisely that— the hidden object that dictates the entire structure of the discourse, even when unseen. The pardon was, to put it plainly, the intervention of the Real.
Charlie Rose
Which brings me to today. Because you look at the political landscape heading into 2004, you look at the Democratic field challenging President Bush. John Kerry, Howard Dean, Ted Turner, Wesley Clark, and now—
Gerald Ford
Trump.
Charlie Rose
Donald Trump, yes. Running as a Democrat, throwing out the rulebook…
Gerald Ford
(nodding slowly) Well, Trump is fascinating. He is a walking, breathing manifestation of the Mirror Stage.
Charlie Rose
Explain that.
Gerald Ford
He is completely captivated by his own specular image, Charlie. There is no mediation through the Symbolic order for him. He bypasses the law, he bypasses standard political decorum, and he speaks directly from the id. Bush, Cheney, Kerry— well, I see they are all trying to operate within the Symbolic. They talk about tax credits, healthcare savings accounts, etcetera, but you can see Trump just points out the lack in the Other.
Charlie Rose
He exposes the lack, you say?
Gerald Ford
Precisely. He functions as a disruption of the Symbolic. But, Charlie, this is entirely an Imaginary ego-struggle. It’s pure narcissism masquerading as policy. Now, getting back to what I was saying about jouissance and the executive branch…
Charlie Rose
But let me just ask you this, because people are wondering. Do you think a disruption like that can win? Does the American public want the id? Would someone like, say, you—
Gerald Ford
(sighs slightly, waving a hand) Charlie, electoral politics is just the management of collective neurosis. We vote to sustain our own fantasies, plain and simple. Whether Donald Trump wins the nomination, or whether John Kerry wins it, or if is someone like Mickey Mouse, it is completely immaterial to the underlying psychological structure of the state. The symptom will just manifest elsewhere, understand this. What I find much more interesting, and what I was trying to get at in the Review, is how the concept of the objet petit a relates to the presidential veto power.
Charlie Rose
(smiles, leaning forward) The veto power.
Gerald Ford
Yes. The veto is the ultimate expression of the unattainable object of desire in the legislative process. Congress produces the bill, the demand, and the President, through the veto, introduces the cut, the lack. And so what you have is the castration of the legislative body. I vetoed sixty-six bills during my time in office, Charlie. And every time I did it, I was inadvertently demonstrating the impossibility of complete political wholeness. We are all just chasing the lost object…

𝕭. Announcement (self.thecampaigntrail)
submitted 4 hours ago by Magic_Kirker_2129 to r/thecampaigntrail

Announcement

It's been three years since the American people, in a fit of delirium, handed him the nuclear launch codes. The Bourdain regency has been characterized by an agonizing realization that the Oval Office is just a boring panic room, and the political establishment is like one of the many bad chain restaurants he'd been to. With the corporate ghouls in Congress eating them alive in the midterms, and the press circling like vultures, it's only a matter of time until he can finally go back to eating noodles in Hanoi.

𝕭. follows an Anthony Bourdain presidency from January 2009 until Election Day 2012, and puts his admittedly nicotine-fueled term in the player's hands to either salvage his failing domestic agenda or completely implode (in many ways actually!) his presidency and the nation. The mod features 14 opponents, Republican and Third Parties alike, multiple endings, and utilizes extensive CYOA to give questions completely different outcomes based on previous decisions.

r/thecampaigntrail - First question

A major feature of the mod is what we call the Global Travel Mechanic. Similar to other mods (such as Affable's Despair or recently released Mister President, you can choose to ditch Washington D.C. entirely to visit diplomatic hotspots (or just a really good street food stall in Oaxaca, there are many of them actually). Do you stay in the Rose Garden to negotiate a milquetoast infrastructure bill, or do you go on a televised bender in Beirut with the Prime Minister of Lebanon to bypass the State Department entirely? Your approval ratings, and your blood pressure, will reflect these choices.

The mod is planned to be released this Thanksgiving day. Stay tuned for more.

` }, { id: "kennedy", condition: (ev) => ev >= 208 && ev < 269 && cynicism <= 3, image: "https://file.garden/aNtAfG887DiA_7lO/TML_2000/thedreamshallneverdie.webp", html: `

Home › Productions › Musicals › The Dream Shall Never Die
Playbill
The Dream
Shall Never
Die
A New Musical
Majestic Theatre
Production
Overview Opening Night Cast Current Cast Creative Team Song List Awards Box Office Photos (48) Reviews
Also Playing

Kennedy Musicals
Camelot (1960) JFK: The Musical (1993) Things That Never Were (1999)
The Dream Shall Never Die
Original Musical  ·  Broadway  ·  2004
★★★★½ 8.9 / 10  ·  1,847 ratings
🏆 Tony Award — Best Musical 2005
CategoryMusical (original)
StatusCurrently playing
TheatreMajestic Theatre
Previews BeganOctober 8, 2004
OpenedNovember 4, 2004
Performances482 (as of March 12, 2005)
BookJohn Weidman
MusicAdam Guettel
LyricsAdam Guettel & Lynn Ahrens
DirectionGeorge C. Wolfe
ChoreographyBill T. Jones
Scenic DesignEugene Lee
ProducerScott Rudin, Lincoln Center Theater
Synopsis
Described in its own program notes as "the best musical about the best Kennedy brother," The Dream Shall Never Die follows Senator Edward M. Kennedy from the night of the 1980 Democratic National Convention through his first year in the White House, as he navigates the twin burdens of the presidency and the Kennedy legacy. Haunted — literally — by the spectral presences of his brothers Jack and Bobby, who appear throughout the show to counsel, contradict, and occasionally ridicule him, Ted must contend with the Iranian hostage crisis, a fractured Democratic Party, and the persistent suspicion that the office he has always wanted is the one thing that cannot give him what he is looking for. The show's second act, set during the first summer of the Kennedy administration, is largely a meditation on grief, institutional power, and what it means to be the last one standing. It has been described by The New York Times as "devastating" and by The Post as "an evening that earns its running time."
Opening Night Cast View replacements →
Actor Character Notes
Brian Stokes Mitchell Edward M. Kennedy Lead. Tony Award — Best Actor in a Musical, 2005. Described by Guettel as "the only possible choice."
Audra McDonald Joan Kennedy Tony Award — Best Featured Actress, 2005. Her Act II aria, "The Shape of Quiet," has been called the best new Broadway song of the decade.
Raúl Esparza John F. Kennedy Ghost Appears in Scenes 2, 5, 7, 11, and the Act II finale.
Daniel Evans Robert F. Kennedy Ghost Challenges Ted directly in "You Should Have Run in '68." Bobby's scenes have been interpreted as the show's emotional center.
John Lithgow Jimmy Carter Described as "comic and tragic in equal measure". First appears in the Act I opener "Plains, Georgia".
Tonya Pinkins Rosemary Kennedy Appears only in Act II dream sequence; no spoken dialogue. Tony nomination — Best Featured Actress.
Jefferson Mays Joseph P. Kennedy Sr. / Multiple Plays six roles including the patriarch (flashback), a Secret Service agent, and, in a controversial casting note, Ronald Reagan in absentia (voice only).
Idina Menzel The American Dream Allegorical figure. Appears in the prologue, Act I finale, and Act II.
Song list — Original Broadway Production
Act One
1. Plains, Georgia Jimmy Carter, Ensemble Opens the show.
2. The Best of Us Ghost JFK, Bobby (Ghosts) First ghost appearance. The brothers argue about whether Ted deserves to be there, though it is not clear they reach a conclusion.
3. Thirteen Children Ted Kennedy A song about being the youngest. Mitchell has said this is the hardest 4 minutes in the show to perform.
4. The Convention Floor (Democracy Is a Beautiful Mess) Full Ensemble Showstopper, ~12 minutes. Choreographed by Bill T. Jones, it has three Tony nominations for this number alone.
5. You Should Have Run in '68 Ghost Bobby (Ghost), Ted Kennedy Bobby's most direct confrontation with Ted.
6. Chappaquiddick (The Water, The Dark) Ted Kennedy, Joan Kennedy Perhaps the most debated song in the production.
7. The Dream (First Statement) Ted Kennedy, The American Dream Act I finale, and Menzel's first full-length scene with Mitchell.
Act Two
8. January 20th Ted Kennedy, Ensemble Inauguration Day. Deliberately underscored, Mitchell sings almost the entire song a cappella. The orchestra enters one bar before the end.
9. The Oval Office at 3 A.M. Ted Kennedy Considered by some, alongside "Being Alive" and "Children Will Listen", as one of the great interior monologue songs in the Broadway canon.
10. What Dallas Took Ghost JFK (Ghost) JFK's only solo. Esparza has noted in interviews that he prepared for six months and still does not feel ready to perform it.
11. The Shape of Quiet Joan Kennedy McDonald's central aria. Performed entirely downstage for about eight minutes.
12. Rose (For My Mother) Ted Kennedy, Rose Kennedy (Voice) Rose Kennedy's voice is heard only; her lines were drawn from the memoir Times to Remember. The Kennedy family approved this use, and they asked that it not be changed.
13. All Three of Us Now Ghost JFK, Bobby, Ted Kennedy The three brothers together for the only time in the show.
14. The Dream (Final Statement) Ted Kennedy, The American Dream, Full Ensemble Finale, reprise and transformation of Act I closer. The last four bars are traditionally performed in silence; the orchestra stops, cast holds position, and the lights fade.
Awards & nominations
Award Year Category Recipient
WON Tony Award2005 Best MusicalProduction
WON Tony Award2005 Best Actor — MusicalBrian Stokes Mitchell
WON Tony Award2005 Best Featured Actress — MusicalAudra McDonald
WON Tony Award2005 Best ScoreAdam Guettel / Lynn Ahrens
WON Tony Award2005 Best ChoreographyBill T. Jones
NOM Tony Award2005 Best Direction — MusicalGeorge C. Wolfe
NOM Tony Award2005 Best Featured Actor — MusicalRaúl Esparza
NOM Tony Award2005 Best Featured Actress — MusicalTonya Pinkins
WON Drama Desk2005 Outstanding MusicalProduction
WON Grammy Award2005 Best Musical Theater AlbumOriginal Cast Recording
11 Tony nominations total · 5 wins. The cast recording (Sony Classical, 2004) entered the Billboard cast album chart at #1, the first original cast recording to do so since Rent in 1996.
Selected Press Review — The New York Times Ben Brantley · November 5, 2004

There is a moment at the end of the first act of The Dream Shall Never Die when Idina Menzel, playing an allegorical figure called simply The American Dream, turns to Brian Stokes Mitchell's Ted Kennedy and sings him a question he cannot answer. The question is, in essence: is this what you wanted? And Mitchell — whose face over the course of this evening does more dramatic work than most actors accomplish in entire careers — does not answer. Eventually, the lights go down, and the audience, on the night I attended, took approximately four seconds to begin applauding, and those four seconds were among the most charged I have spent in a Broadway theatre in many years of reviewing.

John Weidman's book and Adam Guettel's score are not entirely without flaw; the Chappaquiddick sequence is necessary but not fully solved, and the Iranian hostage crisis material in Act II feels slightly underdeveloped in service of the show's emotional larger concerns. But these are the notes you take on a production that is otherwise so good as to make you feel, briefly, that Broadway is capable of the thing it has always promised and only occasionally delivered: a musical that is genuinely about something, and that uses its form to say that thing in a way no other form could.

George C. Wolfe's direction is architectural; here, every scene is placed with a precision that makes the ghosts feel earned rather than theatrical. Bill T. Jones's choreography for "The Convention Floor" alone is worth the price of admission, though it would be a shame to leave before "All Three of Us Now" in Act II, which is as well-written a finale as this season is likely to produce. The claim in the program notes, that this is "the best musical about the best Kennedy brother", is eccentric, but possibly correct.

The New York Times · Ben Brantley · November 5, 2004 ★★★★★ (5/5)
User Reviews Submit your review →
"I came for the novelty, but there was so much more to it" ★★★★★
by TheaterRat_NYC · March 2, 2005 · Verified Ticket Holder
I won't pretend I bought my ticket because I expected high art. I bought it because the idea of a Kennedy presidency musical with actual ghosts sounded like the most gloriously theatrical premise imaginable and I wanted to see if they could pull it off. Turns out they can, and they do! "What Dallas Took" is the best thing Esparza has ever done and I have seen Esparza do a lot of things. "The Shape of Quiet" is genuinely a great song.
"The program notes are not wrong and that is unsettling" ★★★★½
by HistoryMajorNow · January 18, 2005 · Verified Ticket Holder
The program literally describes this as "the best musical about the best Kennedy brother" which I read before the show started and rolled my eyes at. I mean, come on! But by the end of Act II I was prepared to grant them, reluctantly, both claims. Bobby's ghost being more present and more accusatory than Jack's is a genuinely interesting historical argument made in musical form. "You Should Have Run in '68" has been living in my head for six weeks. I have listened to the cast recording more times than I have listened to most albums I actually own.
"Still thinking about it three months later." ★★★★
by SundayMatineeMan · February 28, 2005 · Verified Ticket Holder
Took off half a star because the Chappaquiddick number has not, in my view, been fully worked out dramatically and Weidman is usually better than that. Everything else: four stars minimum. The convention floor sequence is the most exciting fifteen minutes of choreography I have seen in a decade. Mitchell has never been better, and Menzel has never been more interesting. I saw the show with my father, who actually voted for Kennedy in 1980, and he did not speak in the car home. That is not a criticism, not at all...
