Sign In Link Your Subscription Newsletters
October 22, 2012 Subscribe
The New Yorker
est. 1925  ·  newyorker.com
News Politics Culture Television Books Humor Fiction Science Magazine Cartoons Archive
Television › Critic's Notebook

The Strange, Unlikely, Surprisingly Affecting World of The Commissioner

NBC's new workplace comedy about the office of the Major League Baseball Commissioner is funnier than it has any right to be, and that is almost entirely George W. Bush's fault.

By Emily Nussbaum | October 22, 2012 | Television
George W. Bush as Commissioner Dale Hargrove, in the series premiere of The Commissioner. Justin Sullivan / NBC / Getty Images
Show Information
TitleThe Commissioner
NetworkNBC
PremieredOct. 4, 2012
Day/TimeThursdays, 9:30 PM
CreatorMike Schur
Episodes3 aired (of 22)
CastBush, Aaliyah, Moore, Cho, O'Hara

There is a scene in the third episode of NBC's The Commissioner in which George W. Bush, playing Dale Hargrove, the newly appointed Commissioner of Major League Baseball, attempts to explain to his deputy press secretary — played, with a dry exasperation that is fast becoming the show's comic signature, by Aaliyah — why it is essential that the league's official position on the designated hitter rule be delivered in the form of a twelve-minute PowerPoint presentation. The presentation, we understand, was prepared entirely by Hargrove himself, over a weekend, and includes no fewer than four slides that are simply clip art of baseballs with motivational captions. Bush delivers this explanation with the serene confidence of a man who has never once entertained the possibility that the PowerPoint was a bad idea. Aaliyah listens, says nothing for a moment, and then says: "Wait, all this clip art. You put in the clip art yourself?" And Bush says: "I thought it added warmth." And the scene ends.

It is, as weird as it is to admit, a very good scene. It is, in the way of the best scenes in this genre, a scene that is funny on the surface and mildly devastating three layers down, and it could not have been performed by anyone else on television right now. This is the paradox at the center of The Commissioner, and the thing that makes it worth discussing at some length: the show is built around a piece of casting that is, by any conventional measure, preposterous, and the preposterous casting is almost entirely why it works.

"Bush is not doing an impression of anything. He is simply being something — a specific, fully inhabited kind of American man —, and the comedy arrives because the show has placed that man in a situation that reveals him perfectly."

— From this review

Some background, for those who have not been following the industry coverage. George W. Bush, the former part-owner of the Texas Rangers, whose baseball career was interrupted, as he likes to say, by "a couple of terms of public service", was announced as the show's lead in March, to reactions ranging from bewilderment to contempt to a bit of an intrigued wariness that has since resolved, three episodes in, into something approaching genuine enthusiasm. The show was created by Michael Schur, who spent the previous decade making The Office and Parks and Recreation, and who has described The Commissioner in interviews as "a show about what it means to love an institution more than the institution deserves." The Commissioner of Baseball, in this telling, is the perfect vessel for that theme: a man in charge of something that resists being managed, who approaches that unmanageability with undimmed enthusiasm, and who employs, around him, people who are variously more competent and more exhausted than he is.

What Schur has understood — and what the pilot's director, Miguel Arteta, executes with considerable precision —, is that Bush is not acting in the conventional sense. He is not doing an impression of anything, but he is simply being something: a specific, fully inhabited kind of American man, simultaneously incurious and warm, impervious to irony in a way that generates irony effortlessly, and possessed of a relationship to his own dignity that is entirely uncomplicated by self-awareness. The comedy arrives not because Bush is playing a fool, but because the show has placed that man in a situation that reveals him perfectly. Which is, in the end, what good casting has always done!

The ensemble

The show would not survive on Bush alone, and Schur is too experienced a showrunner to let it try. The ensemble is the thing, and it is, with one exception, exceptional. Aaliyah's Tanya Reed is the show's fulcrum: she is the person who sees Hargrove most clearly and cares about him anyway, which is both the most demanding role in this kind of comedy, and the one most likely to be underwritten. Notably, Schur has not underwritten it. Aaliyah, whose acting career was largely suspended during the years of her recording and touring commitments, brings to Tanya a quality that is hard to name but instantly recognizable: her alertness and patience that clearly belongs to someone who is very good at her job, and has accepted that this will not always be appreciated. Her scenes with Bush have an unusual texture; they feel, at moments, less like performance than like two people genuinely trying to figure each other out, and the show is wisest when it stays in that register.

Trevor Moore, as the office's director of community outreach, is given the show's most overtly comic material and handles it with the controlled mania that fans of his earlier work will recognize immediately. His character, Chip Heller, a former minor-league pitcher whose career ended under circumstances the show has so far declined to specify, is the ensemble's wild card, and Moore plays the wildness with just enough precision that keeps the character from tipping into caricature. A recurring bit in which Chip explains his community outreach strategy using terminology that is transparently borrowed from military operations manuals should not be as funny as it is. It is quite funny.

Margaret Cho, as the Commissioner's chief legal counsel, and Catherine O'Hara, as his veteran executive assistant, round out the core group. Cho is, at present, playing a character who could belong to any number of shows in this genre: the competent professional who exists primarily to explain to the protagonist why his latest plan is inadvisable. And yet, the specificity of her delivery suggests that Schur has more in mind. O'Hara, for her part, is doing something that requires no development: she is being Catherine O'Hara, which is to say she is the funniest person in any scene she occupies, deployed with the controlled economy of a resource that the show has correctly identified as finite and irreplaceable.

Principal Cast
George W. Bush
Dale Hargrove, MLB Commissioner
Former co-owner, Texas Rangers; first acting role
Aaliyah
Tanya Reed, Deputy Press Secretary
Three-time Grammy winner; Romeo Must Die (2000)
Trevor Moore
Chip Heller, Director of Community Outreach
Co-creator, The Whitest Kids U' Know
Margaret Cho
Sandra Lim, Chief Legal Counsel
Stand-up comedian; All-American Girl (1994)
Catherine O'Hara
Dolores Pryce, Executive Assistant
Schitt's Creek, Best in Show; seventeen Emmys
Craig Robinson
Marcus Webb, Director of Baseball Operations
The Office (2005–13)
What the show is actually about

It would be easy, and not entirely wrong, to read The Commissioner as a piece of political rehabilitation. Bush, who left his second term as Texas Governor to return full-time to his baseball interests, has spent the subsequent decade largely out of public life, and there is, in the casting, an obvious opportunity for the kind of soft re-introduction that celebrity culture periodically offers to public figures whose image has calcified in unhelpful directions. Schur, in interviews, has denied this framing with sufficient enthusiasm to suggest he is at least aware of it. "Dale Hargrove is not George W. Bush," he told the Times in September. "Dale Hargrove is a fictional character, who happens to be played by a real person, who happens to have certain qualities that illuminate the character. That's what casting is."

This is, as a formulation, somewhat too neat. But it is not wrong, and the show earns it, because Schur has been careful to make Hargrove a character whose limitations are structural rather than personal — he is not stupid, not cruel, not corrupt; he is simply a man who has been given authority over something he loves without being given the ability to understand it —, and because the show distributes sympathy with an even hand. The other characters are not smarter than Hargrove in ways that diminish him; they are smarter than him in ways that make them tired, and the show treats that tiredness as its own kind of subject. Tanya Reed did not get this job in order to spend her afternoons talking a former governor out of announcing the league's designated hitter policy via PowerPoint. She is very good at this job anyway! That gap, between what a person intended for their life and what their life has turned out to require, is where The Commissioner, at its best, lives.

Three episodes in, the show has not yet found the consistency of tone that its best moments suggest it is capable of. The network-comedy machinery occasionally asserts itself in ways that Schur's better instincts resist: a subplot in the second episode involving a fictional player's contract dispute is resolved with a tidiness that the show's more interesting scenes specifically decline to offer. But the best of what The Commissioner can do — Bush and Aaliyah in a conference room, talking at cross-purposes about something that is ostensibly about baseball, and is actually about something else entirely —, well, is very good indeed. Maybe the designated hitter PowerPoint, it turns out, was the right thing to lead with.

Topics: Television The Commissioner NBC George W. Bush Aaliyah Mike Schur Workplace Comedy Baseball

` }, { id: "ned", condition: (ev) => ev > 269 && ev <= 332 && cynicism >= 3, image: "https://i.pinimg.com/1200x/1e/05/02/1e0502a1015883af05cac46f84537d4b.jpg", html: `
NED'S DECLASSIFIED SCHOOL SURVIVAL GUIDE
FIRST DRAFT - Episode 112-a: "Visits & Emergencies"
INT. POLK MIDDLE SCHOOL HALLWAY - DAY
NED BIGBY walks down the hallway holding a pathetic, shriveled potted plant. The school looks completely different. There are no lights on, students are reading by candlelight, and a goat is eating a math textbook near the water fountain.
FREEZE FRAME. The camera zooms in on Ned's stressed face.
NED
(V.O. / to camera)
(Tip number 44:) When the actual President of the United States visits your school for Earth Day, make sure your science project isn't a plastic cup full of dead dirt. And uh... definitely don't let Vice Principal Crubbs go "Zero Carbon."
RESUME ACTION. [BOING SOUND]
VICE PRINCIPAL CRUBBS emerges from the shadows wearing a burlap sack instead of a suit, holding a wooden club.
VP CRUBBS
Bigby! Are you breathing heavily? You're exhaling too much CO2! Breathe shallower... we have to win the Presidential Eco-Trophy!
Crubbs confiscates a kid's mechanical pencil, snaps it in half, and hands him a piece of charcoal. [WHIP CRACK SOUND] He disappears into a dark classroom.
MOZE walks up. She is effortlessly carrying a massive, beautifully constructed, fully operational miniature wind turbine.
MOZE
Hey, Ned. How's your photosynthesis project going? Mr. Sweeney said if you fail this, you're in summer school.
NED
It’s dead, Moze! The sun hasn't shined in here since Crubbs bricked up the windows to "conserve thermal mass"... whatever that means!
Suddenly, COOKIE bursts out of a janitor's closet. He is wearing a giant, clunky suit made entirely of rotting banana peels, eggshells, and a blinking car battery. He reeks. [SQUISHY FOOTSTEP SOUNDS]
COOKIE
Behold! The Cyborg-Composter 3000! I have synced my motherboard to the school's organic waste! I am... erm... absorbing the nutrients.
MOZE
(plugging her nose)
Cookie, you're leaking garbage juice.
COOKIE
It's called bio-sludge, Moze! And it's highly combustible...
Cookie presses a button on his chest. A small spark flies out, instantly igniting a trash can next to them. [FWOOSH SOUND]
COOKIE
Oops.
NED
Cookie, put it out! The President is going to be here any second!
CUT TO:
INT. SCHOOL ENTRANCE - CONTINUOUS
The double doors burst open. [GLORIOUS TRUMPET FANFARE]
PRESIDENT AL GORE strides into the hallway. He is wearing a sharp suit made of hemp, and a pair of Birkenstocks. He is flanked by two SECRET SERVICE AGENTS who are aggressively planting small pine trees as they walk.
AL GORE
(taking a deep breath)
Ah. I smell the future. And... burning plastic, too.
Ned sprints up to Gore, still holding his dead plant.
NED
Mr. President! I'm Ned Bigby. I-I need your help. My science project is dead, Mr. Sweeney is going to hold me back, and my friend over there is... well, I think he's not doing well!
Gore looks at the dead plant, then down the hall at Cookie, who is now flailing around as his suit starts beeping rapidly. [BEEP BEEP BEEP BEEP]
AL GORE
(deadpan, dramatic stare)
Huh. A compost bomb. You know, I warned Congress about this in '98, but they called me crazy. Secret Service, secure the lockbox.
An agent hands Gore a titanium briefcase. Gore sets it on the floor. He looks at Ned.
AL GORE
Ned, what you lack in botanical aptitude, you clearly make up for in desperation. We're going to save your friend. And then, we are going to ace your science class.
Gore opens the lockbox. Inside is a glowing, green, incredibly high-tech spray bottle.
AL GORE
I call it... the Kyoto Protocol.
Gordy the Janitor suddenly runs past them, diving onto the floor. [SLIDE WHISTLE SOUND]
GORDY
I got him! I got him! I finally caught the weasel!
Gordy holds up a struggling weasel. The weasel is holding a half-eaten hotdog.
AL GORE
(gasping)
Great Scott! Is that the North American Middle School Weasel? Put him down immediately! He is the keystone species of this school! And many schools, actually.
GORDY
But he owes me five bucks!
Gore snaps his fingers. A Secret Service agent hands Gordy a five-dollar bill made of recycled algae. Gordy shrugs, seemingly satisfied, drops the weasel, and walks away. The weasel salutes Gore and scurries into a vent.
COOKIE
(off-screen, screaming)
NED! THE METHANE LEVELS ARE CRITICAL! I'M GOING TO DIE!
Cookie is now covered in rapidly growing vines. The car battery on his chest is glowing red hot. MR. SWEENEY steps out of his classroom, holding a clipboard.
MR. SWEENEY
Mr. Bigby! Is your project ready for grading? Because it appears your... uh, associate is about to detonate a rather hazardous organic payload.
Al Gore steps in front of Ned, shielding him. He stares Mr. Sweeney down; the tension is palpable. [WESTERN STANDOFF MUSIC PLAYS]
AL GORE
Sweeney.
MR. SWEENEY
Mr. President. I see you're still interfering in middle school academia. Typical...
AL GORE
I'm protecting the biosphere. Ned's project isn't this dead plant, more than that. His project... is action.
Gore aims the "Kyoto Protocol" spray bottle at Cookie and fires a massive blast of blue foam. [FSHHHHHHHH SOUND] Cookie is instantly frozen in a block of sparkling, surprisingly eco-friendly foam.
COOKIE
(muffled inside the foam)
I feel... so... sustainable...
Gore turns to Sweeney, holding up his hands.
AL GORE
We have neutralized the methane threat, restored the local habitat, and successfully reduced this school's carbon footprint to zero. I believe that earns Mr. Bigby an 'A'.
Sweeney marks his clipboard with his pen. [DING SOUND]
MR. SWEENEY
An 'A-minus'. This foam smells like patchouli.
Sweeney retreats into his classroom. Ned cheers and high-fives Moze. Ned turns to Al Gore.
NED
Mr. President, how can I ever thank you?
AL GORE
Don't thank me, Ned. Thank the internet. Which I invented, of course. Now, I must go. The ice caps are calling.
Gore presses a button on his belt. A pair of solar-powered helicopter blades deploy from his suit jacket. With a quiet hum, he slowly ascends through a broken ceiling tile and disappears. [ANGELIC CHORUS SOUND]
MOZE
Well, I guess that happened.
Ned looks at the camera, smiling.
NED
(Tip number 45:) Well, just remember... if you can't save your grades... let the Leader of the Free World do it for you.
[UPBEAT OUTRO MUSIC PLAYS] IRIS OUT.
` }, // generic slides { id: "madeleine", condition: (ev, opponentEVs) => ev < 270 && opponentEVs < 270, image: "https://file.garden/aNtAfG887DiA_7lO/TML_2000/madeleine.jpg", html: `

Welcome to Billy's Block — Episode 1.07: "Big Feelings and Big Flavors"

[The scene opens on a warm, brightly lit living room with a comfortable yellow sofa, a miniature traffic light, and a large oak bookshelf. A soft, soothing saxophone melody plays in the background.]

[The front door opens. Bill Clinton enters, sporting a gentle smile, silver hair perfectly coiffed, wearing a zip-up navy blue cardigan. He waves at the camera.]

"Hello, neighbor. It's so good to see you today. I was just out taking a walk, and you know, I was thinking about you."

[He walks over to a small wooden bench, sits down, and begins untying his sneakers. He swaps them out for a pair of polished, velvet loafers.]

"Did you have a hard day at kindergarten today? Did somebody take a toy that you were playing with, or maybe... maybe you just felt a little overwhelmed by all the noise on the playground?"

[He looks directly into the camera, with his eyes narrowing slightly in deep, empathetic sincerity. He bites his lower lip.]

"I feel your pain. I really do. It's not easy being small in a world that moves so fast. Sometimes, when my feelings get too big, or when the polls in the states are looking a little tight, I like to go over to the Magic Kitchen and make something special to help me unwind. Would you like to come with me?"

[The camera pans, following him as he walks across the colorful set to a beautifully polished mahogany wet bar, complete with brass rails and crystal decanters. It is eye-level for a toddler, but fully stocked with premium, top-shelf liquor.]

"Here we are. Now, today, boys and girls, we’re going to learn about a very special drink. It's called a Manhattan. Can you say 'Manhattan'?"

[A beat of silence for the children at home to respond.]

"I knew you could. Now, the Manhattan was invented a long, long time ago. Some folks say it was at the Manhattan Club in New York City back in the 1870s. And the secret to a good Manhattan is something called balance. Just like how you have to balance when you ride your tricycle, we have to balance our ingredients."

[He reaches under the counter and pulls out a heavy, crystal mixing glass.]

"First, we need our base spirit. A lot of people will tell you that a bourbon makes a sweeter, more approachable Manhattan. And you know, I believe in choices. There is nothing wrong with bourbon! But if you want a true, classic profile... the kind that makes you want to sit on a porch in Arkansas and just watch the sun go down... you need a good Rye whiskey."

[He holds up a bottle of Michter's US*1 Straight Rye, pointing to the label with a soft smile.]

"Rye has a spicy character, just like your friend Jimmy when he doesn’t want to share his blocks. We’re going to pour two full ounces. We count to four in our heads while we pour. One, two, three, four. Good job."

[He pours the amber liquid into the mixing glass with ease.]

"Next, we need sweet vermouth. Now, listen to me very carefully, neighbors."

[Bill leans over the bar, looking straight into the lens, his tone turning incredibly serious.]

"Do not use the cheap vermouth that has been sitting in the back of your parents' pantry since 1998. Vermouth is a fortified wine, boys and girls. It oxidizes, it goes bad. You must keep it in the refrigerator. I cannot stress this enough. If you use oxidized vermouth, you are compromising your future. We’re going to use exactly one ounce of Carpano Antica Formula. It has lovely notes of vanilla and star anise."

[He measures the dark vermouth and adds it to the glass.]

"Now, for the bitters. Can you say 'Angostura'?"

[Beat.]

"That's wonderful. Bitters are like the rules your teachers give you. A couple of dashes keep everything together and give our lives structure. Too much, and it ruins the whole experience and makes you bitter, too. Just two dashes. Dash, dash. Look at that!"

[He uses a pair of silver tongs to delicately place a large, perfectly clear ice cube into the glass.]

"Now we stir. We never, ever shake a Manhattan. Does anybody know why? It’s because shaking aerates the drink and bruises the spirit. And we never want to bruise our spirits, do we? No, we don't. We want to treat our spirits with kindness, and dignity, and respect."

[He stirs the drink with a long, elegant bar spoon, as ice clinks softly against the glass in a hypnotic rhythm. He stirs for exactly thirty seconds, smiling the entire time.]

"Finally, we strain it into our chilled coupe glass. And we garnish. Now, I see a lot of folks using those bright red, neon cherries you put on an ice cream sundae. Oh, no. Heck no. Not in this neighborhood. We use a Luxardo cherry. It comes all the way from Italy. It’s dark, and rich, and it reminds us that the world is a big, beautiful place full of wonderful things."

[He drops the dark, syrupy cherry into the glass. He picks it up by the stem, admiring the deep, ruby-red color against the studio lights.]

"Mmmm. Look at that. Let's take a little sip, shall we?"

[He takes a slow, measured sip. His eyes close, his shoulders drop, and he lets out a deep, contented sigh. For a moment, he seems to completely forget he is hosting a children's television program.]

"Lord have mercy. That is a strong economy right there. The rye cuts right through the sweetness, just perfectly..."

[He clears his throat, remembering where he is, and gives the camera one last, charming smile.]

"You know, the world can be a scary place sometimes. But as long as you have good friends, a kind heart, and the basic motor skills required to properly balance a 2-to-1 ratio of whiskey to vermouth, I promise you... tomorrow is going to be a brighter day."

[He raises his glass to the camera as the soft saxophone music swells again.]

"I'm proud of you, neighbor. Cheers."

[Fade to black. Text on screen: BILLY'S BLOCK IS MADE POSSIBLE BY VIEWERS LIKE YOU. AND THE DISTILLERS AT WILD TURKEY.]

` }, { id: "aliens", condition: (ev) => ev >= 146 && ev < 208, image: "https://file.garden/aNtAfG887DiA_7lO/TML_2000/lonely.jpg", html: `
Archive of Our Own beta
Hi, bobbydorpz!  |  Post  |  Log Out

The Cue, the Cringe, and the Conan

panopticomar
Rating:
Teen And Up Audiences
Archive Warning:
No Archive Warnings Apply
Fandom:
Celebrity RPF, Late Night Television RPF
Relationships:
Conan O'Brien & Jimmy Fallon, Jimmy Fallon & His Dignity (One-Sided)
Characters:
Conan O'Brien, Jimmy Fallon, Other Characters
Additional Tags:
Snooker, Bars & Pubs, Rivalry, Noir, Humiliation (Comedic), Jimmy Fallon Needs A Hug, Conan Being Insufferable", no beta we die like the nbc ten o'clock slot, crack treated very seriously, buster keaton-esque slapstick comedy, someone get this man a sponsor
Stats:
Published: 2024-03-04  |  Words: 1,552  |  Chapters: 1/1  |  Kudos: 612  |  Bookmarks: 88  |  Hits: 9,441
Notes

okay so funny story. my friend alice sent me a clip of conan doing his podcast thing or whatever at 11pm and i said "i bet jimmy fallon is somewhere having a normal one" and then this happened. three hours later, on a tuesday. oh my god what the fuck have i done

i do not know how snooker works. i watched half a YouTube video and that was about the version of snooker you could play in barnyard (2005), y'know that game. i think i got the gist. please do not @ me about the rules, this is a FEELINGS document not a SPORTS document

title is a play on "the good the bad and the ugly" which felt right. conan is the good (at snooker). jimmy is the ugly (at snooker). there is no bad, only increasing misery, especially on my end

Summary

It’s a Wednesday night and Jimmy Fallon just wants one thing: a quiet game of snooker in a bar no one knows about, a cold drink at his elbow, and zero Conans.

He gets two out of three.

The bar was called The Ivory and it smelled a lot like old wood. It was after 11 PM on a weeknight in midtown Manhattan, and everyone who mattered had already gone home. Jimmy Fallon liked it precisely for this reason. Nobody came here to be seen, you came here to not be seen. You came here, specifically, to unwind and play snooker in peace without the crushing weight of life breathing down your neck.

He pushed open the door. The bartender, a heavyset man named Gus who had probably witnessed several federal crimes and chosen not to comment, gave him a nod. Jimmy nodded back. This was their entire relationship; it was perfect.

Jimmy unbuttoned his coat, turned toward the snooker table in the back, and stopped.

There, bent over the green felt like a praying mantis in a sport coat, lining up what appeared to be a very confident shot on the red ball in the far pocket, was Conan O’Brien.

Six foot four, hair like a tangerine caught in a wind tunnel, he was grinning to himself in the dim light with the self-satisfied air of a man who had recently launched a wildly successful podcast, completed a world tour, and won some kind of indefinable cultural war that Jimmy hadn’t even known they were fighting.

The cue cracked, and the red ball dropped cleanly into the pocket.

“Oh, come on,” Jimmy said, out loud, to no one. Not this bitch.

Conan turned. His face lit up with some delight. He was, of course, a predator spotting something small and flailingly alive. “Jimmy! Hey! This is crazy, I had no idea you came here.”

“I come here specifically because… uh, no one I know comes here.”

“And yet.” Conan spread his arms, gesturing to the full absurdity of his own presence. “Here I am.”

Jimmy looked at Gus. Gus was already polishing a glass, eyes fixed on the middle distance, as a man who had made peace with the indifference of the universe. No help there. Bastard.

“Fine,” Jimmy said, dropping his coat on a barstool. “Fine. I’ll play.”

“I was already playing against myself.”

“And losing, presumably.”

“Actually, winning. I’m very good at snooker, you know.”

“No, I don’t. Nobody is good at snooker. Snooker is a game you endure.”

Conan handed him a cue, as if he were presenting Jimmy a sword. “Not the way I play it.”

Jimmy took it. It was, surprisingly, heavier than he remembered.

The first thing that became apparent to Jimmy, within approximately four minutes, was that Conan O’Brien was genuinely, distressingly good at snooker. Not in a showy way. In a worse way, actually; who could’ve thought this guy had spent a non-trivial portion of his adult life thinking about angles!? He moved around the table with his long limbs folded in on themselves, assessed each shot with the focused stillness of a heron, and then executed it flawlessly. How? He made the balls scatter like they’d personally offended him…

“You’ve been practicing,” Jimmy said, trying to keep the accusation out of his voice.

“A little.” Conan straightened up. “I got really into it during the… well, you know. The period.”

“We all had a period.”

“Mine involved snooker, apparently.”

“Mmph. Mine involved sourdough.”

Conan looked at him with something approaching sympathy. “That tracks.”

Jimmy leaned over his shot. Ah… the cue ball… the pink… a clear line. He breathed out slowly, the way he’d seen people do in movies. This would work. He pulled back, and he shot.

The cue ball drifted left, kissed the cushion, and rolled to a gentle, meaningless stop in the middle of the table, accomplishing absolutely nothing.

“Ah,” said Conan.

“Don’t.”

“I didn’t say anything.”

“You were thinking something.”

“I was thinking,” Conan said, stepping up to take his turn, “’what a fascinating choice of shot.’ That’s all.” He sank the pink without looking up. “Out of curiosity, what were you going for?”

“The pink.”

“Right, right.” Another ball dropped. “The pink that is now, let’s see,” he tilted his head, “roughly forty-seven degrees from where you left the cue ball.”

“Snooker is a game of imperfection.”

“It really isn’t.”

By the midpoint of the game, two things had happened, none of them particularly good: not only had Conan built a lead so large that Jimmy’s calculator app described as “significant,” but the atmosphere at The Ivory had turned into a slow-motion disaster that Gus was observing with some worry. He was merely a man watching a documentary about wildebeests. Jimmy was fucked.

“You know what your problem is,” Conan said, during Jimmy’s fourth consecutive miss, “is you’re playing to the crowd.”

“But there’s no crowd.”

“There’s Gus!”

Gus did not react.

“I’m not playing to Gus.” Jimmy gestured at himself with the cue, nearly taking out a barstool. “I’m just— look, I’m recalibrating. I’m in the process of recalibrating.”

“That’s what NBC said about their Thursday lineup,” Conan observed pleasantly, “for about six years. Look how that ended up.”

“Okay, wow.”

“Too soon?”

“It’s been decades, jeez.”

“The wounds of network television do not heal on a normal human timeline,” Conan said solemnly, and sank the black ball. “I would know. I thought you did too.”

Jimmy opened his mouth, found the full weight of everything he could possibly say about that particular topic, and made a tactical decision to close it again. No, he was not going to do this tonight. He picked up his drink instead. The ice had melted; even the ice, apparently, had given up.

“You’re enjoying this,” he said.

“I’m not not enjoying it.” Conan circled the table, lining up his next shot, and had the absolute audacity to look thoughtful about it. “Jimmy… I want you to know I have genuine affection for you. I think you’re a talented guy, really. You’re very likeable, you have a nice laugh.”

“But?”

“But you are,” Conan said carefully, “playing snooker like you’re a goddamn moron. It’s like you’ve never even seen a snooker table. I could get a golden retriever to take better shots than you do, that’s how bad things are.”

Jimmy stared at him.

And so Conan sank the shot. “That was mean, sorry. That was too far.”

“…a little bit.”

“But the golden retriever is a very nice dog.”

“That doesn’t help.”

The final frames were not, strictly speaking, watchable. Jimmy’s cue slipped on his third-to-last shot and sent the white ball lurching sideways into the cushion hard enough to make Gus look up for the first time in forty minutes. He lost possession on the resulting foul. Conan, magnanimously, did not comment. God, this was somehow worse than if he had. Jimmy had been fucked in so many different ways that his name would be on the definition list, just right below Homer Simpson’s name. He, too, had been fucked, but not like this.

In his last shot of the night, facing an almost comically impossible angle, something seemed to click. Jimmy took a breath. Hold on, hold on, maybe… I can do this. And so he blocked out the bar, blocked out the smell of old wood and failure, and blocked out Conan’s patient, six-foot-four silhouette leaning against the far wall. He lined up, he committed, and then, he shot.

The cue ball sailed into the pocket.

It was his own ball. Into his own pocket. A scratch, a foul! Literally the worst thing you can do in snooker! Short of setting the table on fire, naturally.

There was a long silence.

“So that’s game,” Conan said softly.

“That’s game,” Jimmy agreed.

“You know, in certain circles, scratching on the—”

“If you finish that sentence,” Jimmy said, “I will fucking end you.”

Conan closed his mouth. There was a smile at the corner of it that would haunt Jimmy Fallon for no fewer than three subsequent weeks.

At the bar, waiting for his coat, Jimmy stared into his glass.

“How’s the podcast going,” he said, flatly, not quite a question.

“Great,” said Conan. “Really great. We just passed a hundred million downloads.”

“That’s great.”

“Thank you. The show going well?”

“The show is great.”

“Good. That’s good.”

Gus placed a receipt in front of Jimmy. Jimmy stared at it.

“Did you order anything?” he asked Conan.

“Water.”

The receipt was for Jimmy’s three drinks and Conan’s water, which was free, and had been charged to Jimmy somehow. He did not question this; turns out some forces in the universe simply operated on their own logic.

He paid. Jimmy picked up his coat, and turned toward the door.

“Same time next week?” Conan called after him.

Jimmy paused with his hand on the door. Outside, as midtown Manhattan shimmered in the rain, wet and enormous, he briefly saw as a cab splashed through a puddle. There was also this weird pigeon that made a noise like a broken accordion.

“No,” he said.

“Wednesday’s good for me.”

“No.”

“I’ll be here.”

Jimmy walked out into the rain, and the door swung shut behind him. Inside, very faintly, he could hear the crack of a cue ball doing exactly what it was supposed to do on the very first try.

Fuck.

He hailed a cab. He did not look back. He had, he decided, recalibrated enough for one evening.

End Notes

the receipt thing happened to me at a bar once and i've never recovered ngl. gus is based on no one btw. in fact, gus *is* everyone.

anyway conan would absolutely be weirdly good at snooker. this is not up for debate, it's something about the arms. you know what i mean. you KNOW.

jimmy will be back next wednesday probably. he will lose again. the universe is consistent if nothing else

♥ doctorBobert, SenorLovesexy, notericakane, number1dodiker, miscellaneousUnc, and 607 others left kudos on this work!

`, song: { title: "Monkey Business", artist: "Michael Jackson", cover: "https://images.genius.com/1883d45972818169043843c95ba78f12.600x600x1.png", url: ["LpL0gzU2giw", "zsG5EhvnvJs"] } }, { id: "sombr", condition: (ev) => ev > 332 && ev <= 394, image: "https://file.garden/aNtAfG887DiA_7lO/TML_2000/sombr.jpg", html: `
BBC
Home News Sport iPlayer Sounds Bitesize CBBC CBeebies Weather More ▾
Notifications Sign In
BBC
iPlayer
Home TV Guide Programmes Channels Categories
BBC Four
Available for 29 days
Episode 1 · 58 mins
BBC BBC FOUR

Krombo: On the
Streets of Chicago

Episode 1 · "The Mayor and I"

Presenter Krombopulos Michael visits Chicago to explore the presidency of Rahm Emanuel. He eats a corn dog, he interviews some experts, and things develop from there.

Channel: BBC Four
Year: 2023
Duration: 58 mins per episode
Series: 1 (4 episodes)

Krombo: On the
Streets of Chicago

Comedy Documentary Satire Current Affairs BBC Four

In the tradition of the great outsider documentary, Krombo: On the Streets of Chicago follows television presenter Krombopulos Michael as he sets out to understand the political legacy of President Rahm Emanuel through conversations with academics, political scientists, former staffers, and assorted members of the Chicago public, most of whom immediately regret agreeing to be filmed. Over four episodes, Krombopulos crisscrosses the city in search of answers to questions that range from the genuinely probing to the cosmically wrongheaded, while pausing regularly to purchase and consume food from whatever street vendor happens to be nearby.

Krombopulos Michael first came to public attention performing impersonations of then-President John McCain at regional comedy nights throughout 2008 and 2009.

"Oh, I just love meeting people. I love learning things. And I love corn dogs. Honestly, if I could do all three at the same time, every day, I think I'd be satisfied."
— Krombopulos Michael, Series 1 Press Materials
Episodes — Series 1 All episodes →
58:12
Episode 1 · Now Playing

The Mayor and I

Krombopulos arrives in Chicago and asks a political science professor at the University of Chicago whether President Emanuel "used to be in a different job before, or has it always been this." He then eats a corn dog on the steps of the Richard J. Daley Center while conducting a follow-up interview with a constitutional law expert who has given their name as "Nah." Krombopulos thanks Nah warmly for their time.

58 mins 29 days left First shown: 14 Feb 2023
56:44
Episode 2

The Economy, or Whatever That Is

Krombopulos investigates Emanuel's economic record with the help of a senior fellow at the Brookings Institution, this time with two corn dogs (mustard and plain), and an interview with a retired city alderman who has given their contact name as "If You Talk To Me Again I'll Beat Your Ass Up." Krombopulos opens the interview by asking: "So, If You Talk To Me Again I'll Beat Your Ass Up — may I call you If? — what do you make of the infrastructure bill?"

56 mins 29 days left First shown: 21 Feb 2023
61:02
Episode 3

Foreign Policy: A Long Walk

Attempting to understand the Emanuel administration's foreign policy, Krombopulos walks from the Loop to the lakefront while interviewing a former State Department official and consuming what the production team estimates is his fourteenth corn dog of the shoot. At one point he asks whether the President has ever considered "just not doing foreign policy at all, and seeing how that goes", nothing it went very well for his predecessor Buddy Cianci; he makes no comment.

61 mins 29 days left First shown: 28 Feb 2023
63:18
Episode 4 · Series Finale

The Rahm Legacy

In the final episode, Krombopulos attempts to synthesise everything he has learned into a concluding assessment of the Emanuel legacy. He is assisted in this by a panel discussion featuring scholars whose names he reads from his clipboard as "Get Away From Me," "Absolutely Not," and "Why Is He Still Here." He thanks them all individually. He then eats a corn dog on the shore of Lake Michigan, facing the water, in silence, for approximately three and a half minutes.

63 mins Expires in 2 days First shown: 7 Mar 2023
Cast & crew
PresenterShane Boose as Krombopulos Michael
Series DirectorCharlie Russell also director, Cunk on Earth
WritersShane Boose, Jason Hazeley, Joel Morris
Executive ProducersAnnabel Jones, Charlie Brooker
Production CompanyHouse of Tomorrow / BBC Studios
Commissioning EditorBBC Four
Press & critical reception
"Perhaps the most formally original comedy the BBC has commissioned in a decade. Miraculously, whatever is going on here sustains four hours of television easily, and Michael's interpretation in the show is, without exaggeration, devastating."
★★★★★ The Guardian · Stuart Heritage
"Boose's performance is a masterclass in committed wrongheadedness, for the lack of a better word. He plays Krombopulos as someone who is entirely present in every conversation, and entirely incorrect about everything he takes from it, and this combination turns out to be as lethal a satirical tool as anything that has been applied to political documentary since The Thick of It. The interview with 'If' in Episode 2 alone is worth the licence fee."
★★★★½ The Times · Carol Midgley
"There is a moment in Episode 4 when Krombopulos, having thanked the panellists 'Get Away From Me,' 'Absolutely Not,' and 'Why Is He Still Here' by name, among others, turns to the camera and says, with absolute sincerity, 'I feel like I've really learned something today.' The audience laugh, and then you think about it for a second. Then you stop laughing, and then you sit very quietly for a while wondering what the hell is going on here."
★★★★ Radio Times · Alison Graham
"Won the BAFTA for Best Comedy Programme 2024, and Won the BAFTA for Best Editing. Also nominated for Best Factual Entertainment (the BBC checked whether this was technically possible; it was). The corn dog vendor credited as 'I Will End You' has been nominated for a BAFTA for Best Supporting Performance. Curiously, this is apparently the first time a person identified only as 'I Will End You' has received a BAFTA nomination, and the BAFTA committee has confirmed they intend to read the name out in full."
Source: BAFTA Awards Database, 2024
Programme Info

`, song: { title: "Too Close to the Sun", artist: "Alan Parsons", cover: "https://i.discogs.com/E_PFhAdbnQbvA7sUGT8kb-KgL4oCiuEtwSlRxkMEQkg/rs:fit/g:sm/q:90/h:600/w:589/czM6Ly9kaXNjb2dz/LWRhdGFiYXNlLWlt/YWdlcy9SLTE1ODM2/NjQtMTcxNjA1MjUz/MS01MDc2LmpwZWc.jpeg", url: ["jZwVjrjj9J0", "wH_cnIzrExI"] } }, { id: "hehheh", condition: (ev) => ev >= 64 && ev < 146, image: "https://i.pinimg.com/1200x/7a/33/f6/7a33f6071eeeee2925ed3f509d89e1b8.jpg", html: `

unaccounted for

It was almost one in the morning and the television was on, which was the same as every other night, except that they weren’t really watching it.

The curtains were drawn. In fact They had been drawn for eleven days. This had started as Butt-Head’s idea — “if they can’t see us, they can’t, like, draft us” — and Beavis had agreed immediately, finding this reasoning airtight. Since then, they had been in their living room.

On the television, a man in a suit was talking about something. Of course, neither of them was listening to the man in the suit.

“We should eat something,” said Beavis.

“Yeah,” said Butt-Head.

Neither of them moved. This was also the same as every other night.

After a while Beavis said, “I could go check if there’s anything.”

“Like what.”

“I don’t know. Stuff.”

“There’s no stuff.”

“There might be stuff.”

Butt-Head considered this for a moment, given this was a logistical question. “There’s not.”

But Beavis got up anyway. He went to the kitchen, opened the refrigerator and stood in front of it for a while with the door open, the cold light falling out across the linoleum. There was a jar of something that had been there since before any of this started, a half-empty bottle of ketchup, and one egg that had been kind of lonely there. He closed the refrigerator. He opened it again, as though giving it a second chance. Sighing, he closed it.

He found, in the back of a cabinet, a can of SpaghettiOs with no pull tab, and he spent a while looking for the can opener, before remembering he’d seen it last Tuesday under the couch for reasons he could no longer account for. Thus, Beavis retrieved it, and he opened the can. He did not heat the SpaghettiOs because he thought the idea of microwaving them seemed like a lot right now, and so he poured half into a bowl, brought the can into the living room and set it on the couch between them.

“SpaghettiOs,” he said.

“Cool,” said Butt-Head.

· · ·

They ate with spoons from opposite ends of the arrangement, the bowl and the can, the television murmuring at them about something. At one point the man in the suit said something about troop movements and Butt-Head changed the channel, reflexively, as if he was pulling his hand from something hot. He landed on an infomercial, and left it there.

“Heh,” said Beavis, after a while. “This guy’s selling, like, a mop.”

“That mop sucks,” said Butt-Head.

“Yeah,” said Beavis. “Heh heh. It really sucks.”

This produced, between them, something that was almost like the old rhythm. Butt-Head made a sound in the back of his throat that was adjacent to laughing, Beavis grinned at the television, and for a moment the room felt nearly normal, nearly the same as it had always been, Saturday night on the couch with nothing to do, nowhere to be, and no reason in the world to think about anything they didn’t want to think about.

Then the infomercial ended and they cut to news, just for a second before Butt-Head could change it, and there were the words across the bottom of the screen… coalition forces, Baghdad, casualties… and Butt-Head changed it again, fast, and found static, and left it on static.

The static hummed at them.

Beavis looked at the empty SpaghettiOs can. He picked it up, looked into it and put it back down.

“Hey Butt-Head,” he said.

“What.”

“Do you think they’re, like… do you think they know where we live?”

Butt-Head didn’t answer right away. On the screen the static shifted, nothing more than a slow meaningless churn. “I don’t know,” he said, which for Butt-Head was a strange answer. Usually he had an answer.

“Because like,” said Beavis, “we got that letter.”

“We’re not talking about the letter.”

“I know, but—”

“We’re not talking about it, Beavis.”

Beavis was quiet. He pulled one knee up on the couch, looked at the static, and chewed the inside of his cheek, which he did sometimes when he was thinking about something he wasn’t sure he was allowed to think about.

· · ·

That letter had come eight days ago. It had come in an official-looking envelope that Butt-Head had opened, read and then put face-down on the kitchen table. And then Beavis had picked it up and read it, and then they had both stood in the kitchen for a while not saying anything. Then Butt-Head had said they should close the curtains. So they had closed the curtains.

They had not talked about the letter since. But it was in the kitchen, still face-down on the table, and they both knew it was there, and they walked around it the way as if it were something on the floor in the dark that they didn’t want to step on.

On the television the static continued. Outside, a car passed, and both of them went very still until the headlights moved off the curtains and the engine sound faded. Then they relaxed, incrementally, without acknowledging that they had tensed.

“I don’t want to go,” said Beavis. He said it very quietly, not like he was making a statement, more like he was testing whether the words were real by saying them out loud. “Like. I really, really don’t want to go.”

Butt-Head looked at the television. “Yeah,” he said.

“Do you think it’d be… I mean, do you think guys like us even—”

“I said we’re not talking about it.”

“I know.” Beavis picked at the label on an empty can. “I know, I just—”

“Beavis.”

“What.”

Butt-Head didn’t answer. He just sat there with his arms crossed, trying to work something out that he didn’t have the vocabulary for. Beavis looked at him for a moment and then looked away.

It occurred to Beavis, sitting there, that he could not actually remember a time before this couch. Not in a, uh, sentimental way, not like he was being poetic about it; he genuinely could not locate, in his memory, a version of his life that wasn’t organized around this room, this television, and this particular configuration of the two of them. They had always been here. This was the whole shape of things! He had never thought about it before because there had never been a reason to, and now there was a reason, and thinking about it made something go strange and hollow somewhere in the middle of his chest.

“Hey Butt-Head,” he said.

“What.”

“If we, like…” He stopped. He tried again. “If they make us go. Like, if we have to. Would we be, like… together? Or do they, like, split you up?”

Butt-Head was quiet for a long time. Long enough that Beavis thought he wasn’t going to answer.

Then he said, “I don’t know.”

And Beavis nodded, slowly, like that was the answer he’d expected, and looked back at the television, and the static went on and on.

· · ·

At some point the infomercial came back on. The man was still selling the mop, apparently. Neither of them said anything about it this time.

Beavis lay down on his side on the couch, facing the television, and Butt-Head stayed sitting the way he always sat, where the lamp in the corner made its small orange light, and the room held them the way it always had. The SpaghettiOs can sat where Beavis had left it. And outside, nothing moved.

After a long while Butt-Head said, without looking away from the screen, “You should sleep.”

“I’m not tired,” said Beavis, which was not true.

“Yeah you are.”

Beavis didn’t argue. He pulled the couch cushion further under his head and looked at the television with his eyes already going heavy at the edges. The mop man was demonstrating something enthusiastically. But it didn’t seem to matter what.

“Hey Butt-Head,” he said, very quietly now.

“What.”

“This sucks.”

Butt-Head looked at the curtains. He looked at the empty cans. He then looked at the letter-shaped absence in the kitchen that they had both been navigating around for eight days. Finally, he looked at the television, at the mop man, and then at the static that lived just underneath everything.

“Yeah,” he said.

And so Beavis closed his eyes.

`, song: { title: "Something Beautiful Remains (Urban Remix Edit)", artist: "Tina Turner", cover: "https://yt3.googleusercontent.com/yAX83obtuojW8pXa-6z2uZhMpCueCEvTj3aT1GG2ikD4tACQxt1hZVuAllVZNciL3uH-LX8ziXJvC3mBGQ=w544-h544-l90-rj", url: ["fcs-S4c2DBI"] } }, { id: "trevor", condition: (ev) => ev > 394 && ev <= 456, image: "https://i.pinimg.com/1200x/e2/55/de/e255de44e25e485ec28ec728b6adcdf6.jpg", html: `

jennifer government

The first thing Barack Obama registered was the smell. It wasn’t the sterile, chemical bite of a standard hospital room. It smelled faintly of lavender, ozone, and… wait, was that Cool Ranch?

He groaned, his eyelids fluttering open. The harsh fluorescent lights above him immediately assaulted his retinas, accompanied by a soft, rhythmic chiming. Every time his heart beat, the monitor next to him produced a tiny, cheerful ba-da-ba-ba-ba. It sounded suspiciously like the McDonald’s jingle.

“Well, well, well,” a voice said.

Obama blinked, his vision slowly swimming into focus. Leaning over the bed rail was a tall, lanky man with floppy hair and a completely unearned smirk. He was wearing pristine medical scrubs that were plastered with Nascar-style sponsor patches. The man rested his chin on his hands, looking down at the former President with an expression that was entirely too bright, and perhaps slightly hungry.

“Sleeping Beauty finally decides to grace us with his presence,” the man said, beaming. “I gotta tell you, I was starting to think you were holding out on me.”

“Where…” Obama started, his throat incredibly dry. His voice was a raspy shadow of its former self. “Where am I? What happened?”

“You’re at the Mount Sinai Medical Center, brought to you by Pfizer and Verizon. Can you hear me now?” The man winked. “You definitely can. You had a pretty nasty fall during the, uh, transition period. Been in a coma for a little over seven years. What a shame… I’m Trevor Moore, by the way, but you know me already. But here I’m Trevor White Claw, your primary care nurse. And let me just say right off the bat, it is an absolute honor… truly.”

Trevor reached out, placing a warm hand over Obama’s forearm. He didn’t pull it away. He let it linger, his thumb lightly stroking the President’s skin.

“And I just want to add,” Trevor whispered, leaning in a little closer, “you look fantastic. Like, objectively speaking. For a guy who’s been unconscious for the better part of a decade? You have the lats of a twenty-year-old Olympic swimmer, Barack. Seriously, I’ve been giving you your daily sponge baths, and it has not been a great experience, honestly.”

Obama stared at him, momentarily too stunned by the sheer audacity of the man’s bedside manner to process the rest of the information. He gently pulled his arm away from Trevor’s grasp. “Seven years? A transition period? Uh, let me be clear… what exactly is going on? Where is the, uh, Secret Service? Where is Joe? Michelle?”

“Okay, wow, do the voice again,” Trevor giggled, clapping his hands together. “Do the pauses! I love it when you do the pauses, it’s so commanding. It really gets my heart rate going.”

“Trevor,” Obama said, channeling every ounce of his legendary executive authority, despite wearing a paper-thin hospital gown. “Answer the, uh, question. What is the state of the United States government?”

“Oh! Right. Yeah, so, this is a bit awkward, but about five years ago, the national debt got a little out of hand. Like, mathematically impossible to pay off. But then the CEO of Amazon had this absolute stroke of genius. He asked, what if we just… IPO’d the country? So, uh, they did. The government basically liquidated and sold all of its assets to the highest bidders.”

Obama tried to sit up, a profound, chilling horror settling in his chest. “You’re telling me the United States was sold off to corporations? To Bezos and all the rest?”

“Rebranded!” Trevor corrected brightly, fluffing Obama’s pillow. “We call it the United Corporate States of America, brought to you by State Farm. And honestly, it’s a total upgrade. Everything is so streamlined now, really. The economy is literally flawless because it’s entirely subscription-based.”

“This is a nightmare,” Obama breathed, rubbing his temples. “A complete perversion of the American experiment. Our history, our monuments… the Lincoln Memorial…”

“Oh, you mean the Lincoln McDonald’s Memorial?” Trevor nodded enthusiastically. “Okay so, yeah, they kept the big marble chair, but they put a giant fiberglass McDouble right in his hands. It’s actually very tasteful. He stares out over the Diet Coke Reflecting Pool. The carbonation really helps keep the mosquito population down during the summer. So inspiring…”

“The Statue of Liberty?” Obama asked, his voice trembling.

“The Taco Bell Statue of Liberty. They replaced the torch with a glowing Crunchwrap Supreme, believe it or not. And the Liberty Bell? Yeah, Taco Bell bought that too! They actually filled in the… the crack with mild sauce, and now they ring it every time someone buys a Cheesy Gordita Crunch. It’s a little loud, honestly, but you get used to it.”

“The White House,” Obama said, dreading the answer.

“The Amazon White House! Prime Members get skip-the-line privileges, I think, and free two-day shipping on all executive orders. It’s totally sick.” Trevor leaned against the bedframe, crossing his arms and admiring Obama’s chest again. “Honestly, you’re stressing too much. You should relax. Here, let me check your vitals. I could do some physical therapy if you want. If you didn’t know, I specialize in deep tissue massage. Very deep.”

Obama ignored the flirtation entirely, his legendary intellect racing to piece together the mechanics of this dystopian hellscape. He looked down at the medical bracelet secured around his wrist. There was a barcode on it, and a small corporate logo. He noticed Trevor’s name tag again. Trevor White Claw.

“Wait a minute,” Obama said, his eyes narrowing as he looked back up at the nurse. “You introduced yourself as Trevor White Claw.”

“Yep.” He sighed. “Black Cherry is my favorite flavor, but my blood type is Mango.”

“Is that your legal name for real?”

“Duh,” Trevor smiled. “Now it is. Everyone takes the surname of their corporate sponsor or employer now. It’s just basic synergy, as in Rick and Morty. Like, your wife? Michelle Netflix? She is doing amazing, by the way. Huge streaming deals, number one on the platform.”

“Michelle… Netflix,” Obama repeated, feeling a terrifying coldness wash over him. The monitor next to him beeped faster. Ba-da-ba-ba-ba. Ba-da-ba-ba-ba.

He looked at his own hands. No, this can’t be. Not like this, none of this was real. He was a two-term president, a Nobel Peace Prize laureate, a global icon whose name was etched into the history books of a nation that apparently no longer existed… if everyone had a corporate surname… if every citizen was bought and paid for…

Obama swallowed hard, looking Trevor dead in the eye.

“Trevor,” Obama said, his voice dropping to a dangerous, low register. “What did they do to my name? Who bought my contract?”

Trevor froze. His playful, flirty smirk vanished for a fraction of a second, replaced by a sudden, incredibly awkward wince. He shifted his weight, suddenly looking very interested in the linoleum floor.

“Trevor,” Obama demanded. “Tell me my name.”

Biting his lip, Trevor’s eyes struggled to dart back to the former President. But a smirk slowly crept back onto his face, wide and painfully amused.

“Well,” Trevor chuckled, crossing his arms. “About that, Barack Comcast…”

`, song: { title: "In My Bed", artist: "Amy Winehouse", cover: "https://yt3.googleusercontent.com/_9Rfq6bjdHypuz40wPYQMHZDfraEHWtzLXZYDMneLAJZlpyD35p4ZuFKWQuTjxdcrFfTgw_YWrt5eRib=w544-h544-l90-rj", url: ["3vZHpdo4DHU"] } }, { id: "brandis", // shamelessly recycled from an old LBM slide what am I doing condition: (ev) => ev > 3 && ev < 64, image: "https://i.pinimg.com/1200x/36/b0/25/36b025ad71ab2577ddcdd92b86b3b11c.jpg", html: `

Stephen Baldwin
Jeremy London
Cindy Margolis
CinematographyPete Oshiro
Edited byT. Grumbach
Music byRyan Dolezal
Production
company		Kellner/Snoad Productions
Distributed byParaVox Pictures
Release dates March 11, 2005 (limited)
March 18, 2005 (wide)
Running time94 minutes
CountryUnited States
LanguageEnglish
Budget$11.2 million
Box office$884,210

Reboot is a 2005 American techno-thriller drama film written and directed by Marsh Kellner and produced by ParaVox Pictures. The film stars Jonathan Brandis as Kyle Strand, a former teen actor navigating a creative and personal collapse in the early days of consumer internet culture. The supporting cast includes Tara Reid, Pauly Shore, Corey Feldman, Stephen Baldwin, Jeremy London, and Cindy Margolis.

Released on March 18, 2005 to a wide theatrical run of 1,844 screens, Reboot earned $884,210 against its reported $11.2 million production budget, making it one of the more severe box office failures of the year and among the costliest per-screen underperformances in the first quarter of 2005.[1] Critical reception was broadly hostile, with many reviewers singling out its screenplay, its heavy integration of Apple Inc. products, and the perceived vanity of its central premise. The film received seven nominations at the 26th Golden Raspberry Awards, winning six, including Worst Picture and Worst Actor for Brandis, who attended the ceremony in person to collect all six awards, becoming only the fourth performer in Razzie history to do so.

Reboot attracted unusual public attention in April 2005 when President Steve Jobs, appearing on a CNBC technology segment, offered an unsolicited endorsement of the film, citing its "authentic" and "enthusiastic" use of Apple hardware as evidence that "real creativity happens on a Mac."[2] The remark was widely reported as a curiosity, and helped prompt renewed coverage of the film's commercial failure.

Contents
  1. Plot
  2. Cast
  3. Production
    1. Development
    2. Casting
    3. Apple product placement
  4. Release
    1. Box office
    2. Critical reception
  5. Accolades
  6. Presidential endorsement
  7. Legacy
  8. See also
  9. References

Plot [edit]

Kyle Strand (Brandis) is a 27-year-old former child actor best known for a single moderately successful mid-1990s science fiction television series, Vector Protocol, which was cancelled after two seasons. Now living in a modest apartment in the San Fernando Valley, Kyle supplements stalled auditions by freelancing as a low-level IT consultant, a skill he acquired during the production of his old show. He is in possession of a prototype Apple PowerBook G4 that his former co-star and on-again, off-again girlfriend Dana (Reid) has unknowingly loaded with a proprietary audio file belonging to a shadowy media conglomerate, Centrex Meridian, whose operatives, led by the sardonic Gregg Solis (Baldwin) — are now pursuing them across Los Angeles.

Enlisting the reluctant help of a conspiracy-minded web developer named Pitko (Shore) and a reclusive former technology journalist living in Reseda (Feldman), Kyle attempts to decrypt the file, expose Centrex Meridian, and reconcile with Dana, all while managing a call-back for a pilot that his agent (London) insists "could be huge". A subplot involves Kyle's estranged father (played by Robert Knepper in a cameo), who appears briefly in two scenes and is never mentioned again.

The film ends with Kyle uploading the decrypted audio file to a website using a Power Mac G5, destroying Centrex Meridian's corporate infrastructure.

Cast [edit]

ActorRoleNotes
Jonathan Brandis Kyle Strand, former teen actor and IT consultant Lead; also served as uncredited associate producer
Tara Reid Dana Mull, Kyle's girlfriend
Pauly Shore Pitko, conspiracy theorist and web developer Billed as "Pauly Shore as PITKO"
Corey Feldman Marcus Dunn, reclusive tech journalist
Stephen Baldwin Gregg Solis, Centrex Meridian operative Principal antagonist
Jeremy London Neil Farris, Kyle's agent Supporting
Cindy Margolis Sheila Voss, Centrex Meridian PR director Supporting; Margolis's feature film debut
Robert Knepper Ray Strand, Kyle's father Cameo; appears in scenes 11 and 14
Jake Byrd Desk clerk Uncredited

Production [edit]

Development

Reboot originated as a spec screenplay by Marsh Kellner and Derek Snoad, who had previously collaborated on the direct-to-video action film Countermand (2002). The screenplay was developed in late 2003 and early 2004, with Kellner drawing explicitly on the then-recent wave of internet-paranoia thrillers and what he described in a 2004 interview as "the untold story of what happens to child actors after the credits roll", a framing that several critics would later note was neither subtle nor particularly well-executed.[3]

ParaVox Pictures, a small Los Angeles-based production and distribution company, acquired the script in June 2004 for an undisclosed sum and committed to a theatrical release, reportedly based on confidence in the assembled cast's combined residual name recognition. Production was budgeted at $11.2 million, of which approximately $3.4 million was allocated to talent fees and $1.8 million to what the production notes describe as "tech consultation and visual effects," the latter comprising predominantly the acquisition and display of Apple hardware.[4]

Casting

Jonathan Brandis, whose career had effectively stalled following the cancellation of SeaQuest DSV and a series of low-profile film appearances through the late 1990s and early 2000s, was drawn to the project by what he described in promotional interviews as the "uncomfortable closeness" between Kyle Strand's circumstances and his own.[5] Brandis also reportedly negotiated an informal associate producer credit in exchange for a reduced appearance fee, which the production used toward the Apple hardware budget. In a March 2005 interview with Entertainment Weekly, Brandis said: "Kyle is living in the exact moment I spent a lot of years in. Except he's much better at IT than I am, and he has better equipment."[6]

The casting of Shore, Feldman, and Baldwin drew considerable comment in the trade press, with Variety describing the ensemble as "a kind of informal Museum of the Early Nineties, preserved in amber at someone's evident expense."[7] Jeremy London had been attached to the project from an early stage, while Corey Feldman has stated in subsequent interviews that he accepted the role following a single phone call from Brandis, to whom he said he felt "a certain kinship".[8]

Apple product placement

Reboot features extensive and conspicuous integration of Apple Inc. products, including an iMac G5, two PowerBook G4 laptops, a Power Mac G5, three iPod Mini units (one white, two green), an iSight camera, and what appears in the background of scene 23 to be a Xserve rack being used as a drinks cabinet. The placement was arranged directly between ParaVox and Apple's marketing division, though Apple has never officially confirmed its involvement and the film's credits do not list Apple as a sponsor.[9]

Multiple reviewers noted that the Apple hardware is granted camera time disproportionate to the needs of any given scene, with Roger Ebert observing in his review that "the PowerBook G4 has more close-ups than anyone in the supporting cast, and frankly gives a more consistent performance."[10] In one frequently cited sequence, Kyle solves a plot-critical encryption problem using GarageBand, a use-case with no established precedent in cryptography.

Release [edit]

Box office

Reboot opened on March 18, 2005 across 1,844 screens in the United States and Canada. It earned $562,000 in its opening weekend, placing 22nd at the box office — behind, among others, the fifth weekend of Hitch and a limited rerelease of Ray. By the end of its theatrical run, the film had grossed $884,210 in North America and an additional $44,000 internationally, for a worldwide total of $928,210 against its $11.2 million production budget, not including an estimated $6–8 million in marketing and distribution costs.[11]

The Hollywood Reporter named Reboot one of the ten worst-performing wide releases of 2005, noting that its per-screen average of $304 in opening weekend was the third-lowest of any film to open on more than 1,500 screens that year.[12] ParaVox Pictures ceased operations later that year, in August 2005.

Critical reception

Reboot received broadly negative reviews. On Rotten Tomatoes, the film holds an approval rating of 4% based on 47 reviews, with an average rating of 1.8/10. The critical consensus reads: "A film that earnestly confuses product placement with storytelling, Reboot is ninety-four minutes of remarkably expensive futility, elevated only by the occasional accidental charm of its lead."[13] On Metacritic, the film has a score of 9 out of 100, based on 22 critic reviews, indicating "overwhelming dislike."

"Watching Reboot is the cinematic equivalent of opening a computer only to find it full of smaller, sadder computers. Brandis brings genuine feeling to a role that did not deserve him, and everyone else brings what they can, which is, in several cases, not much."
— Roger Ebert, Chicago Sun-Times, March 18, 2005 (1.5/4 stars)[10]
"The film's peculiar achievement is that it manages to be simultaneously overplotted, boring, and empty. There is a MacGuffin, but it runs on OS X."
— Dana Stevens, Slate, March 21, 2005[14]
"I have seen films that used Apple computers as props. I have never before seen a film in which the computers appear to be the audience."
— A. O. Scott, The New York Times, March 18, 2005[15]

Accolades [edit]

Reboot received seven nominations at the 26th Golden Raspberry Awards, held on March 4, 2006. It won six, including Worst Picture, Worst Director, Worst Actor (Brandis), Worst Supporting Actor (Shore), Worst Screenplay, and Worst Screen Couple (Brandis and Reid). The sole nomination it did not win — Worst Supporting Actress for Margolis — went instead to Jenny McCarthy for Dirty Love.

26th Golden Raspberry Awards (2006)
Worst Picture🏆 WON
Worst Actor – Jonathan Brandis🏆 WON
Worst Director – Marsh Kellner🏆 WON
Worst Screenplay – Kellner, Snoad🏆 WON
Worst Supporting Actor – Pauly Shore🏆 WON
Worst Screen Couple – Brandis & Reid🏆 WON
Worst Supporting Actress – Cindy MargolisNominated (lost to Jenny McCarthy, Dirty Love)

Unexpectedly, Brandis attended the Razzies ceremony at the Barnsdall Art Park in Los Angeles and collected all six awards himself, delivering a brief acceptance speech for each. For Worst Picture, he remarked: "I want to thank Marsh, who believed in this project, and I want to thank Apple, whose computers worked flawlessly throughout filming and deserve better than this. And I want to thank everyone who saw it. Yes, both of you."[16] The Los Angeles Times described his attendance as "an act of either extraordinary self-awareness or extraordinary stubbornness, possibly both."[17] Brandis became only the fourth individual in Razzie history, after Paul Verhoeven, Tom Green, and Halle Berry, to accept a Razzie award in person.

Presidential endorsement [edit]

On April 14, 2005, President Steve Jobs, appearing in a televised roundtable on American technology competitiveness broadcast on CNBC, was asked by host Maria Bartiromo about the role of consumer technology in popular culture. Jobs, departing from the prepared segment, remarked: "Have you seen Reboot? The movie? You should see it. Not because it's a great film, it's not, but because there's a scene where this kid uses a PowerBook to crack open what is essentially a media monopoly, and he does it on his own, at a kitchen table, and the machine just works. That's what we're building. That's why we do this."[2]

The remark attracted significant media coverage, partly for its candid acknowledgment that the film was not of high quality, and partly because it represented an unusually specific pop cultural reference from a sitting president. The Wall Street Journal noted that Jobs's endorsement, though qualified, constituted "perhaps the first White House recommendation of a film that had already received a Razzie nomination."[18] The White House press secretary, when asked about the statement at a briefing the following day, confirmed that it reflected the president's personal opinion and was not a formal executive endorsement. Dana Perino added, without elaboration, that the president "stands by his aesthetic judgment".[19]

Brandis, reached for comment by Entertainment Weekly, said: "I genuinely don't know what to do with that. I have the Razzies on my shelf and now apparently the president liked the movie. I mean, think that covers every possible critical outcome... it's quite flattering."[20] A brief spike in DVD pre-orders followed the coverage, though sales ultimately remained modest.

Legacy [edit]

Reboot has been cited in several academic and journalistic analyses of early-2000s Hollywood as an illustration of the limits of casting-driven financing strategies in the home video and fragmented-audience era.[21] The film has developed a minor cult reputation in certain online communities, partly due to repeated references in so-bad-it's-good film discussion spaces and partly due to Brandis's Razzie ceremony appearances, which have been circulated as video clips independently of the film itself.

The film was released on DVD in June 2005 by Anchor Bay Entertainment with no supplemental features. In a 2011 retrospective in The A.V. Club, Reboot was ranked 14th on a list of the most financially catastrophic wide releases of the 2000s, described as "the rare film that failed commercially, critically, and industrially, yet managed to be charming about it."[22]

  • List of biggest box office bombs

Blog  ·  Arts & Culture  ·  February 5, 2010

West Wing Record Room:
February 2010

The President and Vice President return with their monthly listening notes. A slow month for some, a landmark one for others, and one very surprising EP.
President Wesley K. Clark & Vice President Mary L. Landrieu The White House · Washington, D.C.
February 5, 2010  ·  9:42 a.m. EST
Share Email

We started this column in the second year of our first term as a way of staying honest with ourselves (and, we hope, with the American people) about what the country is actually making. We know that music doesn't care about partisan lines, nor does it respond to the executive orders that come in and out of office. But it does, occasionally, respond to a decent pair of headphones and a quiet Saturday in the residence.

We've kept it going because the mail we get about it is, frankly, among the most civil correspondence this office receives. February is a quiet month for new releases by historical standards, but this cycle it gave us more than we expected. We hope you find something here worth listening to.

— President Clark writes in italic. Vice President Landrieu writes in regular text.
The Reviews
Album · Kitsuné / Glassnote
Tourist History
Two Door Cinema Club
Feb. 26, 2010 10 tracks · 34 min. Antrim, Northern Ireland
★★★★☆ 4 / 5
POTUS Reviewed by President Wesley K. Clark

I want to be careful not to oversell this, because I think overselling it is actually the wrong response to it. Tourist History is a record that does exactly what it intends to do, nothing more, nothing less — and there is a kind of military virtue in that that I find genuinely admirable. Three young men from County Antrim who decided, apparently quite deliberately, to make something tight and fast and disciplined, and then executed that decision without much apparent doubt or deviation.

What strikes me, listening through on the Residence stereo on a Wednesday evening (for context, the Vice President was at the Hill for the budget session and I had the place to myself) is how economical the arrangements are. It seems nothing here is truly wasted. In an infantry unit I would describe this as good fire discipline: everyone shooting, no one shooting at nothing.

"Something Good Can Work" is the best argument for keeping your options open that I have encountered in a pop song. I intend to play it at the appropriate moment.

If I have a reservation, it is that the record is almost too consistent. I would have welcomed one moment of genuine disorder, maybe one track that doesn't quite work or that takes an unnecessary risk. The best albums I have known, like the best campaigns, have at least one moment where something is attempted that might very easily have failed. This one is almost too controlled for its own good. But "almost" is the operative word. Highly recommended.

Indie Rock Dance-Punk Northern Ireland Tracks to Start With: "Cigarettes in the Theatre," "Something Good Can Work"
Album · Beluga Heights / Warner Bros.
Jason Derulo
Jason Derulo
Feb. 2, 2010 12 tracks · 45 min. Miami, FL
★★★☆☆ 3 / 5
VPOTUS Reviewed by Vice President Mary L. Landrieu

I grew up in New Orleans, which means I grew up in a city that has very strong opinions about what R&B is supposed to feel like. Loose, warm, unhurried... in other words, music that assumes the listener has time. Jason Derulo is, well, not that. Jason Derulo is perhaps closer to Miami: everything is fast and engineered within an inch of its life, and there is no texture that hasn't been processed into submission.

I want to be fair to him, because I think there is genuine talent here. "Whatcha Say" got into my head in September and, I will confess, stayed there for the better part of six weeks. That is not a small achievement! A hook that durable is not an accident. The man writes melodies very efficiently, knowing exactly what effect is desired.

The problem with the album as a whole is that it cannot sustain the level of polish it applies to everything. Twelve tracks of that much sonic treatment starts to feel like eating twelve consecutive desserts. By track eight you are grateful for it to stop!

Still, as a debut, it is a considerable statement of intent from a 20-year-old. He will make a better record, surely. I would like for it to breathe slightly more.

R&B / Pop Miami Tracks to Start With: "Whatcha Say," "In My Head"
★ Featured Review · First Studio Album in a Decade
Album · Epic / Sony Music
Soldier of Love
Sade
Feb. 8, 2010 9 tracks · 49 min. London / Lagos
★★★★★ 5 / 5
POTUS & VPOTUS Joint review — President Clark & Vice President Landrieu

Ten years is a pretty long time to be quiet. The Vice President and I discussed this record separately before we discussed it together, and then we sat with it on a Sunday afternoon in the Map Room (there is a record player there that belonged, I believe, to a previous occupant, and we have kept it because it seems important to keep things that work) and we found, to neither of our great surprise, that we had arrived at roughly the same place: this is one of the finest records we have heard in a very long time.

I will add to that: it is a record that knows it has earned the right to take its time. Nothing about Soldier of Love is in a hurry, and it shouldn't be. Sade's voice has always operated at a frequency slightly outside ordinary time... slower, steadier, as though the song had been running long before you pressed play and will continue running long after. This record deepens that quality rather than disrupting it. It is fascinating.

The title track sounds like something that has always existed, that was simply waiting for someone to notate it. That is the highest compliment I can pay a piece of music. — Vice President Landrieu

I would add only this: "Babyfather" is the most technically interesting thing on the record, and its rhythmic instability under a perfectly smooth surface is something that takes real discipline to sustain. In operational terms, it is a record of extraordinary composure under pressure. I have given it five stars. I rarely give five stars, as many of you know. The record has earned them and will not be embarrassed by them.

Soul Quiet Storm Album of the Month Tracks to Start With: "Soldier of Love," "The Moon and the Sky," "Babyfather"
◆ Special Preview
EP Advance Mix · Buddyhead / Universal · est. Spring 2010
Black Sheep (EP Preview)
Nat & Alex Wolff
Advance Mix, Feb. 2010 5 tracks (preview) New York, NY
★★★★☆ 4 / 5
VPOTUS Reviewed by Vice President Mary L. Landrieu

I want to address the obvious thing first: yes, these are the Naked Brothers Band kids. Or they were, until the network situation resolved itself and they emerged on the other side of it with their actual names and, apparently, a significantly better record. I watched that show once, in the second year of our first term, at the insistence of my youngest, and I thought at the time that there was something real underneath the production gloss and the Viacom sheen — something that would survive if given the right conditions.

Black Sheep, or what exists of it in this advance EP mix (these are five tracks, rough-mixed, sent to the residence by their management, I will not say by whom) suggests that the right conditions have arrived. Nat Wolff, who I believe is sixteen, writes with a kind of restlessness that is impossible to fake. He seems to have been wanting to say the things said in this album for a long time, and was told, for commercial reasons, to wait. But the waiting is over, and we can hear it.

"Maybe" is probably the standout of these. There is a bridge in it that opens up in a way that nothing else on the EP prepares you for. It is genuinely surprising, and at sixteen, one could say that genuine surprise is everything.

"Monday Afternoons" is the more intimate of the five tracks, and it is the one I have returned to most. There is just a looseness to it that keeps me listening again, again, and again. Alex Wolff, meanwhile, is the kind of drummer who plays like he invented the drum kit last week and is still finding out what it can do — which is a quality, not a criticism.

I should be clear: this is five tracks of an advance mix, not a finished album. There will be things to refine. But as a statement of direction from two young men stepping deliberately out of the shadows, it is more than promising. Watch this one.

Indie Pop Alternative Preview Only Note: This is an advance mix. Commercial release TBD, Spring 2010.
Brief mentions

Also Listened To This Month

Frightened Rabbit, The Winter of Mixed Drinks (Fatcat) — Mary described this, accurately, as "Scottish weather in album form." I have a more favorable view of it, however. Four and a half stars, and recommended for anyone who is not already sad. — WKC

Vampire Weekend, Contra (XL) — The lead single is both irresistible and energetic. Four stars. — ML: Four stars, easily. "Cousins" is genuinely thrilling.

Beach House, Teen Dream (Sub Pop) — I put this on at eleven in the evening and it made the entire Residence feel like it was several feet underwater in the best possible sense. Mary also fell asleep, which she insists is a compliment? Four stars. — WKC

★  This month's reviewers
